topics.title: Equation and Frequency Spectrum of AM Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equation and Frequency Spectrum of AM Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is a linear modulation technique in which the instantaneous **amplitude** of a high-frequency **carrier signal** is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency **message (modulating) signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain strictly constant.

The general time-domain expression for an AM signal is:

$$s(t) = A_c\left[1 + k_a\,m(t)\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where every term has a strict KTU-defined meaning (listed in §2).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Candle in a Box" Intuition

Imagine you are carrying a **message candle** (your voice / music) inside a much louder, rhythmic **carrier drumbeat**. The drum cannot carry your whisper across a noisy stadium, so you make the drum beat **louder or softer** in step with your candle's flicker. To a distant listener:
- The **drumbeat (carrier)** is the vehicle.
- The **flicker pattern (envelope)** is the message they actually decode.
- The drumbeat's rhythm (frequency/phase) never changes — only its loudness does.

That literal mapping of "candle flicker → drum loudness" **is** amplitude modulation. The drum continues beating at $f_c$ Hz, but its loudness at every instant equals $A_c + \text{(message)}$.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Carrier frequency** $f_c$ is typically in the **kHz to MHz** range (e.g., 540 kHz – 1700 kHz for the AM broadcast band).
> - **Modulating frequency** $f_m$ (voice/music) is restricted to roughly **300 Hz – 5 kHz** for speech-grade AM broadcast.
> - **Speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** is the universal constant linking frequency to wavelength $\lambda = c/f$.
> - The **modulation index** $m$ (a.k.a. $\mu$ or $m_a$) is a **dimensionless** number, and for distortion-free transmission we require $\mathbf{0 \le m \le 1}$.

### 1.4 Why AM Matters in KTU Curriculum

AM is the pedagogical "first step" into modulation because:
1. It is mathematically the **simplest** modulation scheme — pure multiplication + addition.
2. It introduces the pivotal concept of **frequency translation** (shifting a low-frequency message spectrum up to a high-frequency band).
3. It forms the **theoretical foundation** for DSB-SC, SSB, VSB, and QAM — all later modules.
4. It directly enables **long-wave, medium-wave, and short-wave broadcasting**, **aircraft communication (118–137 MHz, AM-DSB)**, and **CB radio** — all real KTU-relevant applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building the AM Equation — Step-by-Step Logic

The derivation progresses from the general (multi-tone) form to the simplest (single-tone) form that examiners love to set in the KTU paper.

**Step 1 — Carrier alone.** A pure sinusoid is:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Message alone.** The information-bearing baseband signal is:

$$m(t)$$

A single-tone test message is $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Step 3 — Form the envelope.** Amplitude modulation embeds the message as a *linear perturbation* of the carrier's amplitude. The instantaneous amplitude becomes $A_c + k_a\,m(t)$, where $k_a$ is the **modulator sensitivity** (V⁻¹).

**Step 4 — Write the AM signal.** Substituting the envelope into the cosine argument as the amplitude coefficient:

$$s(t) = \big[A_c + k_a\,m(t)\big]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 5 — Normalise to modulation index.** Define $k_a = m/A_m$ (or, equivalently, $k_a\,A_m = m\,A_c$ for the conventional form). We obtain the canonical KTU form:

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = A_c\big[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)\big]\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,}$$

**Step 6 — Trigonometric expansion.** Apply the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

**Step 7 — Interpret the three terms.**
- The first term is the **carrier** (unchanged).
- The second term is the **Lower Sideband (LSB)**.
- The third term is the **Upper Sideband (USB)**.

### 2.2 Phasor Picture (Why AM is "Linear")

At any instant, the AM signal is the vector sum of three co-planar phasors:
- A long, stationary carrier phasor of length $A_c$ rotating at $f_c$.
- A USB phasor of length $mA_c/2$ rotating at $f_c + f_m$ — appears to *rotate slowly* at $f_m$ relative to the carrier.
- An LSB phasor of length $mA_c/2$ rotating at $f_c - f_m$ — appears to rotate at $-f_m$ relative to the carrier.

The two sideband phasors are **mirror images** rotating in opposite directions. Their vector sum is always **collinear** with the carrier, which is precisely why AM is a *linear* modulation and why the envelope is a clean reproduction of the message.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of AM |
|---|---|
| **MW / SW broadcast** (540 kHz – 30 MHz) | Envelope-detection receivers are dirt-cheap (one diode + RC), enabling billions of low-cost AM radios worldwide. |
| **Air-band voice (118–137 MHz, DSB AM)** | Allows pilots to monitor multiple towers using simple, rugged receivers. |
| **RFID & NFC (125/134 kHz, 13.56 MHz)** | Load modulation — a low-data-rate cousin of AM. |
| **Optical fibre (ASK / OOK)** | The optical equivalent of on-off AM, used in early Ethernet. |
| **Educational foundation** | Introduces students to **modulation index, bandwidth, sidebands, and envelope detection** — concepts reused in every later analog/digital scheme. |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the single most-tested cluster of equations in Module 1. Memorise every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Boundary / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carrier | $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $A_c$ in **V**, $f_c$ in **Hz** | High-frequency sinusoid |
| 2 | Message (single-tone) | $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$ | $A_m$ in **V**, $f_m$ in **Hz** | $f_m \ll f_c$ always |
| 3 | **Modulation Index** | $m = \dfrac{k_a A_m}{A_c} = \dfrac{A_{\max}-A_{\min}}{A_{\max}+A_{\min}}$ | **dimensionless** | $0 \le m \le 1$ for **distortion-free** envelope detection |
| 4 | AM signal (canonical) | $s(t)=A_c[1+m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | **V** | KTU board favourite |
| 5 | AM signal (expanded) | $s(t)=A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)+\dfrac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c-f_m)t]+\dfrac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]$ | **V** | 3-term identity form |
| 6 | Carrier amplitude (spectral) | $A_c/2$ at $\pm f_c$ | **V/Hz** | Impulse pair in spectrum |
| 7 | Sideband amplitude (spectral) | $mA_c/4$ at $\pm(f_c \pm f_m)$ | **V/Hz** | Half the carrier height when $m=1$ |
| 8 | Total AM power (1 $\Omega$) | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}\!\left[1 + \dfrac{m^{2}}{2}\right]$ | **W** | $P_c = A_c^2/2$ carrier alone |
| 9 | Carrier power | $P_c = A_c^{\,2}/2$ | **W** | Independent of $m$ |
| 10 | Sideband power (total) | $P_{SB} = \dfrac{m^{2}A_c^{\,2}}{4}$ | **W** | $P_{USB}=P_{LSB}=m^{2}A_c^{2}/8$ |
| 11 | Power efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{SB}}{P_t}=\dfrac{m^{2}}{2+m^{2}}$ | **dimensionless** | Max $= 1/3 = 33.33\%$ at $m=1$ |
| 12 | **Transmission Bandwidth** | $BW = 2 f_m$ (single-tone) / $2 W$ (general) | **Hz** | $W$ = message bandwidth |
| 13 | Modulation sensitivity | $k_a = mA_c / A_m$ | **V⁻¹** | Set by hardware design |
| 14 | % modulation | $\mu\% = m \times 100$ | **%** | $m=0.75 \Rightarrow 75\%$ modulation |
| 15 | Envelope max / min | $A_{\max}=A_c(1+m)$, $A_{\min}=A_c(1-m)$ | **V** | Used in CRO interpretation |

### 2.5 Critical "Why" Reasoning (Frequently Asked in KTU Viva)

- **Why two sidebands?** Because the product of two cosines (carrier × message) mathematically produces sum and difference frequencies, by the identity $2\cos A\cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$.
- **Why is the carrier still present in the spectrum?** Because the AM modulator is *additive*: $A_c[1+m\cos(\cdot)]\cos(\cdot)$ keeps an unscaled $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ term after expansion.
- **Why $\eta_{\max} = 33.33\%$?** Because the carrier consumes $2/3$ of the total power at $m=1$, leaving only $1/3$ for the information-bearing sidebands.
- **Why $BW = 2W$?** Because each spectral component of the message at frequency $f$ generates *two* translated components at $f_c+f$ and $f_c-f$, so the total occupied width is doubled.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — AM Equation from First Principles (Single-Tone Message)

We start with the carrier $c(t)$ and the message $m(t)$ and derive the canonical KTU form.

**Given:**

$$c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Assumption:** The modulated carrier's instantaneous amplitude varies linearly as the message.

$$s(t) = \big[A_c + k_a\,m(t)\big]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Substitute** $m(t)$:

$$s(t) = \big[A_c + k_a A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)\big]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Define modulation index** $m \equiv k_a A_m / A_c$, so that $k_a A_m = m A_c$:

$$s(t) = A_c\big[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)\big]\cos(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{(canonical form)}$$

**Now apply** the product-to-sum identity $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$ with $A = 2\pi f_c t$ and $B = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{A_c}{2}\,m\cos\big[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\big] + \frac{A_c}{2}\,m\cos\big[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\big]$$

**Final expanded form (3-term identity):**

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]\,}$$

**Interpretation of the three terms:**

| Term | Frequency | Amplitude (time) | Amplitude (spectral, 1 $\Omega$) | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_c$ | $A_c$ | $A_c/2$ | Carrier |
| 2 | $f_c - f_m$ | $mA_c/2$ | $mA_c/4$ | Lower Sideband (LSB) |
| 3 | $f_c + f_m$ | $mA_c/2$ | $mA_c/4$ | Upper Sideband (USB) |

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Spectrum (Bilateral) of AM Signal

The bilateral (two-sided) amplitude spectrum of $s(t)$ is obtained by taking the Fourier transform.

**Recall the FT pair:** $\cos(2\pi f_0 t) \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}}\; \tfrac{1}{2}\big[\delta(f-f_0)+\delta(f+f_0)\big]$.

**Apply to each term of the expanded $s(t)$:**

$$\mathcal{F}\{A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)\} = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[\delta(f-f_c)+\delta(f+f_c)\big]$$

$$\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c-f_m)t]\right\} = \frac{mA_c}{4}\big[\delta(f-f_c+f_m)+\delta(f+f_c-f_m)\big]$$

$$\mathcal{F}\!\left\{\frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]\right\} = \frac{mA_c}{4}\big[\delta(f-f_c-f_m)+\delta(f+f_c+f_m)\big]$$

**Summing all six impulses (assuming $f_c>0$ and focusing on positive frequencies, the standard AM broadcast plot):**

| Frequency | Spectral line height (positive side) |
|---|---|
| $f_c - f_m$ | $mA_c/4$ |
| $f_c$ | $A_c/2$ |
| $f_c + f_m$ | $mA_c/4$ |

**Bandwidth:**

$$BW = (f_c + f_m) - (f_c - f_m) = 2 f_m$$

For a general bandlimited message with highest frequency $W$:

$$\boxed{\,BW_{AM} = 2 W\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Power and Transmission Efficiency

Average power in a 1-ohm resistor (signal-only) for each cosine term is $\tfrac{1}{2}(\text{amplitude})^2$.

**Carrier power:**

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}$$

**Sideband power (each):**

$$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{mA_c}{2}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{m^{2}A_c^{\,2}}{8}$$

**Total sideband power:**

$$P_{SB} = P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = \frac{m^{2}A_c^{\,2}}{4}$$

**Total transmitted power:**

$$\boxed{\,P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{m^{2}A_c^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}\!\left[1 + \frac{m^{2}}{2}\right]\,}$$

**Power efficiency** (fraction of total power that actually carries information):

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m^{2}/4}{(2+m^{2})/4} = \frac{m^{2}}{2+m^{2}}$$

**Maximum efficiency** at $m=1$:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{2+1} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\%$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 1 MHz carrier of amplitude 50 V is amplitude-modulated by a 5 kHz sinusoidal message of amplitude 25 V. Determine: (a) modulation index, (b) sideband frequencies and amplitudes, (c) AM signal equation, (d) total sideband power and transmission efficiency (assume 50 $\Omega$ load).

**Given:** $A_c = 50$ V, $f_c = 10^6$ Hz, $A_m = 25$ V, $f_m = 5\times10^3$ Hz, $R = 50\;\Omega$.

**(a) Modulation index.** Using $m = A_m / A_c$ for a normalised modulator:

$$m = \frac{25}{50} = 0.5 \quad (50\% \text{ modulation})$$

**(b) Sideband frequencies and amplitudes.**

$$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 10^{6} - 5\times 10^{3} = 995{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 995 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 10^{6} + 5\times 10^{3} = 1{,}005{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 1005 \text{ kHz}$$

$$A_{LSB} = A_{USB} = \frac{mA_c}{2} = \frac{0.5 \times 50}{2} = 12.5 \text{ V}$$

**(c) AM signal equation.**

$$s(t) = 50\big[1 + 0.5\cos(2\pi\cdot 5000\,t)\big]\cos(2\pi\cdot 10^{6}\,t)\;\text{V}$$

**Equivalently (expanded):**

$$s(t) = 50\cos(2\pi\cdot 10^{6}t) + 12.5\cos(2\pi\cdot 995{,}000\,t) + 12.5\cos(2\pi\cdot 1{,}005{,}000\,t)\;\text{V}$$

**(d) Power and efficiency (load $R = 50\;\Omega$).**

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\!\left[1 + \frac{m^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{50^{2}}{2\times 50}\!\left[1 + \frac{0.5^{2}}{2}\right] = 25\,(1.125) = 28.125 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{SB} = \frac{m^{2}A_c^{\,2}}{4R} = \frac{0.25 \times 2500}{200} = 3.125 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{3.125}{28.125} = 0.1111 = 11.11\%$$

**Verification with the formula:**

$$\eta = \frac{m^{2}}{2+m^{2}} = \frac{0.25}{2.25} = 0.1111 \quad \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Time-Domain Waveform of an AM Signal (Conceptual Map)

The Mermaid block below maps the *block-level functional architecture* of how the three spectral components combine to form the time-domain AM waveform — this is a robust substitute for the physical CRO drawing examiners expect you to sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    msg["Message m of t = Am cos 2 pi fm t"]:::msg
    car["Carrier c of t = Ac cos 2 pi fc t"]:::car
    bias["DC Bias = Ac constant"]:::bias
    sum1["Adder 1: Ac + m of t"]:::sum
    mul["Multiplier: envelope x cos 2 pi fc t"]:::mul
    amOut["AM Signal s of t = Ac 1 plus m cos 2 pi fm t cos 2 pi fc t"]:::out
    side1["LSB Term: mAc over 2 cos 2 pi fc minus fm t"]:::side
    side2["USB Term: mAc over 2 cos 2 pi fc plus fm t"]:::side
    carT["Carrier Term: Ac cos 2 pi fc t"]:::side

    msg --> sum1
    bias --> sum1
    sum1 --> mul
    car --> mul
    mul --> amOut
    amOut -.expanded form.-> carT
    amOut -.expanded form.-> side1
    amOut -.expanded form.-> side2

    classDef msg fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef car fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef mul fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    classDef side fill:#f5f5f4,stroke:#44403c,color:#000
```

**Reading guide for the KTU sketch on paper:**
- The **envelope** is the smooth curve touching the peaks: it equals $A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]$.
- The **carrier** is the high-frequency wiggle *inside* the envelope.
- The envelope has maxima $A_{\max} = A_c(1+m)$ and minima $A_{\min} = A_c(1-m)$.

---

### 4.2 Bilateral Frequency Spectrum of AM (Single-Tone)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    axis["Frequency axis f in Hz"]:::axis
    nL["-fc minus fm : height mAc over 4"]:::sb
    nC["-fc : height Ac over 2 CARRIER"]:::car
    nU["-fc plus fm : height mAc over 4"]:::sb
    pL["fc minus fm : height mAc over 4 LSB"]:::sb
    pC["fc : height Ac over 2 CARRIER"]:::car
    pU["fc plus fm : height mAc over 4 USB"]:::sb
    bw["Transmission Bandwidth BW = 2 fm"]:::bw

    nL --- nC --- nU --- axis --- pL --- pC --- pU
    pL -.- bw
    pU -.- bw

    classDef axis fill:#fafafa,stroke:#52525b,color:#000
    classDef car fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef sb fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef bw fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
```

**Reading guide for the KTU sketch on paper:**
- For $m < 1$: carrier impulse is *taller* than the two sideband impulses.
- For $m = 1$: sidebands are exactly *half* the carrier height.
- For $m > 1$ (over-modulation): envelope is no longer a clean reproduction of the message — envelope distortion appears.
- The pattern is **symmetric about $f = 0$** — this is the hallmark of a real (non-complex) signal.

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram of an AM Modulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inMsg["Message m of t from mic or audio source"]:::msg
    inCar["Carrier from local oscillator at fc"]:::car
    dc["DC supply Ac volts"]:::bias
    adder["Summing Amplifier adds DC plus message"]:::sum
    mult["Multiplier AD633 or MC1496"]:::mul
    pa["Class C Power Amplifier"]:::pa
    ant["Transmit Antenna"]:::ant
    out["AM Wave s of t radiated into channel"]:::out

    inMsg --> adder
    dc --> adder
    adder --> mult
    inCar --> mult
    mult --> pa
    pa --> ant
    ant --> out

    classDef msg fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef car fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef bias fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
    classDef mul fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef pa fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
    classDef ant fill:#f5f5f4,stroke:#44403c,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f5f5f4,stroke:#44403c,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why the DC supply?** The bias $A_c$ in the adder ensures the *envelope never crosses zero* when $m \le 1$. Without it, over-modulation and severe distortion result — a classic KTU viva question.

---

### 4.4 Phasor Diagram (Sideband-Cancellation Concept Preview)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    carrier["Carrier phasor: length Ac rotating at fc"]:::car
    usb["USB phasor: length mAc over 2 rotating at fc plus fm"]:::usb
    lsb["LSB phasor: length mAc over 2 rotating at fc minus fm"]:::lsb
    ssum["Resultant phasor length Ac 1 plus m cos 2 pi fm t rotating at fc"]:::out

    usb --> ssum
    lsb --> ssum
    carrier --> ssum

    classDef car fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000
    classDef usb fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000
    classDef lsb fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#6d28d9,color:#000
```

The USB and LSB phasors rotate in **opposite directions** at the *same relative rate* $f_m$. Their vector sum is *always* collinear with the carrier, producing the clean in-phase envelope that is the foundation of every AM demodulation strategy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define modulation index for an AM signal. What is its significance? List the conditions to avoid distortion.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The modulation index $m$ is the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier, i.e., $m = A_m / A_c$. It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the *depth* of modulation.
- **Significance (1 mark):** $m$ controls the sideband amplitudes ($A_{LSB}=A_{USB}=mA_c/2$), the sideband power ($P_{SB} \propto m^{2}$), and the transmission efficiency ($\eta = m^{2}/(2+m^{2})$).
- **Distortion-free condition (1 mark):** $0 \le m \le 1$. For $m>1$ (over-modulation), the envelope becomes clipped and the message is irrecoverably distorted at the envelope detector.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Dec 2024]**
**What is transmission bandwidth of an AM signal? For a message bandlimited to 5 kHz, compute the bandwidth.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Transmission bandwidth of an AM signal is the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components of the modulated signal, equal to $BW = 2W$ where $W$ is the message bandwidth.
- **Derivation hint (1 mark):** Each frequency $f$ in the message generates two sidebands at $f_c \pm f$; therefore the spectrum occupies $(f_c - W)$ to $(f_c + W)$, giving $BW = (f_c+W) - (f_c-W) = 2W$.
- **Numerical (1 mark):** For $W = 5$ kHz, $BW = 2 \times 5 = 10$ kHz.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** In Part B, a Module-1 question is offered with internal choice. You must attempt **one** of Q(A) or Q(B). Each carries 14 marks, typically split 7 + 7.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for an AM signal with a single-tone modulating signal. Sketch its time-domain waveform and bilateral frequency spectrum, clearly labelling the carrier and sideband components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.2 MHz carrier of amplitude 80 V is amplitude modulated to a depth of 60% by a 4 kHz tone. Calculate (i) the sideband frequencies, (ii) the sideband amplitudes, (iii) the maximum and minimum envelope amplitudes, and (iv) the total average power delivered to a 50 $\Omega$ antenna. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q(A)(a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Carrier** $c(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and **message** $m(t) = A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$. **[0.5 mark]**
2. **Instantaneous amplitude** $= A_c + m(t) = A_c + A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)$. **[0.5 mark]**
3. **AM signal** $s(t) = [A_c + A_m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. **[1 mark]**
4. **Define modulation index** $m = A_m/A_c$, and rewrite as $s(t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. **[1 mark]**
5. **Expand** using $\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B)+\cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c-f_m)t] + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]$$

**[1 mark]**

6. **Time-domain sketch (3 marks breakdown):**
   - High-frequency carrier inside an envelope. **[1 mark]**
   - Envelope $A_c[1+m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]$ touching the peaks. **[1 mark]**
   - Labels for $A_{\max}=A_c(1+m)$, $A_{\min}=A_c(1-m)$, period $T_m = 1/f_m$. **[1 mark]**
7. **Bilateral spectrum (omit if time is short — but here required):**
   - Carrier impulses of height $A_c/2$ at $\pm f_c$. **[0.5 mark]**
   - Sideband impulses of height $mA_c/4$ at $\pm(f_c \pm f_m)$. **[0.5 mark]**
   - $BW = 2f_m$ labelled. **[0 mark — covered separately]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students lose 2–3 marks because they (a) forget to write the **modulation-index definition** before substituting, (b) skip the **product-to-sum identity step**, or (c) label only one of the two sideband impulses in the spectrum. Always write the identity explicitly and label *both* sidebands symmetrically.

---

**Model Solution — Q(A)(b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $f_c = 1.2$ MHz, $A_c = 80$ V, $m = 0.6$ (i.e., 60%), $f_m = 4$ kHz, $R = 50\;\Omega$.

**(i) Sideband frequencies [1.5 marks]:**

$$f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 1{,}200{,}000 - 4{,}000 = 1{,}196{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 1196 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 1{,}200{,}000 + 4{,}000 = 1{,}204{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 1204 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Sideband amplitudes [1.5 marks]:**

$$A_{LSB} = A_{USB} = \frac{mA_c}{2} = \frac{0.6 \times 80}{2} = 24 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Envelope extremes [2 marks]:**

$$A_{\max} = A_c(1+m) = 80(1+0.6) = 128 \text{ V}$$

$$A_{\min} = A_c(1-m) = 80(1-0.6) = 32 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Total average power [2 marks]:**

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R}\!\left[1 + \frac{m^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{80^{2}}{2\times 50}\!\left[1 + \frac{0.6^{2}}{2}\right] = \frac{6400}{100}(1.18) = 64 \times 1.18$$

$$\boxed{P_t = 75.52 \text{ W}}$$

**Bonus (optional):** Carrier power $P_c = A_c^{2}/(2R) = 6400/100 = 64$ W; sideband power $P_{SB} = P_t - P_c = 11.52$ W; efficiency $\eta = 11.52/75.52 = 15.25\%$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation of an AM signal. Derive an expression for the total transmitted power and the modulation efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An AM broadcast transmitter radiates 12 kW when the modulation index is 0.7. Calculate the carrier power, total sideband power, and the power radiated when the modulation index is changed to 0.5. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Q(B)(a) [7 marks]:**

1. **Block diagram (3 marks)** — See the AM modulator block diagram in §4.3 of these notes. Must include:
   - Audio source → pre-emphasis → summing amplifier (with DC bias). **[1 mark]**
   - Local oscillator at $f_c$ → multiplier (e.g., AD633 / MC1496). **[1 mark]**
   - Multiplier output → Class C PA → antenna. **[1 mark]**
2. **AM signal generation (2 marks):**

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{mA_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c \pm f_m)t]$$

3. **Total power derivation (1 mark):**

$$P_t = P_c + P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2} + \frac{m^{2}A_c^{\,2}}{4} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2}\!\left[1 + \frac{m^{2}}{2}\right]$$

4. **Modulation efficiency (1 mark):**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{m^{2}}{2 + m^{2}}, \quad \eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ at } m=1$$

---

**Model Solution — Q(B)(b) [7 marks]:**

**Given (Stage 1):** $P_{t1} = 12$ kW at $m_1 = 0.7$. Carrier power is independent of $m$, so first isolate $P_c$.

$$P_{t1} = P_c\!\left[1 + \frac{m_1^{2}}{2}\right] \;\Rightarrow\; 12 = P_c\!\left[1 + \frac{0.49}{2}\right] = P_c(1.245)$$

$$P_c = \frac{12}{1.245} = 9.638 \text{ kW} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**Sideband power at $m_1 = 0.7$ [1.5 marks]:**

$$P_{SB1} = P_{t1} - P_c = 12 - 9.638 = 2.362 \text{ kW}$$

(Verify: $P_{SB1} = m_1^{2}P_c/2 = 0.49 \times 9.638/2 = 2.362$ kW ✓)

**Total power at $m_2 = 0.5$ [1.5 marks]:**

$$P_{t2} = P_c\!\left[1 + \frac{m_2^{2}}{2}\right] = 9.638\!\left[1 + \frac{0.25}{2}\right] = 9.638 \times 1.125$$

$$\boxed{P_{t2} = 10.843 \text{ kW}}$$

**Sideband power at $m_2 = 0.5$ [2 marks]:**

$$P_{SB2} = P_{t2} - P_c = 10.843 - 9.638 = 1.205 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Q(B)(b) style problems, students frequently forget that **carrier power does not change** when $m$ changes. It is the *sideband power* (and therefore total power) that scales with $m^{2}$. Always begin such problems by isolating $P_c$ from the *first* condition, then reuse it in the *second* condition. Mixing up the two stages typically costs 2 full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU board exam — print this and read it 5 minutes before entering the hall.**

- **Canonical AM equation:** $s(t) = A_c[1 + m\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. Memorise it in both compact and expanded forms.
- **Three-term expansion:** Carrier + LSB + USB. Each sideband amplitude is $mA_c/2$ (time-domain) or $mA_c/4$ (spectral impulse height).
- **Modulation index:** $m = A_m/A_c = (A_{\max}-A_{\min})/(A_{\max}+A_{\min})$.
- **Distortion-free envelope detection requires** $0 \le m \le 1$. For $m>1$, envelope clipping occurs.
- **Transmission bandwidth:** $BW = 2W$ (twice the highest message frequency). This is independent of $m$ and $A_c$.
- **Carrier power** $P_c = A_c^{2}/(2R)$ is *constant*; **sideband power** scales as $m^{2}$; **total power** $P_t = P_c(1 + m^{2}/2)$.
- **Efficiency** $\eta = m^{2}/(2+m^{2})$; **maximum** $1/3 = 33.33\%$ at $m=1$ — this is a frequently-asked KTU viva and short-note question.
- **AM is a linear modulation** because the sideband phasors are mirror-symmetric and their sum is always collinear with the carrier.
- **AM is bandwidth-inefficient:** only $1/3$ of total power is information-bearing at best; the carrier consumes $2/3$.
- **Real-world KTU use-cases:** MW/SW broadcasting, aircraft VHF voice, CB radio, optical ASK/OOK, foundational theory for DSB-SC, SSB, VSB, QAM.
- **Carrier is essential in AM** (unlike DSB-SC and SSB) because envelope detection needs an unscaled carrier term.
- **Modulation sensitivity** $k_a$ is a *hardware* parameter; the modulation index $m$ is a *signal* parameter — do not confuse them in derivations.
- **Drawing the spectrum:** always include *both* positive and negative frequency axes, and label the *heights* of all six impulses, not just three.
- **Fourier-transform identity to remember:** $\cos(2\pi f_0 t) \leftrightarrow \tfrac{1}{2}[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)]$ — a 2-mark saver in spectral problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Final pro tip:** When asked to *prove* or *derive*, never skip a step. The KTU 2024 scheme awards step marks generously for showing the product-to-sum identity, the substitution of $m$, and the Fourier transform pair. A 7-mark derivation can yield 6 marks even with one arithmetic slip — provided the logical flow is intact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Double-Side Band Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC) Modulation
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Double-Side Band Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC) Modulation

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Double-Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** is an amplitude modulation scheme in which the message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, and the resulting modulated wave contains **only the two sidebands** — the Upper Sideband (USB) and the Lower Sideband (LSB) — while the **carrier component is mathematically suppressed** (its amplitude becomes zero).

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the DSB-SC signal is defined as:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_c$ is the carrier frequency, and $m(t)$ is the baseband message signal with bandwidth $W$ Hz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** DSB-SC is a foundational modulation scheme in the Communication Engineering syllabus. It is a *linear modulation* technique and is the parent scheme from which SSB and VSB are derived by further spectral manipulation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a concert hall where a singer (the **message signal**) needs to be heard across a large stadium. The singer's voice alone cannot travel far. A loudspeaker system (the **carrier wave**) is used to project the sound.

In **conventional AM (DSB-TC)**, the loudspeaker is kept ON continuously — even when the singer pauses. This wastes a huge amount of electrical power (the carrier consumes ~67% of total transmitted power, even when no information is being sent).

In **DSB-SC modulation**, the loudspeaker is *gated* — it only operates when the singer is actually producing sound. The carrier itself is mathematically **multiplied** (turned ON/OFF) by the message signal. When the singer is silent, the speaker emits **zero power**. Hence, all transmitted power is "useful" information-bearing power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** DSB-SC = "Pure information transmission" — the carrier is sacrificed (suppressed) to maximize the proportion of power that carries actual information.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$):** Typically in the **HF, VHF, or higher** range (e.g., 500 kHz – 100 MHz for broadcast applications).
- **Message Bandwidth ($W$):** Audio signals typically have $W = 4$ kHz (telephony) to **15 kHz** (high-fidelity broadcast).
- **Transmission Efficiency ($\eta$):** For DSB-SC, $\eta = 100\%$ (theoretical), since **all transmitted power is in the sidebands**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Unlike DSB-TC, DSB-SC has no discrete carrier component in its frequency spectrum. This is the defining spectral signature of DSB-SC.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The generation of a DSB-SC signal follows a strict mathematical and physical process. Let us break it down:

**Step 1 — The Message Signal:**
The baseband message $m(t)$ is a low-frequency signal (e.g., voice, music, data) with bandwidth $W$ Hz. Its spectrum $M(f)$ is bandlimited to $\vert f \vert \leq W$.

**Step 2 — The Carrier Signal:**
A pure sinusoidal carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is generated by a stable local oscillator, with $f_c \gg W$.

**Step 3 — Multiplication (The Core Operation):**
The message and carrier are multiplied using a **balanced modulator** or a **ring modulator**. The output is the DSB-SC signal:

$$s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 4 — Spectral Translation:**
Multiplication in the time domain corresponds to **convolution in the frequency domain**. This shifts the baseband spectrum $M(f)$ to two new locations: $\pm f_c$.

$$S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \big[ M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) \big]$$

**Step 5 — The Carrier is "Suppressed":**
Notice that there is **no impulse** at $\pm f_c$ in $S(f)$. The delta functions seen in DSB-TC (conventional AM) are absent. Hence, the term "suppressed carrier."

**Step 6 — Bandwidth:**
Since the message has bandwidth $W$ Hz, the upper sideband occupies $[f_c - W, f_c]$ and the lower sideband occupies $[f_c, f_c + W]$. The total transmission bandwidth is:

$$BW_{DSB-SC} = 2W$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Fact:** DSB-SC has the **same bandwidth as DSB-TC** (conventional AM). The only difference is the absence of the carrier and the DC component, not the spectral width.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why multiply?** Multiplication is the simplest mathematical operation that **translates** a signal to a higher frequency band, making it suitable for radiation via antennas of reasonable size.
- **Why suppress the carrier?** The carrier carries **no information**. Suppressing it improves **power efficiency** dramatically and reduces the dynamic range requirements of RF power amplifiers.
- **How is the carrier suppressed?** Through a *balanced modulator* — a circuit where two AM signals are added in anti-phase, canceling the carrier term while reinforcing the sideband terms.
- **Why synchronous detection is required at the receiver?** Since the carrier is suppressed, the receiver must **regenerate** a local carrier that is *coherent* (same frequency and phase) with the original carrier. This is a non-trivial requirement.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

DSB-SC modulation forms the basis of:
- **Color television transmission** (the chrominance subcarrier uses DSB-SC).
- **Quadrature Multiplexing (QAM)**: Two DSB-SC signals can occupy the same band using sine and cosine carriers, doubling spectral efficiency.
- **Pilot-carrier SSB systems**: A low-level pilot is reinserted at the receiver for synchronous demodulation.
- **Software Defined Radio (SDR)** baseband processing pipelines.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | DSB-SC Time-Domain Signal | $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts (V) | Product of message and carrier |
| 2 | Carrier Signal | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts (V) | Pure sinusoid |
| 3 | DSB-SC Spectrum | $S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}\big[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)\big]$ | V/Hz | Symmetric about $\pm f_c$ |
| 4 | Transmission Bandwidth | $BW = 2W$ | Hz | Same as DSB-TC |
| 5 | Total Transmitted Power | $P_t = \frac{A_c^2 \, P_m}{2}$ | Watts (W) | Where $P_m$ = average power of $m(t)$ |
| 6 | Sideband Power (each) | $P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{P_t}{2}$ | Watts (W) | Both sidebands carry equal power |
| 7 | Transmission Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{P_{sidebands}}{P_{total}} = 100\%$ | % | All power is information-bearing |
| 8 | Modulation Index | $\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless | For sinusoidal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ |
| 9 | Demodulated Output | $v(t) = \frac{A_c^2}{2} m(t)$ | Volts (V) | After coherent detection with LO of amplitude $A_c$ |
| 10 | Phase Reversal (Sign Change) | At $m(t) = 0$ | — | Waveform flips sign every half-cycle of message |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** A DSB-SC waveform exhibits **phase reversals** at every zero crossing of the message signal. This is the time-domain signature that distinguishes it from DSB-TC (where the envelope follows the message).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Domain Expression of DSB-SC

We begin with the standard modulation definition. The DSB-SC signal is mathematically the **product** of the message and the carrier.

**Starting Point:**

Let the message be $m(t)$ and the carrier be $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

By definition of linear modulation (multiplication):

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$$

**Substituting the carrier expression:**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Factoring out the constant $A_c$:**

$$s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

This is the canonical time-domain expression for DSB-SC. ∎

> **Interpretation:** The envelope of $s(t)$ is **not** $\vert m(t) \vert$ (as in DSB-TC). Instead, the envelope **folds** at every zero-crossing of $m(t)$, producing a phase reversal. This is why envelope detection fails for DSB-SC.

## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency-Domain (Spectrum) of DSB-SC

**Step 1 — Apply the Modulation Theorem:**
If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(f)$, then:

$$x(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{2} X(f - f_c) + \frac{1}{2} X(f + f_c)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Variables:**
Here, $x(t) = m(t)$ and $X(f) = M(f)$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Modulation Theorem to Our Signal:**

$$s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{A_c}{2} M(f - f_c) + \frac{A_c}{2} M(f + f_c)$$

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form:**

$$S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2} \big[ M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c) \big]$$

**Step 5 — Verification for Sinusoidal Message:**
Let $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$. Then $M(f) = \frac{A_m}{2} \big[ \delta(f - f_m) + \delta(f + f_m) \big]$.

Substituting:

$$S(f) = \frac{A_c A_m}{4} \big[ \delta(f - f_c - f_m) + \delta(f - f_c + f_m) + \delta(f + f_c - f_m) + \delta(f + f_c + f_m) \big]$$

This shows **four impulses**: two on each side of the origin, separated by $f_m$ from $f_c$. No impulse at $\pm f_c$ alone — confirming carrier suppression. ∎

## 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth of DSB-SC

**Given:** Message signal $m(t)$ is bandlimited to $W$ Hz, i.e., $M(f) \neq 0$ only for $\vert f \vert \leq W$.

**Upper Sideband (USB):** Occupies the band $f_c \leq f \leq f_c + W$.

**Lower Sideband (LSB):** Occupies the band $f_c - W \leq f \leq f_c$.

**Total Bandwidth:**

$$BW = (f_c + W) - (f_c - W) = 2W$$

> **Conclusion:** The bandwidth of a DSB-SC signal is **twice the message bandwidth**, identical to DSB-TC and double that of SSB. ∎

## 3.4 Derivation: Transmitted Power of DSB-SC

**Step 1 — Power Formula for a Random Signal:**
The average power of a signal $s(t)$ over a resistor of $1\,\Omega$ is:

$$P_s = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} s^2(t) \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$:**

$$P_s = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} A_c^2 \, m^2(t) \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1 + \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:**

$$P_s = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} A_c^2 \, m^2(t) \cdot \frac{1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Split the Integral:**

$$P_s = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} m^2(t) \, dt + \frac{A_c^2}{2} \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} m^2(t) \cos(4\pi f_c t) \, dt$$

**Step 5 — The Second Integral Vanishes (Averaging Principle):**
Since $m^2(t)$ is a low-frequency baseband signal and $\cos(4\pi f_c t)$ is a high-frequency sinusoid with zero mean, their product integrates to zero over a long interval:

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} m^2(t) \cos(4\pi f_c t) \, dt = 0$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \cdot P_m$$

where $P_m = \overline{m^2(t)}$ is the average message power. ∎

**Step 7 — Transmission Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{sidebands}}{P_{total}} = \frac{P_t - P_{carrier}}{P_t} = \frac{P_t - 0}{P_t} = 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** In DSB-TC, $\eta_{max} = 33.33\%$ (at $\mu = 1$). In DSB-SC, $\eta = 100\%$ always. This **8 dB power advantage** is the chief reason DSB-SC (and SSB) is preferred in long-distance communication.

## 3.5 Derivation: Coherent (Synchronous) Demodulation

**Step 1 — Received Signal:**
The received DSB-SC signal (assuming ideal channel) is:

$$r(t) = s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by a Locally Generated Coherent Carrier:**
The receiver multiplies $r(t)$ by $\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$, where $\phi$ is the phase error.

$$v(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Product-to-Sum Identity:**

$$v(t) = \frac{A_c \, m(t)}{2} \big[ \cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi) \big]$$

**Step 4 — Pass Through a Low-Pass Filter (LPF):**
The LPF removes the high-frequency term $\cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi)$:

$$v_{out}(t) = \frac{A_c \, m(t)}{2} \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 5 — Perfect Synchronization ($\phi = 0$):**

$$v_{out}(t) = \frac{A_c \, m(t)}{2}$$

**Step 6 — DC Removal / Scaling:**
After removing the DC offset (if any), we recover the original message scaled by $A_c/2$. ∎

> **Phase Error Impact:** If $\phi \neq 0$, the recovered message is attenuated by $\cos(\phi)$, causing **signal fading**. Phase errors of $90°$ cause **complete signal loss** (quadrature null). This is why **PLL-based carrier recovery** is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — DSB-SC Modulator (Balanced Modulator)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeMsg["Message Signal m(t)"] --> nodeBM1["Balanced Modulator Block 1"]
    nodeC1["Carrier cos 2 pi fc t"] --> nodeBM1
    nodeMsg --> nodeBM2["Balanced Modulator Block 2"]
    nodeC2["Carrier cos 2 pi fc t shifted by 180 deg"] --> nodeBM2
    nodeBM1 --> nodeSum["Summing Junction"]
    nodeBM2 --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeOut["DSB-SC Output s(t)"]
```

> **Note on Operation:** The two balanced modulators produce DSB-AM signals with carriers in anti-phase. The summing junction cancels the carrier and reinforces the sidebands, yielding pure DSB-SC.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — DSB-SC Demodulator (Coherent Detector)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR["Received DSB-SC Signal r(t)"] --> nodeMult["Multiplier Block"]
    nodeLO["Local Oscillator cos 2 pi fc t"] --> nodeMult
    nodeMult --> nodeLPF["Low Pass Filter Cutoff W Hz"]
    nodeLPF --> nodeDC["DC Blocking Capacitor"]
    nodeDC --> nodeRec["Recovered Message m(t)"]
```

> **Note on Operation:** The product-to-sum identity creates a baseband component and a high-frequency term at $2f_c$. The LPF eliminates the high-frequency term, leaving the demodulated message.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of DSB-SC System (End-to-End)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Source: Message m(t) Bandwidth W"] --> nodeB["Balanced Modulator Hardware"]
    nodeLO1["Stable Local Oscillator fc MHz"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC["Channel: Bandlimited and Noisy AWGN"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Receiver Front End: BPF and LNA"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Synchronous Detector Multiplier"]
    nodeLO2["PLL Carrier Recovery fc"] --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF["Low Pass Filter Cutoff W"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["DC Block and Amplifier"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Destination: Recovered Message m(t)"]
```

> **Architecture Insight:** The dashed synchronization link between the PLL and the local oscillator is the **most critical** part of a DSB-SC receiver. Without it, coherent detection fails.

## 4.4 Spectral Diagram Description (Mermaid Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSpecA["Baseband M(f) Centered at 0 Hz Width 2W"] --> nodeSpecB["DSB-SC Spectrum S(f) Two sidebands at plus minus fc, No impulse at fc"]
```

> **Spectral Signature:** The DSB-SC spectrum is **two mirrored copies** of the baseband spectrum placed at $\pm f_c$, **with no central impulse** at the carrier frequencies — this is the visual hallmark of carrier suppression.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define DSB-SC modulation. Why is it called "suppressed carrier"?**

**Model Answer:**

DSB-SC (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier) modulation is a linear modulation technique in which the message signal $m(t)$ is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, producing a signal $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ that contains only the upper and lower sidebands.

It is called "suppressed carrier" because, unlike conventional AM (DSB-TC), the carrier component at frequency $f_c$ is **mathematically eliminated** from the transmitted spectrum. The frequency spectrum $S(f) = \frac{A_c}{2}[M(f-f_c) + M(f+f_c)]$ contains **no delta function at $f_c$**, meaning the carrier power is zero. This is achieved using a balanced modulator that cancels the carrier through anti-phase summation.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Compare DSB-SC and DSB-TC with respect to bandwidth, power efficiency, and demodulation technique.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | DSB-TC (Conventional AM) | DSB-SC |
|-----------|--------------------------|--------|
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ |
| Carrier Power | $P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$ | $0$ (Suppressed) |
| Transmission Efficiency $\eta$ | $\frac{\mu^2}{2+\mu^2} \leq 33.33\%$ | $100\%$ |
| Demodulation | Envelope detector (simple) | Coherent/Synchronous detector (complex) |
| Modulator | Simple multiplier with DC bias | Balanced modulator (complex) |
| Message Recovery | Trivial (envelope follower) | Requires carrier synchronization |

**Conclusion:** DSB-SC is more power-efficient but requires a complex receiver, whereas DSB-TC is receiver-friendly but wastes power in the carrier.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Alternative 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**a)** For a DSB-SC system, derive the expression for the modulated signal in both time and frequency domains. Starting from $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, sketch the spectrum. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Time Domain Derivation:**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Applying the identity $2\cos A \cos B = \cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)$:

$$s(t) = \frac{A_m A_c}{2} \big[ \cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t) \big]$$

**Frequency Domain Derivation:**

Taking the Fourier Transform:

$$S(f) = \frac{A_m A_c}{4} \big[ \delta(f - f_c - f_m) + \delta(f - f_c + f_m) + \delta(f + f_c - f_m) + \delta(f + f_c + f_m) \big]$$

**Spectrum Sketch (Descriptive):**

The spectrum consists of **four impulses** located at $\pm(f_c - f_m)$ and $\pm(f_c + f_m)$. There is **no impulse at $\pm f_c$**, confirming carrier suppression. The amplitudes of all four impulses are $\frac{A_m A_c}{4}$.

**Mark Allocation:** Time domain: 2 marks | Frequency domain: 2 marks | Spectrum sketch: 2 marks | Explanation: 1 mark.

---

**b)** Explain the coherent detection method for DSB-SC demodulation. Derive the output expression and discuss the effect of a phase error $\phi$ in the local oscillator. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Coherent Detector:** Multiplier → LPF → Output.

**Received Signal:** $r(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

**Multiplication with Local Carrier:** $\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$

$$v(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

**Apply product-to-sum identity:**

$$v(t) = \frac{A_c m(t)}{2} \big[ \cos(\phi) + \cos(4\pi f_c t + \phi) \big]$$

**After Low-Pass Filter:**

$$v_{out}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2} m(t) \cos(\phi)$$

**Effect of Phase Error:**
- If $\phi = 0$: Output is $\frac{A_c}{2} m(t)$ — **perfect demodulation**.
- If $\phi = 90°$: Output is $0$ — **quadrature null**, complete signal loss.
- For general $\phi$: Output is attenuated by factor $\cos(\phi)$.

**Practical Solution:** Use a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** at the receiver to lock the local oscillator to the carrier phase.

**Mark Allocation:** Block diagram: 1 mark | Derivation: 3 marks | Phase error analysis: 2 marks | Practical solution: 1 mark.

---

### Question B (Alternative 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation of a DSB-SC signal using a **balanced modulator**. Derive the expression for the output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Balanced Modulator:**

```
m(t) ──┬──→ [Multiplier 1] ──┐
        │                     │
        │                  [Summing] ──→ s(t) = DSB-SC
        │                     │
        ├──→ [Multiplier 2] ──┘
        │
A_c cos(2πf_c t) ──┬──→ [Multiplier 1]
                   │
                [-A_c cos(2πf_c t)] ──→ [Multiplier 2]
```

**Operation:**

Multiplier 1 output: $s_1(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

Multiplier 2 output: $s_2(t) = -A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

Summing junction:

$$s(t) = s_1(t) + s_2(t) = A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) - A_c m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) = 0$$

**Wait — this gives zero!** The above is *incorrect*. The correct balanced modulator uses the **square-law characteristic** of nonlinear devices.

**Correct Derivation (Square-Law Balanced Modulator):**

Let the nonlinear device have the characteristic: $v_o = a v_i + b v_i^2$.

**Branch 1:** $v_{i1} = m(t) + A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

$$v_{o1} = a m(t) + a A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + b m^2(t) + 2b m(t) A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + b A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Branch 2:** $v_{i2} = m(t) - A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$

$$v_{o2} = a m(t) - a A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + b m^2(t) - 2b m(t) A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + b A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Sum:** $v_{o1} + v_{o2} = 2a m(t) + 2b m^2(t) + 2b A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)$

**Difference (Summing with anti-phase for branch 2):**

$$s(t) = v_{o1} - v_{o2} = 2a A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + 4b m(t) A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

After bandpass filtering (which removes the first term):

$$s(t) = 4b A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

This is the **DSB-SC signal**, with carrier completely cancelled! ∎

**Mark Allocation:** Block diagram: 2 marks | Branch analysis: 3 marks | Final expression: 2 marks.

---

**b)** A DSB-SC signal is given by $s(t) = 10 \cos(2\pi \times 10^6 t) \cdot \cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$ volts. Find: (i) the carrier and message frequencies, (ii) the total transmitted power across a $50\,\Omega$ load, and (iii) the transmission efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $s(t) = 10 \cos(2\pi \times 10^6 t) \cdot \cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$

**Step 1 — Identify the Frequencies:**

Comparing with $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ (where the message is embedded as part of a unitary amplitude carrier-multiplied product, equivalent to $m(t) = \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and $A_c = 10$):

- Carrier frequency: $f_c = 10^6$ Hz $= 1$ MHz
- Message frequency: $f_m = 10^4$ Hz $= 10$ kHz

**Step 2 — Apply Product-to-Sum Identity:**

$$s(t) = 10 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \big[ \cos(2\pi(10^6 - 10^4)t) + \cos(2\pi(10^6 + 10^4)t) \big]$$

$$s(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \times 0.99 \times 10^6 t) + 5 \cos(2\pi \times 1.01 \times 10^6 t)$$

The two sideband components each have amplitude $5$ V.

**Step 3 — Total Transmitted Power:**

Each sideband power: $P_{SB} = \frac{(5)^2}{2 \times 50} = \frac{25}{100} = 0.25$ W

Total power: $P_t = 2 \times P_{SB} = 0.5$ W

**Alternative Direct Calculation:**

$$P_t = \frac{A_c^2 \cdot P_m}{2}$$

Here, $m(t) = \cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$ has peak $A_m = 1$ and $P_m = \frac{1^2}{2} = 0.5$.

$$P_t = \frac{10^2 \times 0.5}{2} = 25 \text{ W (normalized, in } 1\,\Omega)$$

For $50\,\Omega$: $P_t = \frac{25}{50} = 0.5$ W. ✓

**Step 4 — Transmission Efficiency:**

Since the carrier is suppressed, all power is in sidebands:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{sidebands}}{P_{total}} = \frac{0.5}{0.5} = 100\%$$

**Mark Allocation:** Frequency identification: 2 marks | Power calculation: 3 marks | Efficiency: 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in DSB-SC Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to include the $1/2$ factor** when applying the product-to-sum identity. Many students write $s(t) = A_c A_m \cos(\ldots)$ instead of $\frac{A_c A_m}{2} \cos(\ldots)$. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 
> 2. **Stating the bandwidth is $W$ instead of $2W$**. A common confusion with SSB. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 
> 3. **Claiming the demodulator can be an envelope detector.** This is a critical conceptual error. DSB-SC **requires** coherent detection. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 
> 4. **Ignoring phase error** $\phi$ in coherent detection derivations. Examiners expect explicit discussion of $\phi = 0$ and $\phi \neq 0$ cases. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
> 
> 5. **Spectral sketches missing the suppression of the central carrier impulse** at $\pm f_c$. A common visualization error. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
> 
> 6. **Confusing DSB-SC with SSB in bandwidth questions.** DSB-SC has $BW = 2W$; SSB has $BW = W$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DSB-SC signal:** $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Carrier is suppressed:** No spectral component at $\pm f_c$.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2W$ (same as DSB-TC, double that of SSB).
- **Power Efficiency:** $\eta = 100\%$ — all power is in sidebands.
- **Generation:** Balanced modulator (square-law or ring modulator).
- **Demodulation:** Coherent / synchronous detector — requires **carrier synchronization** (PLL).
- **Time-domain signature:** Phase reversals at every zero-crossing of $m(t)$.
- **Phase error impact:** $\phi = 0 \Rightarrow$ perfect recovery; $\phi = 90° \Rightarrow$ quadrature null.
- **Modulation index $\mu$:** Defined as $A_m/A_c$ for sinusoidal $m(t)$.
- **Spectral symmetry:** $S(f)$ is symmetric about $f = 0$ (Hermitian) and about $\pm f_c$.
- **Practical use:** Color TV chrominance, QAM, pilot-tone SSB systems.
- **Disadvantage:** Complex receiver (carrier recovery circuit required).
- **Advantage:** High power efficiency, simpler modulator than SSB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *"DSB-SC = Multiplication = Carrier Suppressed = 100% Efficient = Coherent Detection Mandatory = Bandwidth 2W."* Memorize this one-liner for instant recall during the examination.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Sideband Modulation (SSB)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Single Sideband Modulation (SSB)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Single Sideband Modulation (SSB)** is an amplitude modulation technique in which the carrier and **one of the two sidebands** (either the Upper Sideband or the Lower Sideband) of a conventional AM signal are suppressed before transmission. Only the remaining sideband is transmitted, thereby reducing the transmitted power and bandwidth by a factor of two compared to DSB-SC and conventional AM.

Mathematically, if the message signal is $m(t)$ and the carrier is $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the two SSB variants are:

- **Upper Sideband (USB)**: $s_{USB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$

- **Lower Sideband (LSB)**: $s_{LSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$

where $\hat{m}(t)$ denotes the **Hilbert Transform** of the message signal $m(t)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: SSB is a high-priority topic in Module 1 of Communication Engineering. Students must master its mathematical derivation, generation methods (Filter & Phase-Shift), and power/bandwidth comparison with AM/DSB-SC.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Gift-Wrapping Waste" Story**

Imagine you are sending a parcel (your information/message) across the world. Conventional AM is like wrapping the gift with **two huge layers of bubble wrap and a giant cardboard box** — most of what you pay for shipping is just packaging waste, not the actual gift. DSB-SC removes the cardboard but still keeps both bubble wrap layers. **SSB is like keeping only ONE bubble wrap layer** — the parcel is still safely protected, weighs half as much, costs half as much to ship, and arrives just as safely.

In radio engineering:
- The **gift** = message signal $m(t)$ (information)
- The **bubble wrap layers** = the two redundant sidebands
- The **cardboard box** = the carrier wave

By transmitting only **one sideband**, we achieve **100% power efficiency** (all transmitted power carries information) and **halve the bandwidth requirement**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Bandwidth of SSB**: $B_{SSB} = W$ (where $W$ is the message bandwidth) — **half** of DSB-SC and conventional AM.
- **Power Saving vs. Conventional AM**: $\dfrac{P_{t,AM} - P_{t,SSB}}{P_{t,AM}} = \dfrac{5}{6} \approx \mathbf{83.33\%}$ for a 100% modulated AM signal.
- **Carrier Frequency Range**: Typically $f_c \gg W$ (high-frequency carrier for practical transmission).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Do We Need SSB?

Starting from conventional AM:

$$s_{AM}(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Expanding using trigonometric identity, this produces:
1. A **carrier component** (no information) — wastes **66.67%** of total power.
2. **Two sidebands** (USB + LSB) — they are **mirror images** carrying **redundant information**.

Both sidebands carry the **same information**. Therefore, transmitting both is wasteful. SSB transmits only one, achieving:
- **Power efficiency = 100%**
- **Bandwidth efficiency doubled**

## 2.2 Generation of the SSB Expression — Conceptual Steps

**Step 1 — Start with DSB-SC**: 
$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Express via Inverse Fourier Transform**:
The USB corresponds to retaining only positive frequencies of $M(f)$ shifted above $f_c$, and the LSB corresponds to retaining only negative frequencies of $M(f)$ flipped above $f_c$.

**Step 3 — Use the Hilbert Transform**:
$$\hat{m}(t) = m(t) * \dfrac{1}{\pi t}$$

In frequency domain: $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\, M(f)$

This **90° phase shift network** is the mathematical foundation of the phase-shift SSB generation method.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | SSB Time Domain (USB) | $s_{USB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \dfrac{A_c}{2} \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 2 | SSB Time Domain (LSB) | $s_{LSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{A_c}{2} \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts |
| 3 | Hilbert Transform | $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\, M(f)$ | Frequency domain |
| 4 | SSB Bandwidth | $B_{SSB} = W$ | Hz |
| 5 | SSB Transmitted Power | $P_{SSB} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4R}\, \overline{m^2(t)} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4R}\, P_m$ | Watts |
| 6 | Conventional AM Power | $P_{AM} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}\left[1 + \dfrac{\overline{m^2(t)}}{2}\right]$ | Watts |
| 7 | Power Saved (vs. AM, 100% mod.) | $\dfrac{P_t}{P_c} = \dfrac{1}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)}$ | $\approx 16.67\%$ for tone mod. |
| 8 | Power Efficiency | $\eta = 100\%$ | All power = information |
| 9 | Demodulation | Coherent (synchronous) detection mandatory | Uses $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ locally |
| 10 | Coherent Demod Output | $v_o(t) = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\, m(t)$ | After LPF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on absolute values in tables**: We have used `\vert` and `\mid` notation (e.g., $\vert \overline{m^2(t)} \vert$) to avoid markdown table syntax conflicts with the vertical pipe symbol.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of SSB

- **AM Radio Broadcasting (Long-wave / Short-wave / Maritime)**: SSB is used for HF (3–30 MHz) communications where bandwidth and power are precious.
- **Military & Aeronautical Communication**: Voice channels use SSB to maximize spectral efficiency.
- **Point-to-Point Telephony**: Carrier telephony systems over long distances.
- **HF Radio for Aircraft and Ships**: Reliable long-distance communication.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From DSB-SC to SSB Using Hilbert Transform

### Step 1 — Frequency Domain Representation of DSB-SC

The Fourier transform of $s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is:

$$
S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\left[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\right]
$$

**Explanation:** Modulation by a cosine shifts $M(f)$ to $\pm f_c$ and halves its amplitude.

### Step 2 — Identify USB and LSB in Frequency Domain

For $f > f_c$: Only $M(f - f_c)$ exists (the **USB**).
For $-f_c < f < f_c$: Both contribute.
For $f < -f_c$: Only $M(f + f_c)$ exists (the **LSB**).

The USB portion of $S_{DSB-SC}(f)$ is:

$$
S_{USB}(f) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, M(f - f_c)\, u(f - f_c) + \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, M(f + f_c)\, u(-f - f_c)
$$

where $u(\cdot)$ is the unit step function.

### Step 3 — Express Step Function Using Signum

Recall the identity:
$$u(f - f_c) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{sgn}(f - f_c)\right]$$

**Explanation:** This decomposes "positive frequencies only" into a DC component (½) and an antisymmetric signum component (½·sgn).

Substituting into $S_{USB}(f)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{USB}(f) &= \dfrac{A_c}{4}\left[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\right] \\
&\quad + \dfrac{A_c}{4}\left[\text{sgn}(f - f_c)\, M(f - f_c) + \text{sgn}(-f - f_c)\, M(f + f_c)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Recognize the Hilbert Transform Property

Using the modulation property of the Hilbert transform:
- $m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{1}{2}[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)]$ — this is the DSB-SC part.
- $m(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t) \;\longleftrightarrow\; \dfrac{1}{2j}[M(f - f_c) - M(f + f_c)]$

And noting that $\text{sgn}(f - f_c)\, M(f - f_c)$ corresponds to a shifted Hilbert transform, we obtain:

$$
\dfrac{A_c}{4}\left[\text{sgn}(f - f_c) M(f - f_c) + \text{sgn}(-f - f_c) M(f + f_c)\right] = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\,\hat{M}(f)\, \text{(shifted)} 
$$

### Step 5 — Inverse Fourier Transform to Get Time Domain

Applying the inverse Fourier transform to each term in Step 3:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{USB}(t) &= \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The Hilbert transform of the message $\hat{m}(t)$ is multiplied with the sine carrier and **subtracted** for USB. This is the **Hartley modulator** output for USB.

### Step 6 — Similar Derivation for LSB

By flipping the sign of the sine term:

$$
s_{LSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Tip for KTU Exams**: USB has a **minus sign** before the sine term; LSB has a **plus sign**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power of SSB Signal

Given $s_{SSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \pm \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$

### Step 1 — Compute Average Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{SSB} &= \overline{s_{SSB}^2(t)} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)} + \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)\sin^2(2\pi f_c t)} \\
&\quad \pm \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m(t)\hat{m}(t)\sin(4\pi f_c t)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Squaring a sum gives three terms — square of first, square of second, cross-term.

### Step 2 — Use Orthogonality of $m(t)$ and $\hat{m}(t)$

It is a property of the Hilbert transform that $m(t)$ and $\hat{m}(t)$ are **orthogonal**, i.e.:

$$
\overline{m(t)\, \hat{m}(t)} = 0
$$

So the cross-term vanishes.

### Step 3 — Apply Time Averaging of Cosine/Sine Squares

$$
\overline{\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)} = \overline{\sin^2(2\pi f_c t)} = \dfrac{1}{2}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Power Expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{SSB} &= \dfrac{A_c^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)} + \dfrac{A_c^2}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\,\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} \\
&= \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)} \quad \text{(since } \overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} = \overline{m^2(t)}\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus: $\boxed{P_{SSB} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4R}\,P_m}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Comparison with Conventional AM

For 100% modulated AM with sinusoidal message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

### Conventional AM:
$$
P_{AM} = P_c\left[1 + \dfrac{\overline{m^2(t)}}{2}\right] = P_c\left[1 + \dfrac{A_m^2}{4}\right]
$$

For 100% modulation: $A_m = 1$ (normalized), so $\overline{m^2(t)} = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

$$
P_{AM} = P_c \cdot \dfrac{3}{2} = 1.5\,P_c
$$

### SSB:
$$
P_{SSB} = \dfrac{P_c}{4} \cdot \overline{m^2(t)} = \dfrac{P_c}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{P_c}{8} = 0.125\,P_c
$$

### Power Saved:
$$
\dfrac{P_{AM} - P_{SSB}}{P_{AM}} = \dfrac{1.5 - 0.125}{1.5} = \dfrac{1.375}{1.5} = \dfrac{11}{12} \approx 91.67\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction for general message**: For arbitrary messages with $\overline{m^2(t)} = \mu^2/2$ where $\mu$ is the modulation index, the power saving fraction is $\dfrac{2 + \mu^2 - \mu^2/2}{2 + \mu^2}$. For $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation), this equals $5/6 \approx 83.33\%$. The "91.67%" applies specifically to single-tone sinusoidal modulation.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Coherent Demodulation of SSB

Multiply received SSB by local carrier $A_c' \cos(2\pi f_c t)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v(t) &= s_{SSB}(t) \cdot A_c' \cos(2\pi f_c t) \\
&= \dfrac{A_c A_c'}{2}\, m(t)\cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \pm \dfrac{A_c A_c'}{2}\,\hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using $2\cos^2\theta = 1 + \cos(2\theta)$ and $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin(2\theta)$:

$$
v(t) = \dfrac{A_c A_c'}{4}\, m(t)[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)] \pm \dfrac{A_c A_c'}{4}\,\hat{m}(t)\sin(4\pi f_c t)
$$

After low-pass filtering (LPF), only the DC-term survives:

$$
v_o(t) = \dfrac{A_c A_c'}{4}\, m(t)
$$

**Explanation:** Coherent demodulation perfectly recovers $m(t)$, proving SSB's reversibility — no information is lost by suppressing one sideband.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Phase-Shift Method (Hartley Modulator)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> B[Multiplier M1]
    C[Local Oscillator cos 2 pi fc t] --> B
    B --> E[Summer / Subtractor]
    A --> F[Hilbert Transformer 90 deg phase shift network]
    F --> G[Multiplier M2]
    D[Local Oscillator sin 2 pi fc t] --> G
    G --> E
    E --> H[SSB Output s of t]
```

**Operation:** The message $m(t)$ is split into two paths:
- **Upper Path (I-branch)**: Multiplied with $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ → in-phase component.
- **Lower Path (Q-branch)**: Passed through a $-90°$ Hilbert transformer → $\hat{m}(t)$, then multiplied with $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ → quadrature component.
- **Summer** adds them for LSB; **Subtractor** removes one for USB.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Filter Method of SSB Generation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message m of t] --> B[Balanced Modulator]
    C[Carrier cos 2 pi fc t] --> B
    B --> D[DSB SC Output USB and LSB]
    D --> E[Bandpass Filter BPF]
    E --> F[SSB Signal Single Sideband]
```

**Operation:**
1. Message modulates carrier in a **balanced modulator** to produce DSB-SC (both USB and LSB).
2. A sharply-tuned **bandpass filter** centered at either $f_c + W$ (to extract USB) or $f_c - W$ (to extract LSB) selects only one sideband.
3. The output is the desired SSB signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note**: The Filter Method is conceptually simpler but requires extremely sharp filters — a major practical challenge, especially at low carrier frequencies where USB and LSB are very close.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coherent SSB Demodulator (Receiver)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Received SSB s t] --> B[Multiplier]
    C[Local Carrier cos 2 pi fc t synchronized] --> B
    B --> D[Low Pass Filter LPF cutoff W]
    D --> E[Recovered Message m of t]
```

**Critical Requirement**: The local oscillator at the receiver must be **synchronized in frequency and phase** with the transmitter carrier. Any frequency/phase offset causes severe distortion — this is the main drawback of SSB.

## 4.4 Mermaid Comparison Chart — AM vs DSB-SC vs SSB

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Modulation Scheme Comparison]
    P --> Q1[Bandwidth]
    P --> Q2[Power Efficiency]
    P --> Q3[Complexity]
    Q1 --> R1[AM = 2W]
    Q1 --> R2[DSB-SC = 2W]
    Q1 --> R3[SSB = W]
    Q2 --> S1[AM = 16.67 percent tone]
    Q2 --> S2[DSB-SC = 100 percent]
    Q2 --> S3[SSB = 100 percent]
    Q3 --> T1[AM = Simple]
    Q3 --> T2[DSB-SC = Moderate]
    Q3 --> T3[SSB = High needs coherent demod]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]

**Q: Define Single Sideband Modulation (SSB). Why is SSB preferred over conventional AM for long-distance communication?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** SSB is an amplitude modulation technique in which the carrier and one of the two sidebands of a conventional AM signal are suppressed before transmission. Only the remaining sideband (either USB or LSB) is transmitted.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):** SSB is preferred for long-distance communication because:
1. **Power efficiency = 100%** — all transmitted power carries information, unlike AM where 66.67% power is in the carrier.
2. **Bandwidth saving = 50%** — SSB requires only $W$ Hz versus $2W$ Hz for AM.
3. **Reduced noise** — narrower bandwidth admits less noise, improving SNR.
4. **Less fading in HF transmission** — fewer sidebands mean reduced selective fading.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Pattern]

**Q: What is the Hilbert transform of a signal? State its key properties used in SSB generation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Hilbert transform of a signal $m(t)$ is defined as:

$$\hat{m}(t) = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \dfrac{m(\tau)}{t - \tau}\,d\tau = m(t) * \dfrac{1}{\pi t}$$

It represents a **$-90°$ phase shift** for all frequency components.

**Key Properties (1.5 marks):**
1. **Frequency domain**: $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\, M(f)$
2. **Orthogonality**: $\overline{m(t)\,\hat{m}(t)} = 0$ (used in power calculation)
3. **Energy preservation**: $\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} = \overline{m^2(t)}$
4. **Double Hilbert**: $\hat{\hat{m}}(t) = -m(t)$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

**Q: Derive the time-domain expression for an SSB signal using the Hilbert transform. Hence, obtain the power of the SSB signal and compare it with conventional AM for 100% sinusoidal modulation.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of SSB Expression (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Start with DSB-SC signal:
$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Take Fourier transform:
$$S_{DSB-SC}(f) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)]$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the unit step function to isolate USB:
$$S_{USB}(f) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}[M(f - f_c)\,u(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\,u(-f - f_c)]$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Substitute $u(f) = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \text{sgn}(f)]$ and simplify using the Hilbert transform property $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\,M(f)$.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Take the inverse Fourier transform to obtain:
$$\boxed{s_{USB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)}$$

#### Part (b) — Power Calculation and Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Power of SSB:
$$P_{SSB} = \overline{s_{SSB}^2(t)} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)} + \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\cdot\dfrac{1}{2}\,\overline{\hat{m}^2(t)} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** For 100% sinusoidal modulation $m(t) = \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $\overline{m^2(t)} = 1/2$:
$$P_{SSB} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{8R} = \dfrac{P_c}{4}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Conventional AM at 100% modulation:
$$P_{AM} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}\left[1 + \dfrac{1}{2}\right] = \dfrac{3A_c^2}{4R} = \dfrac{3P_c}{2}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Comparison — Power saving:
$$\dfrac{P_{AM} - P_{SSB}}{P_{AM}} = \dfrac{3P_c/2 - P_c/4}{3P_c/2} = \dfrac{5}{6} \approx 83.33\%$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]

**Q: With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the Phase-Shift Method (Hartley Modulator) for SSB generation. Also explain the Filter Method and state its limitations.**

#### Part (a) — Phase-Shift Method (Hartley Modulator) (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**

```
m(t) ──┬─────────→ [×] ──→ (+) ──→ s_LSB(t)
       │              ↑
   cos ω_c t ─────────┘
       │
       └→ [−90° Hilbert] ──→ [×] ──→ (±) ──→ s_USB(t)
                          ↑
                     sin ω_c t
```

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The message $m(t)$ is applied directly to one balanced modulator (multiplied by $\cos 2\pi f_c t$).
2. The same message is passed through a **$-90°$ Hilbert transformer** to produce $\hat{m}(t)$, then applied to a second balanced modulator (multiplied by $\sin 2\pi f_c t$).
3. The two outputs are added to produce **LSB** or subtracted to produce **USB**.
4. This method works at any carrier frequency and requires no sharp filters.

**Mathematical Verification (2 marks):**
$$s_{LSB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$
$$s_{USB}(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \dfrac{A_c}{2}\, \hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

#### Part (b) — Filter Method and Limitations (7 marks)

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. The message modulates the carrier in a balanced modulator to produce DSB-SC (USB + LSB).
2. A highly selective bandpass filter centered at either $f_c + W$ (for USB) or $f_c - W$ (for LSB) passes only one sideband.
3. Output is the SSB signal.

**Block Diagram:**
```
m(t) ──→ [Balanced Modulator] ──→ DSB-SC ──→ [BPF] ──→ SSB
         ↑
    cos 2πf_c t
```

**Limitations (4 marks):**
1. **Sharp filter requirement**: USB and LSB are separated by only $2W$; for voice ($W = 4$ kHz), this is 8 kHz apart. Practical filters cannot have such sharp transitions below ~10 MHz.
2. **Low-frequency carrier problem**: At low $f_c$, the BPF cannot distinguish sidebands → must use high $f_c$ → then heterodyne down for transmission (multi-stage process).
3. **Filter design complexity**: Requires crystal/mechanical filters at HF frequencies; expensive.
4. **Phase distortion**: Practical BPFs introduce nonlinear phase, distorting the SSB waveform.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in USB vs LSB**: Forgetting that USB uses a **minus sign** before the sine term while LSB uses a **plus sign**. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of $A_c/2$**: Many students write $A_c\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ instead of $\frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. The factor ½ comes from the fact that we have suppressed the carrier AND one sideband from the original AM. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 
> 3. **Incorrect Power Saving Formula**: Writing "83.33%" without specifying that it applies to **100% sinusoidal modulation only**. For general modulation index $\mu$, the formula is different. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 
> 4. **Confusing DSB-SC with SSB Power**: DSB-SC power is $\frac{A_c^2}{2}\,\overline{m^2(t)}$, whereas SSB power is $\frac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)}$ — half of DSB-SC! Students often write them as the same. (Loss: 2 marks)
> 
> 5. **Skipping Hilbert Transform Justification**: In derivation problems, students must **explicitly state** the Hilbert transform property $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\,M(f)$ before substituting. Skipping this step costs 1–2 marks.
> 
> 6. **No Mention of Coherent Demodulation**: When discussing SSB demodulation, students often forget to mention that **synchronous (coherent) detection is mandatory** because SSB has no carrier. Envelope detection fails for SSB!

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SSB Modulation**

**Core Concepts:**
- ✅ SSB transmits **only one sideband** (USB or LSB) by suppressing the carrier and the other sideband.
- ✅ Achieves **100% power efficiency** and **50% bandwidth** compared to DSB-SC and conventional AM.

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- ✅ USB: $s_{USB}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\cos\omega_c t - \frac{A_c}{2}\,\hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$
- ✅ LSB: $s_{LSB}(t) = \frac{A_c}{2}\,m(t)\cos\omega_c t + \frac{A_c}{2}\,\hat{m}(t)\sin\omega_c t$
- ✅ Hilbert Transform: $\hat{M}(f) = -j\,\text{sgn}(f)\,M(f)$
- ✅ Power: $P_{SSB} = \frac{A_c^2}{4}\,\overline{m^2(t)} = \frac{A_c^2}{4R}\,P_m$
- ✅ Bandwidth: $B_{SSB} = W$
- ✅ Power saving vs. AM (100% tone): **5/6 ≈ 83.33%**

**Generation Methods:**
- ✅ **Filter Method**: DSB-SC → BPF → SSB. Simple concept but needs sharp filters.
- ✅ **Phase-Shift Method (Hartley)**: Uses Hilbert transformer and two multipliers. Works at any carrier frequency.
- ✅ **Weaver's Method (Third Method)**: Uses two-stage modulation; eliminates need for wideband Hilbert transformer.

**Demodulation:**
- ✅ **Coherent / Synchronous detection** is mandatory.
- ✅ Demodulated output: $v_o(t) = \frac{A_c A_c'}{4}\,m(t)$

**Advantages:**
- ✅ Power efficient (100% efficiency).
- ✅ Bandwidth efficient (only $W$ Hz needed).
- ✅ Reduced noise due to narrow bandwidth.

**Disadvantages:**
- ✅ Complex receiver (needs phase-locked local oscillator).
- ✅ Cannot use simple envelope detector.
- ✅ Filter method requires sharp filters (challenging at low $f_c$).
- ✅ Hilbert transformer design is difficult over wide band.

**Applications:**
- ✅ HF radio communication (maritime, military, aeronautical).
- ✅ Point-to-point long-distance telephony.
- ✅ AM broadcast (single-sideband AM — a modern variant).
- ✅ TV sound transmission (vestigial sideband).

**Memory Trick — "USB Minus, LSB Plus":**
- **U**pper **S**ide **B**and uses **M**inus sign
- **L**ower **S**ide **B**and uses **P**lus sign
- (USBM, LSBP — easy to recall!)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison of Spectrum
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison of Spectrum

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Spectrum
In **Communication Engineering**, the **spectrum** of a signal is a mathematical representation of the signal in the **frequency domain**. It describes how the signal's amplitude, phase, or power is distributed across different frequency components. A signal $x(t)$ and its spectrum $X(f)$ form a **Fourier Transform pair**, giving us two equivalent but perceptually different ways of looking at the same physical phenomenon.

> [!IMPORTANT]
