> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The spectrum of a signal is the set of frequencies contained in the signal. For a periodic signal, the spectrum is **discrete** (line spectrum); for an aperiodic signal, it is **continuous**.

### 1.2 Types of Spectra
A complete frequency-domain description of a signal requires comparing the following fundamental spectral representations:

1. **Amplitude Spectrum $\vert X(f) \vert$** – The magnitude of the Fourier coefficients/Fourier transform versus frequency. Indicates "how much" of each frequency is present.
2. **Phase Spectrum $\angle X(f)$** – The argument of $X(f)$ versus frequency. Indicates the relative time alignment of each frequency component.
3. **Power Spectral Density (PSD), $S_x(f)$** – For **power signals**, it is the average power per unit bandwidth. Found via the **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem**.
4. **Energy Spectral Density (ESD), $G_x(f)$** – For **energy signals**, it is the total energy per unit bandwidth.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are a **chef tasting a complex curry**. Your tongue (the time domain) feels the *overall taste over time* — sweet, spicy, sour, all blended. But if you take that curry to a **chemistry lab** (the frequency domain) and pass it through a **spectrometer**, you get a precise chart showing exactly how much turmeric, chili, and salt (different "frequencies") it contains.

- **Amplitude Spectrum** = The amount of each ingredient.
- **Phase Spectrum** = The order in which the ingredients were added (timing).
- **Power/Energy Spectrum** = The total "nutritional content" (energy) carried by each ingredient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:** Frequency is measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, with $1 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ cycle/second}$. The standard angular frequency relation is $\omega = 2\pi f$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Compare Spectra?
A signal looks very different in the time domain versus the frequency domain. Comparison is essential because:
- **Time Domain** $x(t)$: Tells us *when* and *what magnitude* the signal has.
- **Frequency Domain** $X(f)$: Tells us *which frequencies* make up the signal.

Communication systems (modulation, filtering, multiplexing) operate **entirely in the frequency domain**. Hence, comparing spectra of different signals is a board-favorite question.

### 2.2 Comparison Logic (Step-by-Step)
1. **Identify the signal type** → Is it **periodic** (Power Signal) or **aperiodic** (Energy Signal)?
2. **Apply the correct transform**:
   - Periodic → **Exponential/Trigonometric Fourier Series** → Discrete line spectrum.
   - Aperiodic → **Fourier Transform** → Continuous spectrum.
3. **Decompose** the complex spectrum $X(f)$ into magnitude and phase.
4. **Compute spectral density** (Power or Energy) by squaring the magnitude.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Domain | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|------------------------|--------|------|--------------------|
| 1 | Fourier Transform | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi ft}\, dt$ | Aperiodic signals | Volt-second | $\int \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ |
| 2 | Inverse Fourier Transform | $x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi ft}\, df$ | Aperiodic signals | Volt | Always valid for transformable $X(f)$ |
| 3 | Amplitude Spectrum | $\vert X(f) \vert = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2\{X(f)\} + \text{Im}^2\{X(f)\}}$ | All signals | Volt-second | Always non-negative |
| 4 | Phase Spectrum | $\angle X(f) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\text{Im}\{X(f)\}}{\text{Re}\{X(f)\}}\right)$ | All signals | Radians (rad) | Defined only when $\vert X(f) \vert \neq 0$ |
| 5 | Power Spectral Density (PSD) | $S_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}$ | Power signals | Watts/Hz | Total power $P = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_x(f)\, df$ |
| 6 | Energy Spectral Density (ESD) | $G_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ | Energy signals | Joules/Hz | Total energy $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} G_x(f)\, df$ |
| 7 | Parseval's Theorem (Energy) | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$ | Energy signals | Joules | Confirms energy conservation |
| 8 | Parseval's Theorem (Power) | $P = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert c_n \vert^2$ | Power signals | Watts | Discrete frequency sum |
| 9 | Fourier Series Coefficient | $c_n = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{0}^{T_0} x(t) e^{-jn\omega_0 t}\, dt$ | Periodic signals | Volt | $\omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$ |
| 10 | Wiener-Khinchin Relation | $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ | Power signals | Watts/Hz | $R_x(\tau)$ = Autocorrelation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** A common mistake is confusing **Power Spectral Density** with **Energy Spectral Density**. The rule of thumb: **Energy signals** (e.g., single rectangular pulse) use ESD; **Power signals** (e.g., periodic square wave, random noise) use PSD.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Radio Broadcasting (AM/FM):** Spectrum comparison ensures the modulated signal fits inside an allocated bandwidth (e.g., 10 kHz for AM).
- **Optical Fiber Communication:** Different wavelengths (frequencies) of light are multiplexed; spectrum comparison prevents **inter-symbol interference (ISI)**.
- **5G/6G Wireless:** Engineers compare spectra of OFDM subcarriers to maximize spectral efficiency in bits/second/Hz.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Power Spectrum and Autocorrelation
The **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem** establishes a beautiful duality: the Power Spectral Density and Autocorrelation Function are Fourier Transform pairs.

**Starting Definition:** Autocorrelation function of a power signal.

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Truncate the signal to length $T$, forming $x_T(t)$, and compute its Fourier Transform.

$$X_T(f) = \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t) e^{-j2\pi ft}\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Express the autocorrelation of the truncated signal.

$$R_{x_T}(\tau) = \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x_T(t)\, x_T(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 3:** Apply the inverse Fourier transform identity to $X_T(f) X_T^{*}(f) = \vert X_T(f) \vert^2$.

$$R_{x_T}(\tau) = \frac{1}{T} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X_T(f) \vert^2 e^{j2\pi f\tau}\, df$$

**Step 4:** Take the limit as $T \to \infty$ on both sides.

$$\lim_{T \to \infty} R_{x_T}(\tau) = R_x(\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T} \right] e^{j2\pi f\tau}\, df$$

**Step 5:** Identify the term in brackets as the **Power Spectral Density**.

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}}$$

**Step 6:** Conclude the Fourier relationship.

$$R_x(\tau) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} S_x(f)$$

### 3.2 Worked Example: Comparing Spectra of a Rectangular vs Triangular Pulse
A common **KTU board question** asks to compute and compare the spectra of standard pulses.

**Given:** 
- Rectangular pulse: $x_1(t) = A$ for $\vert t \vert \leq \tau/2$, else $0$.
- Triangular pulse: $x_2(t)$ with base $2\tau$ and peak $A$.

**Step A: Rectangular pulse Fourier Transform.**

$$X_1(f) = \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} A e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$$

**Step B: Evaluate the integral.**

$$X_1(f) = A \left[ \frac{e^{-j2\pi f t}}{-j2\pi f} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} = A \cdot \frac{e^{j\pi f \tau} - e^{-j\pi f \tau}}{j2\pi f}$$

**Step C: Apply Euler's identity** ($\sin\theta = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$).

$$X_1(f) = A\tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi f \tau)}{\pi f \tau} = A\tau \cdot \text{sinc}(f\tau)$$

**Step D: Triangular pulse Fourier Transform** (using convolution property: triangle = rect $\ast$ rect, so its spectrum is a squared sinc).

$$X_2(f) = A\tau \cdot \text{sinc}^2(f\tau)$$

**Step E: Compare the amplitude spectra.**

$$\vert X_1(f) \vert = A\tau \,\vert \text{sinc}(f\tau) \vert, \qquad \vert X_2(f) \vert = A\tau \,\text{sinc}^2(f\tau)$$

**Step F: Compare the spectral widths** (using first zero crossing).

| Parameter | Rectangular Pulse | Triangular Pulse |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------|
| Time duration | $\tau$ | $2\tau$ |
| Spectrum shape | $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ | $\text{sinc}^2(f\tau)$ |
| First null at $f =$ | $\frac{1}{\tau}$ | $\frac{1}{\tau}$ |
| Side-lobe amplitude | First side-lobe $\approx -13.2$ dB | First side-lobe $\approx -26.4$ dB |
| Energy | $A^2 \tau$ | $\frac{2}{3} A^2 \tau$ |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Power vs Energy Spectrum

| Feature | Power Spectrum $S_x(f)$ | Energy Spectrum $G_x(f)$ |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| Applicable to | Periodic / Random (Power) Signals | Aperiodic, finite-duration (Energy) Signals |
| Definition | $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ | $G_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ |
| Existence | Exists even if $X(f)$ does not | Requires $X(f)$ to exist |
| Units | Watts/Hz | Joules/Hz |
| Total value | $P = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_x(f)\, df$ (Watts) | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} G_x(f)\, df$ (Joules) |
| Example | Sine wave, square wave | Single rectangular pulse |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spectrum Analysis
The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **signal processing chain** for comparing different spectra in a communication receiver.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal xt] --> B{Signal Type?}
    B -- Periodic Power Signal --> C[Fourier Series cn]
    B -- Aperiodic Energy Signal --> D[Fourier Transform Xf]
    C --> E[Line Spectrum Discrete]
    D --> F[Continuous Spectrum]
    E --> G[Decompose Magnitude and Phase]
    F --> G
    G --> H1[Amplitude Spectrum Xf magnitude]
    G --> H2[Phase Spectrum angle Xf]
    E --> I1[Power Spectral Density Sx f]
    F --> I2[Energy Spectral Density Gx f]
    H1 --> J[Comparison Table]
    H2 --> J
    I1 --> J
    I2 --> J
    J --> K[Design Filter or Allocate Bandwidth]
```

### 4.2 Spectrum Comparison Flow for Different Signals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Rectangular Pulse] --> B1[Amplitude Spectrum: sinc]
    A2[Triangular Pulse] --> B2[Amplitude Spectrum: sinc squared]
    A3[Sinusoidal Signal] --> B3[Line Spectrum: Impulse at f0]
    A4[Square Wave] --> B4[Line Spectrum: Odd Harmonics only]
    B1 --> C[Spectral Roll-off Comparison]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    C --> D{Smoothest Spectrum?}
    D -- Triangular --> E[Triangular: Faster roll-off]
    D -- Rectangular --> F[Rectangular: Slower roll-off]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Insight:** A **smoother time-domain signal** (like a Gaussian pulse) produces a **faster-decaying spectrum** (also Gaussian). This is the time-frequency duality principle. The triangular pulse, being smoother than the rectangular pulse, has a spectrum (sinc²) that decays *faster* than the rectangular pulse's spectrum (sinc).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Power Spectral Density (PSD) and Energy Spectral Density (ESD).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Power Spectral Density $S_x(f)$ | Energy Spectral Density $G_x(f)$ |
|-----------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| Signal Type | Power signals (periodic, random) | Energy signals (aperiodic, finite duration) |
| Mathematical Definition | $S_x(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X_T(f) \vert^2}{T}$ | $G_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ |
| Unit | Watts/Hz | Joules/Hz |
| Total Value | $P = \int S_x(f) df$ | $E = \int G_x(f) df$ |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define amplitude spectrum and phase spectrum. What is the significance of phase spectrum in communication?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Amplitude Spectrum $\vert X(f) \vert$:** The plot of the magnitude of the Fourier Transform $X(f)$ versus frequency. It indicates the relative strength of various frequency components present in the signal.
- **Phase Spectrum $\angle X(f)$:** The plot of the angle of $X(f)$ versus frequency. It indicates the relative time shifts of the sinusoidal components.
- **Significance:** The phase spectrum preserves the **time-domain shape** of the signal. A loss of phase information (only keeping magnitude) means we lose the timing/structure of the signal, even though amplitude information is retained.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** and the **autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau)$ of a power signal. Show that they form a Fourier Transform pair. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A periodic signal $x(t) = 2 + 4\cos(100\pi t) + 3\cos(200\pi t + \pi/4)$ is applied to a system. Sketch and compare the **amplitude spectrum** and **phase spectrum** of the signal. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Starting with the autocorrelation function:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Truncate $x(t)$ to $x_T(t)$ for $-T/2 \leq t \leq T/2$. Its Fourier transform is $X_T(f) = \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t) e^{-j2\pi ft}\, dt$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Express $R_x(\tau)$ using the truncated signal.

$$R_{x_T}(\tau) = \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x_T(t)\, x_T(t+\tau)\, dt$$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Substitute the inverse Fourier representation of $x_T(t+\tau)$.

$$x_T(t+\tau) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X_T(f') e^{j2\pi f'(t+\tau)}\, df'$$ **(1 Mark)**

**Step 4:** Perform the time integration, using $\int_{-T/2}^{T/2} e^{j2\pi (f+f')t}\, dt = T\, \delta(f+f')$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 5:** Take the limit $T \to \infty$ to identify the **PSD** as the Fourier Transform of $R_x(\tau)$.

$$\boxed{S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_x(\tau) e^{-j2\pi f\tau}\, d\tau}$$ **(2 Marks)**

**(b) Amplitude and Phase Spectrum Sketch (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify frequency components from $x(t) = 2 + 4\cos(100\pi t) + 3\cos(200\pi t + \pi/4)$. **(1 Mark)**
- DC component: $2$ at $f = 0$ Hz.
- $4\cos(100\pi t) = 4\cos(2\pi \cdot 50\, t)$ → $f_1 = 50$ Hz, amplitude 4, phase 0.
- $3\cos(200\pi t + \pi/4) = 3\cos(2\pi \cdot 100\, t + \pi/4)$ → $f_2 = 100$ Hz, amplitude 3, phase $\pi/4$.

**Step 2:** Construct amplitude spectrum (showing both positive and negative frequencies). **(3 Marks)**

$$\vert X(f) \vert = 2\delta(f) + 2\delta(f-50) + 2\delta(f+50) + 1.5\,\delta(f-100) + 1.5\,\delta(f+100)$$

**Step 3:** Construct phase spectrum. **(2 Marks)**
- At $f = 0$: phase undefined (DC).
- At $f = \pm 50$ Hz: phase = $0$ rad.
- At $f = 100$ Hz: phase = $+\pi/4$ rad.
- At $f = -100$ Hz: phase = $-\pi/4$ rad (odd symmetry of phase).

**Step 4:** Compare: Amplitude spectrum is **even-symmetric**; phase spectrum is **odd-symmetric** for real signals. **(1 Mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Explain the difference between **continuous spectrum** and **discrete (line) spectrum**. Support your answer with a mathematical example of a periodic rectangular pulse train. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 5\, \text{sinc}(100t)$ is transmitted over a channel. Find the **bandwidth** of the signal and sketch its **energy spectral density**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Continuous vs Discrete Spectrum (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** A **discrete spectrum** arises from periodic signals, where energy is concentrated at discrete frequencies $f = nf_0$. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** A **continuous spectrum** arises from aperiodic (energy) signals, where energy is spread over a continuous range of frequencies. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Consider a rectangular pulse train of period $T_0$ and pulse width $\tau$. Its Fourier Series coefficient is: **(2 Marks)**

$$c_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_0} \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 4:** As $T_0 \to \infty$ (becomes aperiodic), the spectral lines become infinitely close → continuous spectrum $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 5:** Comparison table: **(1 Mark)**

| Parameter | Discrete Spectrum | Continuous Spectrum |
|-----------|------------------|--------------------|
| Source | Periodic signals | Aperiodic signals |
| Math tool | Fourier Series | Fourier Transform |
| Frequency axis | $f = nf_0$ | Continuous $f$ |
| Example | Square wave | Single pulse |

**(b) Bandwidth and ESD of $x(t) = 5\, \text{sinc}(100t)$ (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Recall the Fourier pair: $\text{sinc}(2Wt) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{2W}\, \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{f}{2W}\right)$ (with proper normalization). **(1 Mark)**

**Step 2:** Identify $2W = 100$ → $W = 50$ Hz. The signal is band-limited. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 3:** Compute the Fourier Transform: $X(f) = \frac{5}{100}\, \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{f}{100}\right) = 0.05$ for $\vert f \vert \leq 50$ Hz, else $0$. **(2 Marks)**

**Step 4:** Energy Spectral Density: $G_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2 = (0.05)^2 = 0.0025$ for $\vert f \vert \leq 50$ Hz. **(1 Mark)**

**Step 5:** Bandwidth $B = 50$ Hz. Sketch: A flat-topped rectangle of height $0.0025$ extending from $-50$ Hz to $+50$ Hz. **(2 Marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing PSD with ESD:** Always check if the signal is periodic (use PSD) or aperiodic (use ESD). Examiners deduct **2 marks** instantly for this.
> 2. **Forgetting symmetry:** For real signals, amplitude spectrum is **even**, phase spectrum is **odd**. Skipping this loses **1 mark** in sketches.
> 3. **Missing Units:** Always write Hz, rad/s, W/Hz, or J/Hz. A unit-less spectrum answer is incomplete.
> 4. **Sign error in phase:** At negative frequencies, the phase is the **negative** of the positive-frequency phase. Many students plot the same phase on both sides.
> 5. **Skipping Parseval's check:** Examiners love Parseval's theorem as a verification step. Mention it for **bonus 1 mark**.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Spectrum** = Frequency-domain representation of a signal.
- **Amplitude Spectrum** $\vert X(f) \vert$ = Magnitude, always **even and non-negative** for real signals.
- **Phase Spectrum** $\angle X(f)$ = Angle, **odd-symmetric** for real signals.
- **Power Spectrum** $S_x(f)$ → used for **periodic/random** signals; obtained via **Wiener-Khinchin Theorem**: $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$.
- **Energy Spectrum** $G_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ → used for **aperiodic finite-duration** signals.
- **Discrete Line Spectrum** → Fourier Series; frequencies at $nf_0$.
- **Continuous Spectrum** → Fourier Transform; spread over all $f$.
- **Parseval's Theorem** links time-domain and frequency-domain energy/power — a must-memorize verification formula.
- **Bandwidth** is the range of frequencies where the spectrum has significant non-zero values (typically the first null of sinc).
- **Smoother time-domain signal ⇒ faster frequency-domain roll-off** (uncertainty principle).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power and Efficiency of All the Three Variants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power and Efficiency of All the Three Variants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Three Variants of Amplitude Modulation

In KTU 2024 Scheme Communication Engineering (PEAET411), **Module 1** introduces **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** as the foundational analog modulation scheme. When a message signal $m(t)$ modulates a high-frequency carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the resulting modulated wave can be transmitted in three principal variants, each with drastically different power requirements and transmission efficiencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**: Amplitude Modulation is the process in which the **amplitude of the high-frequency carrier signal** is varied in accordance with the **instantaneous amplitude of the message (modulating) signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

The three variants studied under Module 1 are:

| # | Variant Name | Carrier Sent? | Sidebands Sent? |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **DSB-SC** (Double Sideband – Suppressed Carrier) | ❌ No | ✅ Both USB & LSB |
| 2 | **DSB-FC** (Double Sideband – Full Carrier) / Conventional AM | ✅ Yes | ✅ Both USB & LSB |
| 3 | **SSB-SC** (Single Sideband – Suppressed Carrier) | ❌ No | ✅ Only ONE sideband |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Delivery Truck" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you run a courier company and must send a fragile package (your **message / information**) from city A to city B (from **transmitter to receiver**).

- **DSB-FC (Conventional AM)** is like sending the package inside a **huge, heavy, fully-loaded truck** plus two identical escort vans (one on each side — the two sidebands). The big truck (carrier) burns enormous fuel but carries **no actual information**; it's only there to "carry" the vans. This is the standard AM broadcast used in commercial radio, but it is **extremely fuel-inefficient**.

- **DSB-SC (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier)** is like sending the **two escort vans together** but **scrapping the heavy truck**. Both vans carry **duplicate copies** of the package, so one van is wasted bandwidth. All the transmitter power now goes into the actual information. **Efficiency reaches 100%** because no power is wasted on the carrier.

- **SSB-SC (Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier)** is like sending **only one escort van** carrying the package — the other van is discarded. This is the **most spectrally efficient and power efficient** form of AM. It is used in military HF communication, amateur radio, and ship-to-shore telephony.

The **trade-off** in SSB is the complexity of the receiver (it must precisely regenerate the carrier using a local oscillator to recover the original message).

---

## 1.3 The Common Building Block — Modulated Signal Expression

For all three variants, the starting point is the standard AM expression derived from the product of the carrier and the modulating signal:

$$s(t) = A_c \left[1 + k_a \, m(t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where:
- $A_c$ → amplitude of the carrier (in **volts**)
- $k_a$ → amplitude sensitivity of the modulator (in **V⁻¹**)
- $m(t)$ → normalized modulating message signal with $\vert m(t) \vert_{\max} \le 1$
- $\mu = k_a \cdot m(t)_{\max}$ → **modulation index** (dimensionless, normally $\mu \le 1$ to avoid envelope distortion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The modulation index **$\mu$** (Greek letter *mu*) is the single most important parameter that controls power distribution between the carrier and sidebands. It is **directly tested** in KTU ESE Part A and Part B questions.

For a single-tone modulating signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

The three terms represent the **carrier**, the **Lower Sideband (LSB)**, and the **Upper Sideband (USB)** — and how each variant treats these three components defines its power and efficiency.

---

## 1.4 Why Study Power and Efficiency?

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Relevance:** Power calculations determine the **transmitter power supply rating, antenna size, battery life, heat dissipation, and overall link budget**. Efficiency determines how much of the precious DC power drawn from the source is actually converted into usable information-bearing sideband power — a critical parameter for broadcast transmitters and portable communication devices.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Requisite: Standard Powers of Sinusoids

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Before deriving AM powers, recall that the **average power of a cosine wave** of amplitude $A$ across a $1\,\Omega$ resistive load is $A^2/2$.

$$P_{avg} = \frac{A^2}{2R} \quad \text{for a load } R \text{ (assumed } 1\,\Omega \text{ in normalized derivations)}$$

This result arises because the RMS value of $\cos(\omega t)$ is $1/\sqrt{2}$, so $P = V_{rms}^2/R = (A/\sqrt{2})^2/R = A^2/2R$.

---

## 2.2 Variant 1 — DSB-FC (Conventional / Full Carrier AM)

### 2.2.1 Signal Expression

$$s_{DSB\text{-}FC}(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

### 2.2.2 Power Breakdown

**Carrier Power** $P_c$:

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \quad (\text{for } R=1\,\Omega)$$

**Power in Each Sideband** $P_{SB}$ (LSB and USB have equal power):

$$P_{SB} = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

**Total Transmitted Power** $P_t$:

$$P_t = P_c + 2 P_{SB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + 2 \cdot \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)}$$

### 2.2.3 Transmission Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{DSB\text{-}FC} = \frac{P_{SB,total}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2} \times 100\%}$$

- At **$\mu = 1$** (100% modulation, maximum without distortion): $\eta = \dfrac{1/2}{1 + 1/2} = \dfrac{1/3} = 33.33\%$
- At **$\mu = 0.5$**: $\eta = \dfrac{0.125}{1.125} = 11.11\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often report "33%" efficiency as a *fixed* property of AM. It is **NOT fixed** — it depends entirely on $\mu$. The maximum achievable efficiency of conventional AM at distortion-free modulation ($\mu=1$) is **only 33.33%**. This means **two-thirds of transmitter power is always wasted** on the carrier which carries no message.

---

## 2.3 Variant 2 — DSB-SC (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier)

### 2.3.1 Signal Expression

The carrier term is mathematically subtracted, leaving only the two sidebands:

$$s_{DSB\text{-}SC}(t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

Equivalently, using $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (for normalized message):

$$s_{DSB\text{-}SC}(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### 2.3.2 Power Breakdown

There is **NO carrier** ($P_c = 0$). Total power is entirely in the sidebands:

$$P_t = 2 \cdot \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}}$$

### 2.3.3 Transmission Efficiency

Since **all** transmitted power contains the message:

$$\boxed{\eta_{DSB\text{-}SC} = 100\% \quad \text{(always)}}$$

---

## 2.4 Variant 3 — SSB-SC (Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier)

### 2.4.1 Signal Expression

Only one sideband is transmitted (say, USB):

$$s_{SSB}(t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

### 2.4.2 Power Breakdown

There is **only ONE sideband**, so its power alone is the total power:

$$P_t = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{P_{SSB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{4}}$$

### 2.4.3 Transmission Efficiency

$$\boxed{\eta_{SSB} = 100\% \quad \text{(always)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** SSB-SC transmits **half** the power of DSB-SC because it discards one of the two redundant sidebands. Since both sidebands carry the **same information** (mirror images of each other), discarding one does not lose any information — but it **halves the transmitted power** and **halves the bandwidth**. This is why SSB is the preferred choice for power-starved applications like maritime and aviation HF radio.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | DSB-FC (Conventional AM) | DSB-SC | SSB-SC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carrier Power $P_c$ | $A_c^2/2$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| Power per Sideband | $\mu^2 A_c^2/8$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/8$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/8$ |
| Total Sideband Power $P_{SB}$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/4$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/4$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/8$ |
| Total Transmitted Power $P_t$ | $A_c^2/2 (1 + \mu^2/2)$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/4$ | $\mu^2 A_c^2/8$ |
| Transmission Efficiency $\eta$ | $\mu^2/(2+\mu^2) \cdot 100\%$ | $100\%$ | $100\%$ |
| Max Efficiency ($\mu = 1$) | $\mathbf{33.33\%}$ | $100\%$ | $100\%$ |
| Bandwidth $BW$ | $2 f_m$ | $2 f_m$ | $f_m$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ in modular conditions has been written as plain text ("modulus") to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Always verify $\vert m(t) \vert_{\max} \le 1$ when computing $\mu$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Broadcast Radio (MW/SW)**: Uses **DSB-FC** because the receivers (crystal sets, cheap AM radios) are simple and demodulation does not need carrier synchronization.
2. **TV Video Transmission**: Uses **Vestigial Sideband (VSB)** — a hybrid close to SSB that preserves low-frequency components.
3. **Military & Amateur HF Radio**: Uses **SSB-SC** because the **bandwidth savings** allow more channels in crowded HF bands, and **power savings** matter on battery / generator operation.
4. **Telemetry & Instrumentation**: Uses **DSB-SC** because efficiency is high and coherent demodulation is acceptable in fixed installations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Power of Conventional AM (DSB-FC)

**Given:** Single-tone modulating signal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and modulation index $\mu = A_m/A_c$ (assuming $k_a = 1/A_c$).

**Step 1 — Write the modulated wave** by substituting into the general AM equation:

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Expand using the product-to-sum identity** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$\begin{aligned}
s(t) &= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \\
&= A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]
\end{aligned}$$

> The three terms are mutually orthogonal sinusoids at different frequencies — each contributes **independent** average power.

**Step 3 — Compute carrier average power** across $1\,\Omega$:

$$P_c = \left\langle A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \right\rangle = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compute sideband average power.** For the USB term with amplitude $\mu A_c/2$:

$$P_{USB} = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

By symmetry, $P_{LSB} = \mu^2 A_c^2/8$.

**Step 5 — Total power by superposition:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} \\
&= \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8} \\
&= \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $A_c^2/2$** to express in compact form:

$$\boxed{P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency derivation.** The useful power is the sideband power only:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB,total}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2/4}{A_c^2/2 \cdot (1 + \mu^2/2)} = \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by 2:

$$\boxed{\eta_{DSB\text{-}FC} = \frac{\mu^2}{\mu^2 + 2} \times 100\%}$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate the maximum.** For $\mu = 1$:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{1+2} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\%$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Power of DSB-SC

**Given:** Signal $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$.

**Step 1 — Expand** using $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s_{DSB\text{-}SC}(t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

> There is **no DC term** $A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — the carrier is mathematically suppressed.

**Step 2 — Power per sideband:**

$$P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Total power:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_t &= P_{USB} + P_{LSB} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8} \\
&= \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency.** All power carries the message:

$$\boxed{\eta_{DSB\text{-}SC} = 100\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Power of SSB-SC

**Given:** Only the upper sideband is retained, $s(t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$.

**Step 1 — Power of this single cosine of amplitude $\mu A_c/2$:**

$$P_t = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{P_{SSB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}}$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency.** All transmitted power is information-bearing:

$$\boxed{\eta_{SSB} = 100\%}$$

**Step 3 — Cross-comparison.** For the same $A_c$ and same $\mu = 1$:

$$P_{DSB\text{-}FC} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \frac{3 A_c^2}{4}$$

$$P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = \frac{A_c^2}{4}$$

$$P_{SSB} = \frac{A_c^2}{8}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ratio Check:** $P_{DSB\text{-}FC} : P_{DSB\text{-}SC} : P_{SSB} = 6 : 2 : 1$. Conventional AM wastes **6× more power** than SSB for the same message — this is precisely why SSB dominates long-distance point-to-point links.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 600 kHz carrier of amplitude 100 V is amplitude-modulated by a 5 kHz sinusoidal signal of amplitude 60 V. Calculate the carrier power, sideband power, total power, and efficiency for **(a)** DSB-FC, **(b)** DSB-SC, and **(c)** SSB-SC. Assume a $50\,\Omega$ load.

**Step 1 — Compute modulation index** $\mu = A_m/A_c = 60/100 = 0.6$.

**Step 2 — Carrier power across $50\,\Omega$:**

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{(100)^2}{2 \times 50} = 100 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — DSB-FC results:**

$$P_{SB,total} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2} = \frac{(0.6)^2 \times 100}{2} = 18 \text{ W}$$

$$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 100\left(1 + \frac{0.36}{2}\right) = 118 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{18}{118} \times 100\% = 15.25\%$$

**Step 4 — DSB-SC results:**

$$P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2} = 18 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{DSB\text{-}SC} = 100\%$$

**Step 5 — SSB-SC results:**

$$P_{SSB} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{4} = 9 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{SSB} = 100\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** All three variants deliver **the same sideband power** (18 W) when transmitting a single-tone signal — but DSB-FC needs to push a **100 W carrier** along with it. SSB transmits **only 9 W** because it transmits **one sideband only** (half of 18 W).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Allocation Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The diagram below shows the **functional power-flow architecture** for the three AM variants. It maps how the total DC input power gets divided between the carrier, the useful sidebands, and the wasted spectrum.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DCSource["DC Power Supply"] --> TxChain["Transmitter Chain"]
    TxChain --> Mod["AM Modulator Block"]
    Mod --> SignalOut["Modulated Signal s of t"]
    SignalOut --> Splitter{"Carrier Present?"}

    Splitter -->|"Yes DSB FC"| CarrierBlock["Carrier Term A_c cos 2 pi f_c t"]
    Splitter -->|"No DSB SC or SSB"| NoCarrier["Carrier Suppressed P_c equals 0"]

    CarrierBlock --> CarrierPower["Carrier Power P_c equals A_c squared over 2R"]
    CarrierBlock --> Wasted["Wasted Power No Information"]
    NoCarrier --> NoWasted["Zero Wasted Carrier Power"]

    Wasted --> Antenna1["Transmitted RF Total Power P_t"]
    NoWasted --> Antenna2["Transmitted RF Total Power P_t"]
    CarrierPower --> Antenna1

    Antenna1 --> DSBFC_Out["DSB FC P_t equals P_c times 1 plus mu squared over 2"]
    Antenna2 --> DSBOut{"Which Variant?"}

    DSBOut -->|"DSB SC"| DSBOut_Final["Both Sidebands P_t equals mu squared A_c squared over 4"]
    DSBOut -->|"SSB SC"| SSBOut_Final["Single Sideband Only P_t equals mu squared A_c squared over 8"]

    DSBFC_Out --> Eff1["Efficiency eta equals mu squared over mu squared plus 2 times 100"]
    DSBOut_Final --> Eff2["Efficiency equals 100 percent"]
    SSBOut_Final --> Eff3["Efficiency equals 100 percent"]

    style Eff1 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style Eff2 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
    style Eff3 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
    style Wasted fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style NoWasted fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.2 Frequency Spectrum Comparison Diagram (Mermaid)

This block diagram maps the **spectral occupancy** of each variant for a single-tone modulating signal of frequency $f_m$. Each node represents a frequency component and its amplitude weight.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Message["Message Spectrum at f_m"] --> Modulation["Multiplication by Carrier at f_c"]

    Modulation --> DSBFC_Spec["DSB FC Spectrum"]
    Modulation --> DSBSC_Spec["DSB SC Spectrum"]
    Modulation --> SSBSC_Spec["SSB SC Spectrum"]

    DSBFC_Spec --> Carrier_Tone["Impulse at f_c with Weight A_c over 2"]
    DSBFC_Spec --> USB_Tone["Impulse at f_c plus f_m Weight mu A_c over 4"]
    DSBFC_Spec --> LSB_Tone["Impulse at f_c minus f_m Weight mu A_c over 4"]
    Carrier_Tone --> Note1["Useless Carrier 67 percent of P_t at mu equals 1"]
    USB_Tone --> Note2["Information USB 16.67 percent of P_t"]
    LSB_Tone --> Note3["Information LSB 16.67 percent of P_t Duplicate of USB"]

    DSBSC_Spec --> USB2["USB Only mu A_c over 4"]
    DSBSC_Spec --> LSB2["LSB Only mu A_c over 4"]
    USB2 --> Note4["Information USB 50 percent of P_t"]
    LSB2 --> Note5["Information LSB 50 percent of P_t"]

    SSBSC_Spec --> USB3["USB Only mu A_c over 4"]
    USB3 --> Note6["Single Sideband Carries 100 percent of P_t"]

    style Note1 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style Note2 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
    style Note3 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
    style Note4 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
    style Note5 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
    style Note6 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.3 Power vs Modulation Index Trade-off (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This visualization explains **why** $\mu = 1$ is the practical limit and how efficiency varies as $\mu$ increases.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MuAxis["Modulation Index mu from 0 to 1"] --> EffCalc["Compute eta equals mu squared over mu squared plus 2"]

    EffCalc --> MuPoint0["mu equals 0 Silent Carrier eta equals 0 percent"]
    EffCalc --> MuPoint05["mu equals 0.5 eta equals 11.11 percent"]
    EffCalc --> MuPoint1["mu equals 1.0 eta equals 33.33 percent MAX"]

    MuPoint1 --> DistortionWarning["Above mu equals 1 Envelope Distortion Over modulation"]
    DistortionWarning --> RxFailure["Receiver Cannot Demodulate Cleanly"]

    style MuPoint1 fill:#ffe5b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style DistortionWarning fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style RxFailure fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000
    style MuPoint0 fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#666666
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this diagram carefully:** As $\mu \to 0$, the carrier dominates and efficiency collapses to 0%. As $\mu \to 1$, efficiency peaks at 33.33%. Pushing $\mu$ beyond 1 causes **envelope distortion** — the modulated waveform's envelope no longer matches the original message, and information is lost during demodulation. This is a **standard 7-mark KTU question** on the topic.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]`**

> Define modulation index for an AM signal. A 750 kHz carrier is amplitude modulated by an audio signal of bandwidth 5 kHz. If the peak voltage of the modulating signal is 4 V and that of the carrier is 16 V, determine the modulation index and the transmission bandwidth.

**Model Answer:**

The **modulation index $\mu$** is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the modulating signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier:

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{4}{16} = 0.25$$

The transmission bandwidth for conventional AM is **twice the highest modulating frequency**:

$$BW = 2 \times f_{m,\max} = 2 \times 5 \text{ kHz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

The frequencies present are: LSB at $750 - 5 = 745$ kHz, Carrier at $750$ kHz, USB at $750 + 5 = 755$ kHz. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]`**

> List the three variants of amplitude modulation and compare their transmission efficiencies. Why is the efficiency of conventional AM only 33.33% at most?

**Model Answer:**

The three variants are:

1. **DSB-FC** (Conventional AM) — carrier and both sidebands transmitted.
2. **DSB-SC** (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier) — both sidebands only, carrier suppressed.
3. **SSB-SC** (Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier) — only one sideband transmitted, carrier suppressed.

Efficiencies:

$$\eta_{DSB\text{-}FC} = \frac{\mu^2}{\mu^2 + 2} \times 100\%, \quad \eta_{DSB\text{-}SC} = 100\%, \quad \eta_{SSB} = 100\%$$

The efficiency of conventional AM is limited because a large portion of the total transmitted power resides in the **carrier**, which contains **no information**. The carrier consumes $P_c = A_c^2/2$ watts continuously, regardless of whether $m(t)$ is present or zero. At maximum allowable modulation ($\mu = 1$), the sidebands carry only one-third of the total power; the remaining two-thirds is wasted in the carrier — making 33.33% the absolute ceiling. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Solve both sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Set A]`**

> **(a)** With the help of mathematical expressions, derive the total transmitted power and the transmission efficiency of a conventional AM (DSB-FC) signal for a single-tone modulating signal. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 1 MHz carrier of amplitude 200 V is amplitude-modulated by a 5 kHz sinusoidal signal of amplitude 100 V. Calculate:
> 1. Modulation index
> 2. Sideband power
> 3. Total transmitted power
> 4. Transmission efficiency
> 5. The bandwidth of the AM signal
>
> Assume a load resistance of $50\,\Omega$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Let the modulating signal be $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ and the carrier be $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.

The standard AM equation is:

$$s(t) = A_c[1 + \mu \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad \text{where } \mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Expanding using $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$$

Computing the average power of each term (assuming $1\,\Omega$ load):

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}, \quad P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

Total transmitted power:

$$P_t = P_c + P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = \frac{A_c^2}{2} + \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

Sideband power:

$$P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2}$$

Efficiency (ratio of information-carrying power to total):

$$\eta = \frac{P_{SB}}{P_t} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2/4}{A_c^2/2 \cdot (1 + \mu^2/2)} = \frac{\mu^2}{\mu^2 + 2}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Given: $A_c = 200$ V, $A_m = 100$ V, $f_c = 1$ MHz, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $R = 50\,\Omega$.

**(i) Modulation index:**

$$\mu = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{100}{200} = 0.5$$

**(ii) Sideband power:**

$$P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2R} = \frac{(200)^2}{2 \times 50} = 400 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{SB} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2} = \frac{(0.5)^2 \times 400}{2} = 50 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Total transmitted power:**

$$P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 400 \left(1 + \frac{0.25}{2}\right) = 400 \times 1.125 = 450 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Transmission efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{50}{450} \times 100\% = 11.11\%$$

(Equivalently: $\eta = \dfrac{\mu^2}{\mu^2 + 2} = \dfrac{0.25}{2.25} = 11.11\%$)

**(v) Bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use $P_c = A_c^2/2$ instead of $A_c^2/(2R)$ when the load is given as $R \neq 1\,\Omega$. **Always divide by $R$** — the formula $A^2/2$ assumes a normalized $1\,\Omega$ load. Losing 1 mark here is the most common error. Also remember to express the final power in **Watts**, not volts.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice Alternative**
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Set B]`**

> **(a)** Derive expressions for the total transmitted power and transmission efficiency of **(i) DSB-SC** and **(ii) SSB-SC** signals. Show that both achieve 100% efficiency. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** An SSB transmitter radiates 10 W of power when the modulation index is 0.6. Compute:
> 1. The carrier power of the equivalent unmodulated carrier
> 2. The total power if the same signal were transmitted as DSB-SC
> 3. The total power if the same signal were transmitted as conventional AM (DSB-FC)
>
> State the bandwidth comparison among the three variants for a message bandwidth of 4 kHz. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivations:**

**(i) DSB-SC:**

The signal is $s(t) = A_c \, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$.

Power of each sideband: $P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = \mu^2 A_c^2/8$.

Total power:

$$P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = P_{USB} + P_{LSB} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{4}$$

Since there is no carrier, the **entire** transmitted power lies in the sidebands, giving:

$$\eta_{DSB\text{-}SC} = \frac{P_{DSB\text{-}SC}}{P_{DSB\text{-}SC}} = 100\%$$

**(ii) SSB-SC:**

The signal contains only one sideband: $s(t) = \frac{\mu A_c}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t]$.

Power of this single sideband:

$$P_{SSB} = \frac{(\mu A_c/2)^2}{2} = \frac{\mu^2 A_c^2}{8}$$

Since this is the only transmitted component, $\eta_{SSB} = 100\%$.

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

Given: $P_{SSB} = 10$ W, $\mu = 0.6$.

**(i) Carrier power of equivalent unmodulated carrier:**

From $P_{SSB} = \mu^2 P_c/4$:

$$P_c = \frac{4 \cdot P_{SSB}}{\mu^2} = \frac{4 \times 10}{(0.6)^2} = \frac{40}{0.36} = 111.11 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Total power if DSB-SC:**

$$P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = \frac{\mu^2 P_c}{2} = \frac{0.36 \times 111.11}{2} = 20 \text{ W}$$

(Verification: DSB-SC has 2 sidebands, so it is exactly twice the SSB power.)

**(iii) Total power if DSB-FC:**

$$P_t = P_c\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 111.11\left(1 + \frac{0.36}{2}\right) = 111.11 \times 1.18 = 131.11 \text{ W}$$

**Bandwidth comparison for $f_m = 4$ kHz:**

- DSB-FC: $BW = 2 f_m = 8$ kHz
- DSB-SC: $BW = 2 f_m = 8$ kHz
- SSB-SC: $BW = f_m = 4$ kHz ← **most bandwidth-efficient**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** For Part (b), students frequently confuse $P_{SSB}$ with $P_{DSB\text{-}SC}$. Remember: **DSB-SC has two sidebands, SSB has only one**. So under identical modulation conditions, $P_{DSB\text{-}SC} = 2 \times P_{SSB}$. Losing 2 marks is common when this relationship is forgotten.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 1**

- ⭐ The **three variants** are **DSB-FC (Conventional AM)**, **DSB-SC**, and **SSB-SC**.
- ⭐ **Modulation index $\mu = A_m/A_c$**, must satisfy $\mu \le 1$ for distortion-free envelope demodulation.
- ⭐ **Carrier power** (in any equivalent carrier reference) is always $P_c = A_c^2/(2R)$.
- ⭐ **DSB-FC total power**: $P_t = P_c(1 + \mu^2/2)$.
- ⭐ **DSB-FC efficiency**: $\eta = \mu^2/(\mu^2 + 2) \times 100\%$; maximum is **33.33% at $\mu = 1$**.
- ⭐ **DSB-SC total power**: $P_t = \mu^2 A_c^2/4 = \mu^2 P_c/2$; efficiency is **100%**.
- ⭐ **SSB-SC total power**: $P_t = \mu^2 A_c^2/8 = \mu^2 P_c/4$; efficiency is **100%**.
- ⭐ **Bandwidth**: DSB-FC and DSB-SC occupy $2 f_m$; SSB-SC occupies only $f_m$ — **most spectrally efficient**.
- ⭐ **Power ratio** for the same modulation at $\mu=1$: $P_{DSB\text{-}FC} : P_{DSB\text{-}SC} : P_{SSB} = 6 : 2 : 1$.
- ⭐ The **carrier carries no information** in any AM variant — it is a power and bandwidth overhead.
- ⭐ For numerical problems, **always include the load resistance $R$** in power calculations if specified.
- ⭐ For multi-tone modulation, replace $\mu^2$ with $\overline{\mu^2} = \sum \mu_i^2/2$ (the sum of squared modulation indices of individual tones).
- ⭐ **SSB** is used in HF military, amateur, and maritime radio; **DSB-FC** dominates commercial MW/SW broadcasting; **DSB-SC** is used in telemetry and coherent detection systems.
- ⭐ The KTU 2024 ESE commonly tests the **comparison table** between the three variants — memorize the power, efficiency, and bandwidth columns.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to "compare" the three variants, always structure your answer around the **three pillars — Power, Efficiency, and Bandwidth**. This guarantees full marks for the comparative analysis part of any 7-mark or 14-mark KTU Module 1 question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplitude Modulator Circuits - Balanced Modulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amplitude Modulator Circuits - Balanced Modulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Balanced Modulator** is an amplitude modulation circuit that multiplies the modulating baseband signal $m(t)$ with the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in such a way that the **carrier component is mathematically cancelled** at the output, leaving only the **Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** signal. The term "balanced" refers to the symmetric circuit topology (typically a bridge or push-pull arrangement) where two matched signal paths reinforce the sidebands while cancelling the carrier.

Mathematically, the ideal balanced modulator output is:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Balanced modulators are the fundamental building block for generating DSB-SC, SSB, and vestigial sideband (VSB) signals. The course code PEAET411 (Communication Engineering) lists this under Module 1: "Amplitude Modulator Circuits."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "See-Saw" Mixer

Imagine a perfectly balanced **see-saw (teeter-totter)**. Two children of **equal weight** sit on opposite ends. When a parent pushes the see-saw, both children move in **opposite directions simultaneously**. If you are standing on the ground and can only observe the *average position* of the two children, you see **zero motion** (the carrier cancels).

Now, suppose one child is given a small **secret drumbeat (the message)** to tap on the see-saw plank. The drumbeat transmits energy to the plank, and the plank vibrates with the drum's rhythm. The side vibrations (sidebands) are what you "hear" — the up-down motion of the see-saw's pivot (the carrier) is gone, but the rhythm is preserved. This is precisely how a balanced modulator works.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Cheat Box

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Definitions for KTU Examinations:**
> - **DSB-SC:** Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier. The output contains **upper sideband (USB)** and **lower sideband (LSB)** but **no** carrier.
> - **Carrier Suppression Ratio:** The ratio (in dB) of the unwanted carrier leakage to the desired sideband level. Typical value: **40 dB to 60 dB**.
> - **Switching Modulator:** A balanced modulator in which the carrier is driven hard enough to act as a **two-position electronic switch** (square-wave equivalent).
> - **Diode Ring Modulator:** A four-diode bridge topology used in classical balanced modulator designs.
> - **IC Balanced Modulator:** A monolithic chip (e.g., **MC1496 / MC1596 / AD630**) that performs balanced multiplication using Gilbert Cell architecture.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Carrier Suppression Occurs

A balanced modulator contains **two identical multipliers** (or two matched switching paths) connected such that the carrier enters them in **opposite phase**, while the modulating signal enters them in the **same phase**.

Let the two path outputs be:
- **Path 1:** $s_1(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t)$
- **Path 2:** $s_2(t) = m(t) \cdot [-c(t)] = -m(t) \cdot c(t)$

When summed, $s_1(t) + s_2(t) = 0$, which is *trivial*. The **real-world trick** is that the carrier is used as a **switching signal** (square wave) rather than a continuous sinusoid. The square wave has equal positive and negative half-cycles, so when summed at the output, the fundamental component (and all odd harmonics) is **cancelled**, but the sidebands (which are products of $m(t)$ with these harmonics) survive.

### 2.2 Switching Modulator Analysis (Fourier Series of the Switching Function)

The carrier-driven switch generates a unipolar square wave $g(t)$:

$$g(t) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{when } c(t) > 0 \\ -1 & \text{when } c(t) < 0 \end{cases}$$

The Fourier series expansion of $g(t)$:

$$g(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{(2n+1)} \cos(2\pi(2n+1)f_c t)$$

The output of the balanced (switching) modulator is:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot g(t) = \frac{4}{\pi} m(t) \left[ \cos(2\pi f_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(2\pi \cdot 3f_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(2\pi \cdot 5f_c t) - \cdots \right]$$

After low-pass or band-pass filtering, only the fundamental sidebands around $f_c$ remain — yielding the **DSB-SC** signal.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Units** | **Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carrier Signal | $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Reference oscillation |
| Modulating Signal | $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ | Volts | Information-bearing |
| **DSB-SC Output** | $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts | Suppressed carrier output |
| **DSB-SC (Single Tone)** | $s(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\left[\cos 2\pi(f_c-f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c+f_m)t\right]$ | Volts | Two sidebands, no carrier |
| Switching Function | $g(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\cos\omega_c t - \frac{1}{3}\cos 3\omega_c t + \frac{1}{5}\cos 5\omega_c t - \cdots\right]$ | Dimensionless | Square-wave Fourier series |
| Modulation Index (DSB-SC) | $\mu = A_m / A_c$ | Unitless | For envelope reference only |
| Output Power (DSB-SC) | $P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{4R}\langle m^2(t)\rangle$ | Watts | Carrier power is **zero** |
| Carrier Suppression Ratio | $CSR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_c^{\text{leak}}}{A_c^{\text{ideal}}}\right)$ | **dB** | Quality metric (lower is better) |
| Bandwidth of DSB-SC | $BW = 2 W$ (where $W$ is message bandwidth) | **Hz** | Same as conventional AM |
| Gilbert Cell Transconductance | $I_{out} = I_{EE} \cdot \tanh\left(\frac{v_1}{2V_T}\right) \cdot \tanh\left(\frac{v_2}{2V_T}\right)$ | Amps | Internal IC operation |
| Sideband Power Ratio | $P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = P_t / 2$ | Watts | Equal upper and lower sidebands |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where balanced modulators are deployed in industry:**
> - **Single Sideband (SSB) Transmitters:** The balanced modulator output passes through a **sideband filter** to yield SSB-SC, drastically saving power and bandwidth.
> - **Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM):** Two balanced modulators operate with carriers in **phase quadrature** to encode two independent data streams in the same bandwidth.
> - **Synchronous Detection (Coherent Demodulation):** A balanced modulator at the receiver multiplies the incoming signal with a locally generated carrier, recovering the baseband.
> - **Radio-Frequency (RF) Mixers:** Modern communication front-ends use **Gilbert Cell** balanced mixers in superheterodyne receivers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — DSB-SC Output for a Single-Tone Modulating Signal

**Given:**
- Carrier: $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
- Modulating signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$
- Balanced modulator is ideal (perfect carrier cancellation).

**Goal:** Derive the time-domain and frequency-domain expression of the output.

**Step 1 — Multiplication of modulating signal and carrier**

The ideal balanced modulator multiplies the two inputs:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cdot A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

This uses the **product-to-sum trigonometric identity**:

$$\cos(\alpha)\cos(\beta) = \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the identity**

Substitute $\alpha = 2\pi f_c t$ and $\beta = 2\pi f_m t$:

$$s(t) = A_c A_m \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\cos(2\pi f_c t - 2\pi f_m t) + \cos(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor and simplify**

$$s(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\left[\cos\left(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right) + \cos\left(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify the two sideband components**

- **Lower Sideband (LSB):** $\dfrac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos\left(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t\right)$ — exists at frequency $f_c - f_m$
- **Upper Sideband (USB):** $\dfrac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos\left(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right)$ — exists at frequency $f_c + f_m$

**Step 5 — Confirm carrier absence**

The DC carrier term $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is **NOT** present. This is the defining feature of DSB-SC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Insight:** If you write the final answer as $\frac{A_c A_m}{2}\cos(\omega_c t)\cos(\omega_m t)$ without applying the identity, the examiner will **not award full marks** because the sideband structure is not visible.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Switching Modulator Output and Harmonic Content

**Given:**
- Switching function: $g(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_c t) - \cdots\right]$
- Modulating signal: $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$

**Step 1 — Write the product**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot g(t)$$

$$s(t) = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi} \cos(\omega_m t) \left[\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_c t) - \cdots\right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply product-to-sum identity term by term**

Using $\cos(A)\cos(B) = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\Bigg[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

$$- \frac{1}{3}\cos((3\omega_c - \omega_m)t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos((3\omega_c + \omega_m)t)$$

$$+ \frac{1}{5}\cos((5\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos((5\omega_c + \omega_m)t) - \cdots\Bigg]$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the coefficient**

$$s(t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}\left[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right] - \frac{2 A_m}{3\pi}\left[\cos((3\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((3\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right] + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Identify the filter requirement**

The first bracketed term is the **desired DSB-SC** around $f_c$. The remaining terms are centered at $3f_c, 5f_c, \ldots$ and are removed by a **band-pass filter** centered at $f_c$ with bandwidth $2W$.

**Step 5 — Compare with ideal DSB-SC**

From Derivation 1, the ideal DSB-SC amplitude is $\frac{A_c A_m}{2}$. From this switching analysis, the filtered output amplitude is $\frac{2 A_m}{\pi}$, which corresponds to choosing $A_c = 4/\pi$ in normalized form — a common KTU normalization.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power and Efficiency Comparison

**Conventional AM (with carrier):**

$$P_{AM} = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$

where $P_c$ is the carrier power and $\mu$ is the modulation index.

**DSB-SC:**

$$P_{DSB-SC} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2}$$

**Step 1 — Carrier power is removed in DSB-SC**

$P_{DSB-SC}$ contains only sideband power. The "carrier" power term $P_c$ is mathematically **absent** because the carrier itself is suppressed.

**Step 2 — Power efficiency**

$$\eta_{DSB-SC} = \frac{P_{\text{sidebands}}}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{P_c \mu^2 / 2}{P_c \mu^2 / 2} = 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare with conventional AM efficiency**

For conventional AM at $\mu = 1$:

$$\eta_{AM} = \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = 33.3\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** DSB-SC delivers **100% power efficiency** because all transmitted power resides in the information-bearing sidebands. This is the primary motivation for using balanced modulators in long-distance and satellite communication.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Balanced Modulator Topologies

| **Parameter** | **Diode Bridge** | **Transistor Pair** | **Gilbert Cell IC (MC1496)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carrier Suppression | 30 – 40 dB | 30 – 50 dB | **50 – 65 dB** |
| Frequency Range | Up to 100 MHz | Up to 50 MHz | Up to 300 MHz (with care) |
| Power Consumption | Low (passive if biased) | Medium | High (active biasing) |
| Linearity | Moderate (diode non-linearity) | Good | **Excellent** |
| Adjustment | Transformer taps | Potentiometer trim | Single external resistor |
| Modern Use | Educational demos | Discrete RF designs | **Industry standard** |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Low (mass-produced IC) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.4 Frequency Spectrum — DSB-SC vs Conventional AM

| **Signal Type** | **Spectrum Line at $f_c$** | **Sidebands at $f_c \pm f_m$** | **Efficiency** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Conventional AM | Large discrete line (carrier power) | Two sidebands (half of total) | Max 33.3% |
| **DSB-SC (Balanced)** | **Absent (suppressed)** | Two sidebands (all of total) | **100%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Spectrum:** The presence of a tall vertical line at the carrier frequency $f_c$ in the spectrum plot is the visual signature of conventional AM. A balanced modulator's output plot will show **two symmetric sideband peaks** with **no central carrier spike** — this is the "balanced" or "suppressed carrier" signature.
