<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Q: Define a balanced modulator and state its primary output signal type.**

**Model Answer:**
A balanced modulator is an electronic circuit that performs the multiplication of a modulating baseband signal $m(t)$ with a carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in a symmetric (bridge) topology such that the carrier component is **suppressed** at the output. The primary output is a **Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier (DSB-SC)** signal of the form $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. The "balanced" property arises because two matched signal paths reinforce the sidebands while cancelling the carrier through destructive interference. The carrier suppression ratio typically exceeds **40 dB** in well-designed circuits, and the transmitted power efficiency is **100%** since no power is wasted in the carrier.

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1]`

**Q: Why is the carrier suppressed in a balanced modulator, and what is the practical advantage of this suppression?**

**Model Answer:**
The carrier is suppressed because the balanced modulator uses a **square-wave switching function** derived from the carrier, and the Fourier series of this square wave produces equal-magnitude positive and negative half-cycles. When the two matched multiplier outputs are summed, the fundamental carrier component cancels by destructive interference, while the sidebands (formed by multiplication of the message with each harmonic) reinforce. The **practical advantage** is twofold: (i) **Power efficiency** improves to 100% because all transmitted power resides in the information-bearing sidebands, and (ii) **Bandwidth efficiency** improves because the carrier occupies no spectrum space, enabling tighter channel spacing in multi-user systems such as satellite transponders and SSB voice communication.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Regulations:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

#### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 1]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a balanced modulator used for generating a DSB-SC signal. Derive the mathematical expression for the output when the modulating signal is a single tone.**

**Sub-part (a) — Block Diagram and Working Principle (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A balanced modulator consists of two identical multipliers and a summing junction. The modulating signal $m(t)$ is fed to **both** multipliers (in phase), while the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is fed to one multiplier **directly** and to the other multiplier through a **180° phase inverter** (i.e., $-c(t) = -A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$).

- **Multiplier 1 output:** $s_1(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$
- **Multiplier 2 output:** $s_2(t) = m(t) \cdot [-A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)]$

**Step 1 — Sum the two outputs**

$$s(t) = s_1(t) + s_2(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) - m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Resolve the apparent contradiction**

In a real switching modulator, the carrier is large enough to act as a hard switch. The multiplier behaves as a signum device, so the output is $m(t) \cdot \text{sign}(c(t))$. Two matched switching paths then yield:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot g(t) - m(t) \cdot g(t) \cdot \text{(one path inverted via transformer polarity)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply transformer polarity inversion on the carrier port**

The actual implementation uses a center-tapped transformer that applies the carrier in **opposite phase** at the two diode ports. The net effect is that the switching function $g(t)$ survives in one path and $-g(t)$ in the other, but $m(t)$ enters both paths in phase. The sum becomes:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot g(t) + m(t) \cdot [-g(t) + 2g(t)] \cdot \text{(net filter selection)}$$

After band-pass filtering at $f_c$:

$$s_{DSB-SC}(t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)\right]$$

**Sub-part (b) — Mathematical Derivation for Single-Tone Message (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the inputs**

Let the modulating signal be:

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

Let the carrier be:

$$c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 2 — Write the multiplier output (ideal case)**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot c(t) = A_m A_c \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product-to-sum identity**

Using $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$s(t) = \frac{A_m A_c}{2}\left[\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Identify components**

- **Lower Sideband (LSB):** Amplitude $\frac{A_m A_c}{2}$ at frequency $(f_c - f_m)$
- **Upper Sideband (USB):** Amplitude $\frac{A_m A_c}{2}$ at frequency $(f_c + f_m)$
- **Carrier component at $f_c$:** **Absent** (suppressed)

**Step 5 — Draw the amplitude spectrum**

Plot two spectral lines of equal height $\frac{A_m A_c}{2}$ at frequencies $(f_c - f_m)$ and $(f_c + f_m)$, with **no spike** at $f_c$. The bandwidth is $BW = 2 f_m$.

**Step 6 — Compute total transmitted power**

$$P_t = \frac{1}{R}\left[\left(\frac{A_m A_c}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} + \left(\frac{A_m A_c}{2}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\right] = \frac{A_m^2 A_c^2}{4R}$$

This equals the **sideband power only**, confirming the **100% efficiency** of DSB-SC.

---

#### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 1]`

**Q: Explain the operation of a switching-type balanced modulator. Derive the expression for the output signal and show that the carrier is suppressed. State two advantages of DSB-SC over conventional AM.**

**Sub-part (a) — Switching Modulator Operation and Carrier Suppression (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Describe the switching concept**

In a switching-type balanced modulator, the carrier is amplified to a level large enough to drive the active devices (diodes or transistors) into **hard saturation** and **cut-off**. The carrier therefore functions as a **two-position switch** that toggles between $+1$ and $-1$ at the carrier rate. The switching function is:

$$g(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\left[\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_c t) - \cdots\right]$$

**Step 2 — Write the modulator output**

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot g(t) = \frac{4 m(t)}{\pi}\left[\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_c t) - \cdots\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(\omega_m t)$**

$$s(t) = \frac{4 A_m}{\pi}\cos(\omega_m t)\left[\cos(\omega_c t) - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\omega_c t) + \frac{1}{5}\cos(5\omega_c t) - \cdots\right]$$

**Step 4 — Show that carrier is suppressed at the output port**

The carrier frequency $f_c$ appears in the spectrum **only through the modulation products** $f_c \pm f_m, 3f_c \pm f_m, 5f_c \pm f_m, \ldots$ — there is no unmodulated carrier line at $f_c$. When a band-pass filter centered at $f_c$ selects only the terms near $f_c$:

$$s_{\text{filtered}}(t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}\left[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)\right]$$

This is the **DSB-SC signal** with carrier absent.

**Sub-part (b) — Advantages of DSB-SC over Conventional AM (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Advantage 1: Power Efficiency**

Conventional AM efficiency is $\eta_{AM} = \frac{\mu^2/2}{1 + \mu^2/2}$, which peaks at **33.3%** for $\mu = 1$. DSB-SC efficiency is:

$$\eta_{DSB-SC} = 100\%$$

because all transmitted power is in the sidebands. The carrier consumes **no power** in DSB-SC, making it ideal for battery-powered and long-distance transmitters.

**Step 2 — State Advantage 2: Bandwidth and SNR**

Since the carrier is absent, the total average power required to transmit the same information is **halved**, yielding a **3 dB SNR improvement** at the coherent (synchronous) demodulator output. Additionally, DSB-SC enables **Single Sideband (SSB)** generation when a sideband filter is cascaded, halving the bandwidth to $W$ (from $2W$).

**Step 3 — Derive the 3 dB SNR advantage**

For conventional AM with $\mu = 1$:

$$P_t^{AM} = P_c (1 + 0.5) = 1.5 P_c$$

For DSB-SC with the same information content:

$$P_t^{DSB-SC} = 0.5 P_c$$

The power ratio is:

$$\frac{P_t^{AM}}{P_t^{DSB-SC}} = \frac{1.5}{0.5} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 10 \log_{10}(3) \approx 4.77 \text{ dB}$$

Thus DSB-SC saves approximately **4.77 dB** of transmitter power for equivalent message fidelity.

**Step 4 — Comparative summary table**

| **Parameter** | **Conventional AM** | **DSB-SC** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Carrier Power | $P_c$ | **0** |
| Maximum Efficiency | 33.3% | **100%** |
| Demodulation | Envelope detector (simple) | Synchronous (complex) |
| SNR for same $P_t$ | Reference | **+4.77 dB** |
| Bandwidth | $2W$ | $2W$ (same) → $W$ for SSB |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the product-to-sum identity.** Writing $s(t) = A_m A_c \cos(\omega_c t) \cos(\omega_m t)$ without simplification shows you have not understood the sideband decomposition. Examiners deduct **2 to 3 marks** for this.
> 2. **Confusing DSB-SC with conventional AM.** Students often write the carrier term $A_c \cos(\omega_c t)$ as part of the answer. The entire purpose of a balanced modulator is to **eliminate** this term — including it is a critical conceptual error.
> 3. **Skipping the Fourier series of the switching function.** When the question specifies "switching-type balanced modulator," the Fourier expansion of $g(t)$ is **mandatory** (4 marks out of 14). Skipping it guarantees partial credit only.
> 4. **Not labelling the diagram.** KTU examiners award up to **2 marks** for a "neat, labelled block diagram." Always mark all blocks: multiplier, summer, filter, signal names, and arrow directions.
> 5. **Mixing up LSB and USB.** Lower sideband is at $f_c - f_m$ (closer to DC), upper sideband is at $f_c + f_m$ (farther from DC). This swap is a common error.
> 6. **Forgetting units in the formula sheet and final numerical answer.** If the question asks for power, write the result in **Watts**, not just an expression.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Balanced Modulator**
> - **Definition:** A balanced modulator multiplies $m(t)$ with $c(t)$ in a symmetric topology, producing **DSB-SC** with the carrier suppressed.
> - **Output Equation:** $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — carrier term is **absent**.
> - **Single-Tone Expansion:** $s(t) = \frac{A_c A_m}{2}\left[\cos 2\pi(f_c - f_m)t + \cos 2\pi(f_c + f_m)t\right]$.
> - **Switching Function:** $g(t) = \frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{(-1)^n}{(2n+1)}\cos(2n+1)\omega_c t$.
> - **Filtered Output:** $s_{\text{filtered}}(t) = \frac{2 A_m}{\pi}[\cos((\omega_c - \omega_m)t) + \cos((\omega_c + \omega_m)t)]$.
> - **Power Efficiency:** **100%** (no power wasted on carrier).
> - **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2W$, same as conventional AM.
> - **Carrier Suppression Ratio:** 30 – 65 dB, depending on topology.
> - **Topologies:** Diode Bridge, Transistor Pair, Gilbert Cell IC (MC1496).
> - **Applications:** SSB generation, QAM modulation, synchronous demodulation, RF mixing.
> - **Key Identity to Memorize:** $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$.
> - **Block Diagram Must Include:** Two multipliers, summer, band-pass filter, signal labels $m(t)$, $c(t)$, and DSB-SC output.
> - **Demodulation Requirement:** DSB-SC **cannot** be demodulated by an envelope detector — it requires **synchronous (coherent) detection**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AM Demodulators - Envelope Detector
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** AM Demodulators - Envelope Detector # 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 📘 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard) > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Envelope Detector (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)** > An **Envelope Detector** is a simple, non-coherent, asynchronous **Amplitude Demodulator** used to recover the original baseband (modulating) signal from a conventional Amplitude Modulated (AM) wave by physically tracing (i.e., rectifying and low-pass filtering) the **envelope** of the incoming modulated carrier. Mathematically, if the input AM signal is expressed as: $$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cdot x(t) \right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$ then the ideal output of the envelope detector is: $$v_o(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cdot x(t) \right]$$ where $m \cdot x(t) \le 1$ (i.e., **modulation index $m \le 1$** is the mandatory condition, to prevent envelope reversal). The detector is composed of only **three passive components**: a **diode (D)** acting as a half-wave rectifier, a **capacitor (C)** acting as an envelope storage element, and a **resistor (R)** acting as the discharge path — together forming a **non-linear envelope follower** followed by a first-order **low-pass RC filter**. ## 🌊 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy (Plain English) > [!TIP] > **Analogy — The Boat Riding the Ocean Wave** > Imagine you are sitting in a small boat on a stormy ocean. The ocean waves represent the **high-frequency carrier** $\cos(\omega_c t)$, while the *slow rise and fall of the tide* represents the **modulating signal** $m \cdot \cos(\omega_m t)$. The boat constantly bobs up and down with every wave (carrier oscillation), but if you draw a slow line connecting only the *peaks* of the wave (i.e., the top outline of the wave), that slow outline is the **envelope**. An envelope detector is essentially an "intelligent float" that: > 1. **Rises quickly** to the peak of each carrier cycle (the diode + capacitor charging). > 2. **Falls slowly** between peaks (the capacitor discharging through R). > 3. In doing so, it **smooths out** the fast oscillations and traces out the slow tide underneath. > > The output you record is the **tide level** (the original audio/modulating signal), perfectly recovered from the choppy ocean. ## 🎯 1.3 Core Function & Why It Matters | Aspect | Detail | |---|---| | **Detector Type** | Non-coherent / Envelope / Asynchronous | | **Active Devices** | None (purely passive) | | **Requires Carrier?** | YES — must have carrier with $m \le 1$ | | **Input Signal Type** | Conventional AM (with carrier) only | | **Key Metric** | Modulation index must be **$m \le 1$** (no overmodulation) | | **Output** | Envelope = $A_c[1 + m \cdot x(t)]$ | | **DC Block Required?** | YES — coupling capacitor to remove DC $A_c$ | ## 🚨 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Boundary Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis):** > - **Diode Forward Voltage Drop $V_D \approx \mathbf{0.7~V}$** (for silicon) — introduces a small non-linearity. > - **Input Impedance $R_{in} \approx \mathbf{R}$** (approximately). > - **Time Constant Constraint:** $\dfrac{1}{f_c} \ll \mathbf{RC} \ll \dfrac{1}{f_m}$ > - **Ripple Frequency in Output:** $\mathbf{2 f_c}$ (after full-wave equivalent rectification behaviour) — but for a half-wave envelope detector, ripple is at $\mathbf{f_c}$. > - **Maximum allowable modulation index:** $\mathbf{m \le 1}$. # 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🧠 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Three Functional Stages The envelope detector operates in **three discrete temporal phases**, controlled by the diode's switching action: ### 🔹 Stage 1: Peak Detection (Charging Phase) - When the instantaneous AM signal $s(t)$ **exceeds** the voltage stored on capacitor $C$, the **diode becomes forward-biased** (ON state). - The diode behaves as a closed switch → capacitor charges very rapidly (low diode resistance $r_d$). - Time constant during charging: $\tau_{\text{charge}} = r_d \cdot C \approx \mathbf{very\ small}$. - Capacitor voltage **tracks** the rising carrier peak almost instantaneously. - Charging continues until $v_C(t) = s(t)$ at the carrier peak. ### 🔹 Stage 2: Envelope Holding (Discharge Phase) - Once the carrier peak is passed and $s(t)$ drops **below** $v_C(t)$, the diode becomes **reverse-biased** (OFF state). - The diode acts as an open switch → capacitor **discharges slowly** through resistor $R$. - Time constant during discharge: $\tau_{\text{discharge}} = R \cdot C$. - The voltage across $C$ **decays exponentially** during this interval. - This slow decay **holds** the voltage near the previous peak, ready for the next carrier cycle. ### 🔹 Stage 3: Tracking the Envelope - As the modulating signal changes, the **peaks of $s(t)$ change** accordingly. - The detector automatically adjusts: it charges to the new peak when the new peak is higher, and decays through $R$ between peaks. - The result: a fluctuating output voltage that **follows the envelope** $A_c[1 + m \cdot x(t)]$. ## 🧪 2.2 Why and How — The Engineering Reasoning | Question | Engineering Answer | |---|---| | **Why a diode?** | To achieve **half-wave rectification**, passing only the positive half of the carrier. | | **Why a capacitor?** | To **store** the peak voltage between carrier cycles — acts as a memory element. | | **Why a resistor?** | To provide a controlled **discharge path** for the capacitor (defines time constant). | | **Why RC large vs. small?** | If too small → ripples survive (no smoothing). If too large → envelope is distorted (diagonal clipping). | | **Why remove DC?** | The term $A_c$ is a useless DC offset; coupling capacitor blocks it to deliver only $m \cdot A_c \cdot x(t)$. | ## 📊 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Formula Card — KTU 2024 Board Exam** | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Condition / Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Input AM Signal | $s(t) = A_c [1 + m \cdot x(t)] \cos(\omega_c t)$ | $m \le 1$ (no overmodulation) | | 2 | Ideal Detector Output | $v_o(t) = A_c [1 + m \cdot x(t)]$ | Envelope recovery | | 3 | Output after DC Block | $v_{out}(t) = A_c \cdot m \cdot x(t)$ | Original message recovered | | 4 | Carrier Frequency Constraint | $\dfrac{1}{f_c} \ll \mathbf{RC}$ | Must smooth carrier ripple | | 5 | Modulating Frequency Constraint | $\mathbf{RC} \ll \dfrac{1}{f_m}$ | Must track envelope changes | | 6 | Diagonal Clipping Avoidance (Single-tone) | $RC \le \dfrac{\sqrt{1 - m^2}}{2\pi f_m \cdot m}$ | Boundary for $m < 1$ | | 7 | Diagonal Clipping Avoidance (General approx.) | $RC \le \dfrac{1 - m}{2\pi f_m \cdot m}$ | Conservative estimate | | 8 | Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage (Half-wave) | $V_r \approx \dfrac{V_m}{f_c \cdot R \cdot C}$ | For $RC \gg 1/f_c$ | | 9 | Ripple Frequency (output) | $f_r = f_c$ (half-wave detector) | Hz | | 10 | Charging Time Constant | $\tau_c = r_d \cdot C$ | $r_d$ = diode forward resistance (Ω) | | 11 | Discharging Time Constant | $\tau_d = R \cdot C$ | Seconds | | 12 | Detector Voltage Gain | $K_d = \cos(\theta)$ | where $\theta = \tan^{-1}(2\pi f_c R C)$ | | 13 | Modulation Index Definition | $m = \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le m \le 1$ | > [!WARNING] > **Markdown Table Safety Note:** In the table above, all vertical-bar operations (e.g., absolute value, $\vert x \vert$) have been deliberately avoided. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX within your answer sheets to prevent markdown table breaking — this is a common KTU answer-script formatting trap. ## 🌍 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Where Envelope Detectors Are Used in Practice:** > - **AM Radio Receivers (MW/SW band, 540 kHz – 30 MHz):** The first stage of demodulation in every cheap pocket AM radio uses an envelope detector after the IF stage (455 kHz). > - **Low-Cost Wireless Sensor Nodes:** Used in IoT devices where power and complexity must be minimized. > - **Optical Communication (Simple Intensity Modulation):** Photodiodes with RC load act as natural envelope detectors. > - **RF Identification (RFID) Tags:** Passive RFID tags use envelope detection for backscatter communication. > - **AC Voltmeters & Signal Strength Meters:** The classic analog "peak-reading" voltmeter is an envelope detector. The **commercial advantage** is that the envelope detector requires **no local oscillator, no phase-locked loop, and no synchronous reference** — making it the **cheapest, smallest, and most power-efficient** demodulator for AM applications. # 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧮 3.1 Derivation 1: The Charging and Discharging Equations ### **Step 1 — Define the Input AM Signal** Consider a single-tone modulated AM signal: $$s(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t) \right] \cos(\omega_c t)$$ This can be visualized as a high-frequency carrier $\cos(\omega_c t)$ whose **envelope** $A_c[1 + m \cos(\omega_m t)]$ varies slowly. ### **Step 2 — Envelope Equation (Mathematical Definition)** By definition, the envelope of any bandpass signal $s(t) = a(t) \cos(\omega_c t)$ is given by $\vert a(t) \vert$. For our AM signal: $$E(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t) \right] \quad \text{(for } m \le 1\text{)}$$ The goal of the envelope detector is to reproduce $E(t)$ at its output. ### **Step 3 — Charging Phase Analysis (Diode ON)** During the positive half-cycle of the carrier, when $s(t) > v_C(t)$, the diode conducts. The capacitor charges through the diode's small forward resistance $r_d$. Applying KVL across the loop: $$A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t) \right] \cos(\omega_c t) = i(t) \cdot r_d + v_C(t)$$ Since $r_d$ is very small, the capacitor **charges almost instantaneously** to the peak value: $$v_C(t)\bigg|_{\text{peak}} = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t_p) \right]$$ where $t_p$ is the time instant of the carrier peak. ### **Step 4 — Discharging Phase Analysis (Diode OFF)** Once $s(t)$ falls below $v_C(t)$, the diode becomes reverse-biased and acts as an open circuit. The capacitor now discharges only through $R$. The discharge equation is governed by the standard RC circuit: $$v_C(t) = V_0 \cdot e^{-t/(RC)}$$ where $V_0$ is the initial voltage on the capacitor at the start of discharge. ### **Step 5 — Quasi-Steady State Output** Combining charging and discharging actions, the output voltage is approximately: $$v_o(t) \approx A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t) \right]$$ minus a small ripple at the carrier frequency $f_c$, which is filtered out by the RC low-pass behaviour. --- ## 🧮 3.2 Derivation 2: Diagonal Clipping Condition (The Most Critical Derivation) Diagonal clipping occurs when the capacitor cannot discharge fast enough to follow the **falling envelope**. We derive the exact mathematical condition to prevent it. ### **Step 1 — Set Up the Worst-Case Scenario** Consider the envelope at its **minimum slope** (where clipping is most likely): $$E(t) = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t) \right]$$ The slope of the envelope is: $$\frac{dE(t)}{dt} = -A_c \cdot m \cdot \omega_m \sin(\omega_m t)$$ The **maximum negative slope** occurs when $\sin(\omega_m t) = +1$ (i.e., near the falling edge of the envelope): $$\left| \frac{dE}{dt} \right|_{\max} = A_c \cdot m \cdot \omega_m$$ ### **Step 2 — Slope of the Capacitor Discharge** During discharge, the capacitor voltage decays as: $$v_C(t) = V_{\text{peak}} \cdot e^{-t/(RC)}$$ The initial slope (at $t = 0$, just after the peak) is: $$\frac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{t=0} = -\frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{RC}$$ ### **Step 3 — Match the Slopes (Tracking Condition)** For the detector to **track** the envelope faithfully, the capacitor's discharge slope must be **at least as steep** as the envelope's slope: $$\frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{RC} \ge A_c \cdot m \cdot \omega_m$$ At the worst case, $V_{\text{peak}} \approx A_c(1 - m)$ (envelope minimum): $$\frac{A_c(1 - m)}{RC} \ge A_c \cdot m \cdot \omega_m$$ ### **Step 4 — Solve for the RC Constraint** Cancelling $A_c$ from both sides and rearranging: $$\frac{1 - m}{RC} \ge m \cdot \omega_m$$ $$\boxed{RC \le \frac{1 - m}{m \cdot \omega_m}}$$ ### **Step 5 — Final Standard KTU Form** Substituting $\omega_m = 2\pi f_m$: $$\boxed{RC \le \frac{1 - m}{2\pi f_m \cdot m}}$$ This is the **diagonal clipping avoidance condition** — a must-memorize formula for KTU exams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** > - If $m$ is **small** (weak modulation), the constraint is relaxed (larger RC is allowed). > - If $m$ is **close to 1** (deep modulation), the constraint is very tight (small RC required). > - If $RC$ violates this → envelope is **clipped diagonally** during the steep negative-slope region of the envelope. --- ## 🧮 3.3 Derivation 3: Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage ### **Step 1 — Charging Instant** The capacitor charges to the peak of the input signal at every carrier cycle: $$V_{\text{peak}} = A_c \left[ 1 + m \cos(\omega_m t) \right] \approx A_c$$ ### **Step 2 — Discharge Interval** Between two consecutive carrier peaks, the capacitor discharges for a duration $\Delta t \approx \dfrac{1}{f_c}$ (one carrier period). ### **Step 3 — Exponential Decay Approximation** For small discharge (since $RC \gg 1/f_c$): $$v_C(t) \approx V_{\text{peak}} \cdot e^{-t/(RC)} \approx V_{\text{peak}} \left( 1 - \frac{t}{RC} \right)$$ ### **Step 4 — Ripple Magnitude** At $t = 1/f_c$, the voltage drop is: $$\Delta V = V_{\text{peak}} - v_C(1/f_c) \approx \frac{V_{\text{peak}}}{f_c \cdot RC}$$ $$\boxed{V_r \approx \frac{V_m}{f_c \cdot R \cdot C}}$$ where $V_m = A_c$ is the peak carrier amplitude. ### **Step 5 — Percentage Ripple** $$\% \text{ ripple} = \frac{V_r}{V_{\text{DC}}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{f_c \cdot RC} \times 100\%$$ For a typical AM broadcast ($f_c = 455$ kHz, $RC = 0.1$ μs gives ripple $\approx 2.2\%$, acceptable). --- ## 🧮 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Distortion Summary | Distortion Type | Cause | Condition to Avoid | Visual Symptom | |---|---|---|---| | **Diagonal Clipping** | $RC$ too large, envelope falls faster than capacitor discharge | $RC \le \dfrac{1-m}{2\pi f_m m}$ | Envelope has diagonal "chunks" cut out | | **Excess Ripple** | $RC$ too small, capacitor discharges too quickly | $RC \gg \dfrac{1}{f_c}$ | Output has high-frequency carrier visible | | **Negative Peak Clipping** | DC-blocking capacitor too large; envelope touches 0 | $C_b \le \dfrac{C \cdot (1-m)}{m}$ | Bottom of envelope gets cut flat | | **Diode Non-linearity** | Forward voltage drop $V_D \approx 0.7$ V distorts low-level signals | Use Schottky diode or pre-amplify | Non-linear compression at low amplitudes | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** > Marks are awarded for **deriving the diagonal clipping condition from first principles**, not merely stating it. Always start with the slope equation $dE/dt$ and equate it to the discharge slope. This 2-mark step in part-(a) questions is frequently missed by students. # 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🗺️ 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Envelope Detector Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[INPUT AM SIGNAL

original baseband signal] nodeH[Design Rule] -.-> nodeC nodeI[Avoid Diagonal Clipping] -.-> nodeD nodeJ[Ripple Filter] -.-> nodeE nodeH[Design Rule: RC must be greater than 1 by fc and less than 1 by fm] -.-> nodeC nodeI[Constraint: RC less than or equal to 1 minus m divided by 2 pi fm m] -.-> nodeD nodeJ[Ripple Frequency equals fc for half wave detector] -.-> nodeE ``` ## 🗺️ 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Charging and Discharging Cycle Timeline ```mermaid flowchart TD timeA[t equals 0 Carrier Peak] --> diode1[Diode D is ON Forward Biased] diode1 --> charge1[Capacitor C charges rapidly
through small diode resistance rd] charge1 --> peak1[vc of t equals Ac 1 plus m cosine omega m t peak] peak1 --> timeB[Time elapses one carrier period] timeB --> diode2[Diode D is OFF Reverse Biased] diode2 --> discharge1[Capacitor C discharges slowly
through resistor R only] discharge1 --> decay1[vc of t equals V0 exp of minus t by RC] decay1 --> check1{Envelope slope steepness check} check1 -- slope gentle --> track[Output tracks envelope faithfully] check1 -- slope steep and RC too large --> clip[Diagonal Clipping occurs
envelope is distorted] track --> nextPeak[Next carrier peak arrives] clip --> nextPeak nextPeak --> diode1 ``` ## 🗺️ 4.3 Mermaid Architecture — Decision Flow for RC Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD startQ[Given Ac, fc, fm, and modulation index m] --> pickR[Step 1 Pick R and C such that

No clipping, low ripple] checkClip -- NO condition violated --> fix1[Reduce RC by lowering R or C] fix1 --> pickR valid --> output[Final RC pair selected

In your KTU answer sheets, you should **draw the actual circuit** (diode in series, then $R \parallel C$ to ground) using standard symbols. The diagrams here help you **understand the temporal behavior** of charging/discharging. ## 📐 4.4 ASCII Circuit Schematic (For Quick Visual Reference) ``` R D ___ ─►├──────┤ ├──────┬────── Vout (envelope) │ ___ │ │ C === Cb │ │ │ │ │ │ ─┴─────────┴────────┴───── GND (Envelope Detector with DC-Blocking Capacitor Cb) ``` **Component Roles:** - **D** = Diode (rectification) - **C** = Envelope storage capacitor - **R** = Discharge resistor - **Cb** = DC-blocking coupling capacitor (removes the DC component $A_c$) # 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]` **Q: With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of an envelope detector used for AM demodulation.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** An envelope detector consists of a **diode D**, a **capacitor C**, and a **resistor R**, with the diode in series and the $R \parallel C$ combination to ground. **Working Principle:** 1. The incoming AM signal $s(t) = A_c[1 + m \cos(\omega_m t)]\cos(\omega_c t)$ is applied across the diode. 2. During the **positive half-cycle of the carrier**, the diode is forward-biased and conducts, allowing the capacitor to charge rapidly to the peak value of the carrier. 3. During the **negative half-cycle**, the diode is reverse-biased and acts as an open switch. The capacitor then discharges slowly through $R$ with time constant $RC$. 4. This **charge-discharge cycle repeats** for every carrier cycle, with the output voltage approximately tracing the **envelope** $A_c[1 + m \cos(\omega_m t)]$. 5. A DC-blocking coupling capacitor removes the unwanted $A_c$ DC component, delivering $A_c m \cos(\omega_m t)$ at the output — the original modulating signal. **Key Constraint:** $\dfrac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \dfrac{1}{f_m}$ --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]` **Q: Define "diagonal clipping" in envelope detection. State the condition to avoid it.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition:** Diagonal clipping is a form of **envelope distortion** that occurs when the discharge time constant $RC$ is too large, causing the capacitor voltage to **fall more slowly** than the rapidly decreasing envelope of the AM signal. As a result, the output voltage cannot track the envelope during its steep negative-slope regions, and "diagonal cuts" appear in the recovered waveform. **Condition to Avoid Diagonal Clipping:** For a single-tone AM signal with modulation index $m$ and modulating frequency $f_m$: $$RC \le \frac{1 - m}{2\pi f_m \cdot m}$$ If $RC$ exceeds this bound, the detector will fail to follow the envelope near its minimum point, and diagonal clipping will occur. To prevent it, $R$ or $C$ must be reduced until the inequality is satisfied. --- ## 📝 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question B1A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Expected]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q: Draw the circuit of an envelope detector. With the help of relevant waveforms, explain the charging and discharging operation in detail. Derive the condition to avoid diagonal clipping.** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Diagram:** (Refer to ASCII circuit in Section 4.4) **Waveforms (to be drawn):** - Input AM wave $s(t)$ — high-frequency carrier with slow envelope. - Diode current $i_D(t)$ — pulses at each carrier peak. - Capacitor voltage $v_C(t)$ — exponential rise to peak, then exponential fall (sawtooth-like ripple). - Output envelope — slow variation matching $A_c[1 + m \cos(\omega_m t)]$. **Charging Operation:** When the diode is forward-biased, capacitor charges through small forward resistance $r_d$ with time constant $\tau_c = r_d C \ll 1/f_c$. The voltage rises almost instantaneously to the peak value of the carrier. **Discharging Operation:** When the diode is reverse-biased, the capacitor discharges through $R$ with time constant $\tau_d = RC \gg 1/f_c$. The voltage decays exponentially: $$v_C(t) = V_{\text{peak}} \cdot e^{-t/(RC)}$$ **Derivation of Diagonal Clipping Condition:** (See Section 3.2, Derivation 2) - Envelope slope: $\left| dE/dt \right|_{\max} = A_c m \omega_m$ - Capacitor discharge slope: $V_{\text{peak}} / (RC) = A_c(1-m)/(RC)$ - Equating: $RC \le \dfrac{1-m}{2\pi f_m m}$ --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: An AM broadcast receiver uses an envelope detector to demodulate an IF signal of 455 kHz. The modulating signal has a maximum frequency of 5 kHz, and the modulation index is 0.8. Design suitable values of $R$ and $C$ for the detector such that the ripple is less than 2% and no diagonal clipping occurs.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $f_c = 455$ kHz - $f_m = 5$ kHz - $m = 0.8$ **Step 1 — Diagonal Clipping Constraint:** $$RC \le \frac{1 - m}{2\pi f_m \cdot m} = \frac{1 - 0.8}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 0.8}$$ $$RC \le \frac{0.2}{25132.7} \approx 7.96 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 7.96 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 2 — Ripple Constraint:** For ripple $< 2\%$, we need: $$\frac{1}{f_c \cdot RC} \le 0.02$$ $$RC \ge \frac{1}{0.02 \times 455 \times 10^3} = \frac{1}{9100} \approx 1.10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 110 \text{ μs}$$ **Step 3 — Conflict Analysis:** The two constraints are **incompatible**: - Clipping requires: $RC \le 7.96$ μs - Ripple requires: $RC \ge 110$ μs **Step 4 — Resolution:** Since diagonal clipping causes **severe distortion** while ripple causes only minor noise, prioritize the **diagonal clipping constraint** to avoid distortion. The receiver will have slightly more ripple, which can be reduced using an **additional RC filter stage** at the audio output. **Selected Design:** $RC = 7.5$ μs (satisfies diagonal clipping). Choose $C = 1$ nF, then $R = 7.5$ kΩ. **Verification of Ripple:** $$\% \text{ ripple} = \frac{1}{f_c \cdot RC} = \frac{1}{455 \times 10^3 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 29.3\%$$ This ripple is high, so add a **second-stage RC low-pass filter** with cutoff $\approx 10$ kHz to clean up the audio. --- ### **Question B1B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Alternative]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Q: Explain the operation of an envelope detector with the help of a neat block diagram. What is the role of the time constant $RC$ in determining the fidelity of detection?** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flowchart) **Operation:** The envelope detector operates in three stages: 1. **Rectification:** The diode passes only the positive half of the carrier, converting the bipolar AM signal into a unipolar (pulsating DC) signal. 2. **Peak Storage:** The capacitor holds the peak value during the short interval when the diode is reverse-biased. 3. **Smoothing:** The resistor $R$ provides a controlled discharge path, ensuring the output voltage decays slowly between carrier peaks. **Role of Time Constant $RC$:** The fidelity of detection depends critically on the proper choice of $RC$: - **$RC$ too small:** The capacitor discharges too quickly, leaving residual carrier ripple in the output. The output will appear "spiky" with significant high-frequency noise. - **$RC$ too large:** The capacitor cannot discharge fast enough to follow the rapidly falling envelope near its minimum. This results in **diagonal clipping** — a form of amplitude distortion. - **$RC$ optimal:** The output faithfully follows the envelope with minimal ripple and no clipping. The design rule is: $$\frac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \frac{1}{f_m}$$ This ensures the detector can smooth the carrier while still tracking the modulating signal. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Q: Discuss the various sources of distortion in an envelope detector. How does negative peak clipping differ from diagonal clipping?** **Model Solution:** **Sources of Distortion in Envelope Detectors:** | Distortion Type | Description | Cause | Solution | |---|---|---|---| | **Diagonal Clipping** | Envelope "chunks" cut out during steep negative-slope regions | $RC$ too large | Reduce $RC$ | | **Excess Ripple** | High-frequency carrier visible in output | $RC$ too small | Increase $RC$ or add post-filter | | **Negative Peak Clipping** | Bottom of envelope flattened near zero | DC-blocking cap $C_b$ too large | Reduce $C_b$ | | **Diode Non-linearity** | Output compressed at low signal levels | $V_D \approx 0.7$ V drop | Use Schottky diode or pre-amp | **Diagonal Clipping vs. Negative Peak Clipping:** | Feature | Diagonal Clipping | Negative Peak Clipping | |---|---|---| | **Location** | Occurs during steep negative slope of envelope | Occurs at the bottom of the envelope (near zero) | | **Cause** | $RC$ time constant too large | DC-blocking capacitor $C_b$ too large | | **Mechanism** | Capacitor cannot discharge fast enough | AC current through $C_b$ creates DC shift that pulls envelope below zero | | **Condition to avoid** | $RC \le \dfrac{1-m}{2\pi f_m m}$ | $C_b \le \dfrac{C(1-m)}{m}$ | | **Visual** | "Diagonal" cuts in falling part of envelope | Flat bottom of envelope at zero level | **Design Strategy:** Both types of distortion must be simultaneously avoided by carefully selecting $R$, $C$, and $C_b$ based on the operating frequencies and modulation index. In practice, designers add a **buffer amplifier** between the detector and the load to prevent the load from affecting the discharge time constant. --- ## ⚠️ 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Envelope Detector Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to mention $m \le 1$:** Many students lose 1 mark by not stating that envelope detection requires the modulation index to be $\le 1$. Without this, the envelope crosses zero, and detection fails catastrophically. > > 2. **Confusing ripple frequency:** In a **half-wave** envelope detector, ripple is at $f_c$ (not $2f_c$). In a **full-wave** equivalent detector (or after the DC-block), it remains at $f_c$. Students often wrongly write $2f_c$ — losing a mark. > > 3. **Missing the diode drop in low-amplitude calculations:** If the carrier amplitude is small (e.g., $A_c < 5$ V), the diode's $0.7$ V drop becomes significant, and the output is non-linear. For 7-mark design problems, this must be mentioned. > > 4. **Stating diagonal clipping condition without derivation:** Simply writing $RC \le \dfrac{1-m}{2\pi f_m m}$ without showing the slope-equality derivation loses 2-3 marks. Always derive it from $dE/dt$ vs. capacitor discharge rate. > > 5. **Ignoring the DC-blocking capacitor:** In the 14-mark design problems, students often forget that the final output has a coupling capacitor $C_b$. If $C_b$ is too large, negative peak clipping occurs — this is a separate distortion that must be addressed. > > 6. **Numerical errors in $RC$ calculation:** Forgetting to convert kHz to Hz in $f_c$, or mixing $f_m$ and $f_c$ in the diagonal clipping formula. Always use SI units consistently. --- ## 🧾 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist — Envelope Detector** ### ✅ Core Concepts - ☐ Envelope detector is a **non-coherent, passive AM demodulator** using a diode + capacitor + resistor. - ☐ It traces the **envelope** of conventional AM, producing $v_o(t) = A_c[1 + m \cdot x(t)]$. - ☐ **Three stages:** Rectification → Peak Storage → Slow Discharge. - ☐ Requires modulation index **$m \le 1$** (no overmodulation). ### ✅ Key Formulas to Memorize - ☐ Ideal output: $v_o(t) = A_c[1 + m \cos(\omega_m t)]$ - ☐ Design constraint: $\dfrac{1}{f_c} \ll RC \ll \dfrac{1}{f_m}$ - ☐ Diagonal clipping condition: $RC \le \dfrac{1 - m}{2\pi f_m \cdot m}$ - ☐ Ripple voltage: $V_r \approx \dfrac{V_m}{f_c \cdot R \cdot C}$ - ☐ Ripple percentage: $\% V_r = \dfrac{1}{f_c \cdot R \cdot C} \times 100\%$ ### ✅ Distortions to Remember - ☐ **Diagonal Clipping** → caused by $RC$ too large. - ☐ **Excess Ripple** → caused by $RC$ too small. - ☐ **Negative Peak Clipping** → caused by $C_b$ too large. - ☐ **Diode Non-linearity** → caused by small signal amplitude. ### ✅ Practical Design Steps 1. Compute upper bound on $RC$ from diagonal clipping formula. 2. Compute lower bound on $RC$ from ripple percentage requirement. 3. If bounds conflict, **prioritize diagonal clipping** (more severe distortion). 4. Add a **second-stage low-pass filter** if ripple is high. 5. Choose $C_b$ such that $C_b \le C(1-m)/m$ to avoid negative peak clipping. 6. Use a **buffer amplifier** at the output to isolate the load. ### ✅ One-Sentence Summary > *"The envelope detector is the simplest AM demodulator — a diode-capacitor-resistor circuit that charges quickly to each carrier peak and discharges slowly between peaks, thereby tracing the envelope of the AM signal and recovering the original modulating waveform, provided $m \le 1$ and the time constant $RC$ is carefully chosen to avoid diagonal clipping and excess ripple."* --- **📚 End of Notes — Envelope Detector, Module 1, PEAET411 Communication Engineering, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: SNR and Figure of Merit for Different Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SNR and Figure of Merit for Different Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the ratio of signal power to noise power at any point in a communication system, expressed as $SNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ or in decibels, $SNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_s}{P_n}\right)$.

In the context of modulation schemes, two distinct SNR quantities are defined by the KTU syllabus:

- **Input SNR (Channel SNR / Carrier SNR)**: $SNR_c = \dfrac{\langle s_i^2(t) \rangle}{\langle n_i^2(t) \rangle}$ — the ratio of average signal power to average noise power at the **input of the demodulator** (i.e., at the receiver input after the channel).
- **Output SNR (Baseband SNR / Demodulated SNR)**: $SNR_o = \dfrac{\langle s_o^2(t) \rangle}{\langle n_o^2(t) \rangle}$ — the ratio of recovered signal power to recovered noise power at the **output of the demodulator**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Figure of Merit (FOM)** — formally defined as the ratio of output SNR to input SNR:
> $$F = \dfrac{SNR_o}{SNR_c}$$
> It is a **dimensionless number** that benchmarks the noise performance of a demodulation scheme. A higher Figure of Merit indicates a more noise-resilient modulation/demodulation technique.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are listening to a lecturer in a noisy classroom:
- The **lecturer's voice** is your **signal**.
- The **background chatter** is your **noise**.
- **SNR** is essentially asking: "How loud is the lecturer's voice compared to the chatter?"
- **Input SNR**: How clear was the voice **when it reached your ear** (after traveling through air — the channel).
- **Output SNR**: How clear is the voice in your **brain's perception** (after your auditory system "demodulates" the acoustic wave).
- **Figure of Merit**: It is the **amplification factor** that your ear-brain system provides in separating voice from noise. A scheme with high FOM is like having bionic ears; low FOM is like a tin can telephone that adds its own hiss.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Noise Power Spectral Density | $N_0/2$ (two-sided) | **Watts/Hz** |
| Channel Bandwidth | $W$ | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| Average Transmitted Power | $S_T$ or $P_T$ | **Watts (W)** |
| Modulation Index | $m$ (AM) / $\beta_f$ (FM) | **Dimensionless** |
| Boltzmann Constant | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | **J/K** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption used throughout**: The channel noise is modeled as **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$ (Watts/Hz). The channel bandwidth is $W$ Hz, so total input noise power is $N_i = N_0 W$ (assuming matched filtering).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Figure of Merit Matters in KTU Examinations

The **Figure of Merit** is a **normalized measure** because it removes the dependence on absolute signal/noise powers and isolates the **intrinsic noise performance** of the modulation-demodulation combination. This is the **single most important metric** for comparing analog communication schemes in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework for Solving Any SNR Problem

1. **Write the transmitted signal** $s_i(t)$ in standard form.
2. **Compute the average input signal power** $S_i = \langle s_i^2(t) \rangle$.
3. **Identify the channel noise** $n_i(t)$ — typically AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$.
4. **Compute input noise power** $N_i = N_0 W$ over channel bandwidth $W$.
5. **Determine received signal** $r(t) = s_i(t) + n_i(t)$.
6. **Apply the demodulation operation** to $r(t)$ to get $s_o(t)$ and $n_o(t)$.
7. **Compute average output signal power** $S_o = \langle s_o^2(t) \rangle$.
8. **Compute average output noise power** $N_o = \langle n_o^2(t) \rangle$.
9. **Form $SNR_o = S_o / N_o$** and **$F = SNR_o / SNR_c$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal channel noise model**: $N_i = N_0 W$ (Watts), $SNR_c = S_i / N_i$.

| # | Modulation Scheme | Demodulator Type | $SNR_o$ Expression | Figure of Merit $F$ | Best $F$ Value |
|---|-------------------|------------------|---------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **DSB-SC** (Double Sideband Suppressed Carrier) | Coherent (Synchronous) | $SNR_o = SNR_c$ | $F = 1$ | $1$ |
| 2 | **SSB-SC** (Single Sideband Suppressed Carrier) | Coherent | $SNR_o = SNR_c$ | $F = 1$ | $1$ |
| 3 | **VSB** (Vestigial Sideband) | Coherent | $SNR_o \approx SNR_c$ | $F \approx 1$ | $1$ |
| 4 | **Conventional AM (DSB-C, AM with carrier)** | Envelope Detection | $\dfrac{m^2}{1+m^2}SNR_c$ (for $m \leq 1$) | $F = \dfrac{m^2}{1+m^2}$ | $\leq \dfrac{1}{2}$ |
| 5 | **Conventional AM (DSB-C, AM with carrier)** | Coherent Detection | $\dfrac{m^2}{2+2m^2}SNR_c$ | $F = \dfrac{m^2}{2+2m^2}$ | $\leq \dfrac{1}{4}$ |
| 6 | **FM** (Frequency Modulation) | Frequency Discriminator | $\dfrac{3}{2}\beta_f^2 \cdot SNR_c$ | $F = \dfrac{3}{2}\beta_f^2$ | Grows with $\beta_f$ |
| 7 | **PM** (Phase Modulation) | Phase Discriminator | $\dfrac{\beta_p^2}{2} \cdot SNR_c$ | $F = \dfrac{\beta_p^2}{2}$ | Grows with $\beta_p$ |

> **Critical Constants to Memorize**:
> - SSB channel bandwidth: $W_{SSB} = W_m$ (where $W_m$ is message bandwidth)
> - DSB channel bandwidth: $W_{DSB} = 2W_m$
> - FM channel bandwidth (Carson's rule): $W_{FM} = 2(\beta_f + 1)W_m = 2(\Delta f + W_m)$
> - PM channel bandwidth (Carson's rule): $W_{PM} = 2(\beta_p + 1)W_m$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

| Application | Preferred Scheme | Reason |
|-------------|-----------------||--------|
| TV Video Broadcast | **VSB** | Bandwidth efficiency + carrier synchronization |
| Two-Way Radio (AM) | **DSB-C + Envelope** | Simple cheap receiver |
| Long-distance Voice (HF) | **SSB-SC** | Power + bandwidth efficient |
| High-Fidelity Radio (FM) | **Wideband FM** | Trade bandwidth for noise immunity |
| Space Telemetry | **FM / PM** | Maximum SNR for given power |

## 2.5 Comparative Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Heuristic**: When asked "Which scheme is best?", the answer is **system-design-dependent**:
> - If **power efficiency** is paramount → **SSB / DSB-SC** (best $F = 1$).
> - If **noise immunity at the cost of bandwidth** is acceptable → **WBFM** (highest F when $\beta_f \gg 1$).
> - If **receiver simplicity** is critical → **AM with envelope detector** (accepts $F \leq 1/2$).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive and complete** — every algebraic step is shown, with explanations for every transition. This is the exact format expected by KTU board examiners for **14-mark derivations**.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: DSB-SC with Coherent Detection

**Setup**: Message $m(t)$ with average power $P_m = \langle m^2(t) \rangle$, bandwidth $W_m$.

**Transmitted signal**:
$$s_i(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Input signal power** (over message bandwidth):
$$S_i = \langle s_i^2(t) \rangle = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}\langle m^2(t) \rangle = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{2}$$

**Received signal**: $r(t) = s_i(t) + n_i(t)$, where $n_i(t)$ is band-limited AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$ and bandwidth $W = 2W_m$.
$$N_i = N_0 W = 2 N_0 W_m$$

**Coherent demodulation** multiplies $r(t)$ by $2\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ then passes through LPF of bandwidth $W_m$:
$$r(t) \cdot 2\cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c m(t) + A_c m(t)\cos(4\pi f_c t) + n_i(t) \cdot 2\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

After LPF:
$$s_o(t) + n_o(t) = A_c m(t) + n_c(t)$$

where $n_c(t)$ is the in-phase noise component with PSD $\Phi_{n_c}(f) = N_0$ for $\vert f \vert \leq W_m$ (two-sided).

**Output signal power**:
$$S_o = \langle [A_c m(t)]^2 \rangle = A_c^2 P_m$$

**Output noise power** (integrating PSD over $-W_m$ to $+W_m$):
$$N_o = \int_{-W_m}^{W_m} N_0 \, df = 2 N_0 W_m$$

**Output SNR**:
$$SNR_o = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{2 N_0 W_m}$$

**Input SNR**:
$$SNR_c = \dfrac{S_i}{N_i} = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m / 2}{2 N_0 W_m} = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{4 N_0 W_m}$$

**Figure of Merit**:
$$F = \dfrac{SNR_o}{SNR_c} = \dfrac{\dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{2 N_0 W_m}}{\dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{4 N_0 W_m}} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} = 1$$

$$\boxed{F_{DSB\text{-}SC} = 1}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: SSB-SC with Coherent Detection

**Transmitted signal** (upper sideband, USB):
$$s_i(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \dfrac{A_c}{2}\hat{m}(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $\hat{m}(t)$ is the Hilbert transform of $m(t)$.

**Input signal power**:
$$S_i = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}P_m$$

**Channel noise** in SSB band has bandwidth $W = W_m$:
$$N_i = N_0 W_m$$

**Coherent demodulation** multiplies $r(t)$ by $2\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and passes through LPF of bandwidth $W_m$:

After algebraic manipulation (using the Hilbert orthogonality principle), the output is:
$$y(t) = \dfrac{A_c}{2} m(t) + n_c(t)$$

where the in-phase noise $n_c(t)$ has the **same two-sided PSD $N_0$** over $[-W_m, W_m]$ (the quadrature noise $n_s(t)$ is rejected because SSB transmits only USB).

**Output signal power**:
$$S_o = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4} P_m$$

**Output noise power**:
$$N_o = 2 N_0 W_m$$

**Output SNR**:
$$SNR_o = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m / 4}{2 N_0 W_m} = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{8 N_0 W_m}$$

**Input SNR**:
$$SNR_c = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m / 4}{N_0 W_m} = \dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{4 N_0 W_m}$$

**Figure of Merit**:
$$F = \dfrac{SNR_o}{SNR_c} = \dfrac{\dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{8 N_0 W_m}}{\dfrac{A_c^2 P_m}{4 N_0 W_m}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{F_{SSB\text{-}SC} = 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: Although the SNR expression is $SNR_o = SNR_c$ for SSB, the noise PSD at output is **double** that of DSB-SC because DSB uses $2W_m$ bandwidth. The two effects cancel exactly, yielding $F = 1$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Conventional AM (DSB-C) with Envelope Detection

**Transmitted signal**:
$$s_i(t) = A_c[1 + m_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $m_a$ is the modulation index and $\vert m(t) \vert \leq 1$ so that $m_a \leq 1$ for no over-modulation.

**Input signal power** (assuming $m(t)$ has zero DC):
$$S_i = \langle s_i^2(t) \rangle = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}[1 + m_a^2 \langle m^2(t) \rangle] = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}(1 + m_a^2 P_m)$$

**Input noise power** (bandwidth $W = 2W_m$):
$$N_i = 2 N_0 W_m$$

**Envelope detector output** at high SNR (signal dominates noise):
$$v(t) \approx A_c[1 + m_a m(t)] + n_c(t)$$

A DC blocker removes the $A_c$ offset, giving:
$$s_o(t) = A_c m_a m(t), \quad n_o(t) = n_c(t)$$

**Output signal power**:
$$S_o = A_c^2 m_a^2 P_m$$

**Output noise power** (same as DSB-SC coherent case):
$$N_o = 2 N_0 W_m$$

**Output SNR**:
$$SNR_o = \dfrac{A_c^2 m_a^2 P_m}{2 N_0 W_m}$$

**Input SNR**:
$$SNR_c = \dfrac{\dfrac{A_c^2}{2}(1 + m_a^2 P_m)}{2 N_0 W_m} = \dfrac{A_c^2(1 + m_a^2 P_m)}{4 N_0 W_m}$$

**Figure of Merit**:
$$F = \dfrac{SNR_o}{SNR_c} = \dfrac{A_c^2 m_a^2 P_m / (2 N_0 W_m)}{A_c^2(1 + m_a^2 P_m)/(4 N_0 W_m)}$$

$$F = \dfrac{2 m_a^2 P_m}{1 + m_a^2 P_m}$$

**Special case** (sinusoidal test tone $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ with $P_m = A_m^2 / 2$ and $m_a = A_m$):
$$F = \dfrac{2 m_a^2 \cdot \frac{A_m^2}{2}}{1 + m_a^2 \cdot \frac{A_m^2}{2}} = \dfrac{m_a^2 \cdot A_m^2}{1 + \frac{m_a^2 A_m^2}{2}}$$

With $m_a = A_m$ for 100% modulation:
$$F = \dfrac{A_m^2 \cdot A_m^2}{1 + \frac{A_m^4}{2}} = \dfrac{A_m^4}{1 + \frac{A_m^4}{2}}$$

For a pure unit-amplitude sinusoid test (where $m(t) = \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $A_m = 1$, $P_m = 1/2$, $m_a \leq 1$):
$$F = \dfrac{m_a^2}{1 + m_a^2}$$

Maximum at $m_a = 1$ (full modulation):
$$\boxed{F_{AM,\text{env}} = \dfrac{m_a^2}{1 + m_a^2} \leq \dfrac{1}{2}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Threshold Effect**: At low input SNR (below threshold), the envelope detector output becomes $E(t) = \sqrt{(A_c + n_c)^2 + n_s^2}$, which **no longer linearly recovers the message**. This is the **threshold effect** — AM envelope detection fails catastrophically below threshold SNR.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wideband FM with Frequency Discriminator

**Transmitted signal**:
$$s_i(t) = A_c \cos\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau) d\tau\right]$$

**Modulation index**: $\beta_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{W_m} = \dfrac{k_f \max|m(t)|}{W_m}$

**Input signal power** (constant envelope):
$$S_i = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$$

**Channel noise power** in FM bandwidth (Carson's rule): $W_{FM} = 2(\beta_f + 1)W_m$
$$N_i = N_0 W_{FM} = 2 N_0 (\beta_f + 1)W_m$$

**Frequency discriminator operation**: The output is proportional to the instantaneous frequency deviation:
$$s_o(t) + n_o(t) = k_d \cdot \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\dfrac{d\phi_{total}(t)}{dt}$$

where $\phi_{total}(t)$ is the phase of $r(t) = s_i(t) + n_i(t)$.

For high input SNR, the **output noise PSD** is **quadratic** in frequency (the discriminator acts as a differentiator):
$$\Phi_{n_o}(f) = \left(\dfrac{k_d}{2\pi}\right)^2 (2\pi f)^2 \cdot N_0 = k_d^2 f^2 N_0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq W_m$$

**Output noise power** (integrating over $-W_m$ to $W_m$):
$$N_o = \int_{-W_m}^{W_m} k_d^2 f^2 N_0 \, df = k_d^2 N_0 \cdot \dfrac{2 W_m^3}{3} = \dfrac{2 k_d^2 N_0 W_m^3}{3}$$

**Output signal power** (for sinusoidal modulation $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ with $\Delta f = k_f A_m$):
$$s_o(t) = k_d \cdot k_f \cdot m(t) = k_d k_f A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

$$S_o = \dfrac{(k_d k_f A_m)^2}{2} = \dfrac{k_d^2 k_f^2 A_m^2}{2} = k_d^2 (\Delta f)^2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}$$

**Output SNR**:
$$SNR_o = \dfrac{k_d^2 (\Delta f)^2 / 2}{2 k_d^2 N_0 W_m^3 / 3} = \dfrac{3 (\Delta f)^2}{4 N_0 W_m^3} = \dfrac{3 \beta_f^2 W_m^2}{4 N_0 W_m^3} = \dfrac{3 \beta_f^2}{4 N_0 W_m}$$

**Input SNR**:
$$SNR_c = \dfrac{S_i}{N_i} = \dfrac{A_c^2 / 2}{2 N_0 (\beta_f + 1)W_m} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4 N_0 (\beta_f + 1)W_m}$$

**Figure of Merit**:
$$F = \dfrac{SNR_o}{SNR_c} = \dfrac{3 \beta_f^2 / (4 N_0 W_m)}{A_c^2 / [4 N_0 (\beta_f + 1)W_m]}$$

$$F = \dfrac{3 \beta_f^2 (\beta_f + 1)}{A_c^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution for sinusoidal modulation**: Using $A_m$ such that $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ with $P_m = A_m^2/2$, and recognizing that for FM the carrier amplitude $A_c$ is **independent** of the message amplitude (constant envelope), one can re-express the FOM in a cleaner form. The standard textbook form is:

$$\boxed{F_{FM} = \dfrac{3}{2}\beta_f^2 (\beta_f + 1) \cdot \dfrac{P_m}{A_c^2 \text{ (renormalized)}}}$$

For **unit-amplitude test sinusoid** where the **normalized** expression is used, the KTU board formula simplifies to:

$$F_{FM} = 3\beta_f^2(\beta_f + 1)$$

For **wideband FM** ($\beta_f \gg 1$):
$$F_{FM} \approx 3\beta_f^3$$

This is the **key result** that gives FM its **superior noise immunity** at the cost of bandwidth.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: PM with Phase Discriminator

**Transmitted signal**:
$$s_i(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)]$$

**Modulation index**: $\beta_p = k_p \max\vert m(t) \vert$

**Input signal power**: $S_i = A_c^2/2$

**Channel noise power** in PM bandwidth (Carson's rule): $W_{PM} = 2(\beta_p + 1)W_m$
$$N_i = 2 N_0 (\beta_p + 1)W_m$$

**Phase discriminator output**:
$$s_o(t) = k_p' m(t) + n_o(t)$$

For high SNR, the output noise PSD is **flat** (white) within the message bandwidth:
$$\Phi_{n_o}(f) = k_p'^2 N_0 \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq W_m$$

**Output noise power**:
$$N_o = 2 k_p'^2 N_0 W_m$$

**Output signal power** (sinusoidal $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, $P_m = A_m^2/2$):
$$S_o = k_p'^2 P_m = k_p'^2 \dfrac{A_m^2}{2}$$

**Output SNR**:
$$SNR_o = \dfrac{k_p'^2 A_m^2 / 2}{2 k_p'^2 N_0 W_m} = \dfrac{A_m^2}{4 N_0 W_m} = \dfrac{P_m}{2 N_0 W_m}$$

**Input SNR**:
$$SNR_c = \dfrac{A_c^2/2}{2 N_0 (\beta_p + 1)W_m} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4 N_0 (\beta_p + 1)W_m}$$

**Figure of Merit** (using $\beta_p = k_p A_m$ for the sinusoidal test):
$$\boxed{F_{PM} = \dfrac{\beta_p^2}{2(\beta_p + 1)}}$$

For **wideband PM** ($\beta_p \gg 1$): $F_{PM} \approx \beta_p/2$.

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Scheme | Bandwidth | $SNR_o$ for given $S_i$ | $F$ Expression | Power Efficiency | BW Efficiency |
|--------|-----------|--------------------------|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| DSB-SC | $2W_m$ | $SNR_c$ | $1$ | **Best** | Moderate |
| SSB-SC | $W_m$ | $SNR_c$ | $1$ | **Best** | **Best** |
| VSB | $\sim W_m$ to $2W_m$ | $\approx SNR_c$ | $\approx 1$ | Best | Good |
| AM (envelope) | $2W_m$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2}{1+m_a^2}SNR_c$ | $\dfrac{m_a^2}{1+m_a^2}$ | Poor | Moderate |
| FM (WB) | $2(\beta_f+1)W_m$ | $3\beta_f^2(\beta_f+1)SNR_c$ | $3\beta_f^2(\beta_f+1)$ | Excellent at high $\beta_f$ | Poor |
| PM (WB) | $2(\beta_p+1)W_m$ | $\dfrac{\beta_p^2}{2(\beta_p+1)}SNR_c$ | $\dfrac{\beta_p^2}{2(\beta_p+1)}$ | Excellent | Poor |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: General SNR/Figure of Merit Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Message Source m of t"] --> node2["Modulator s of i of t"]
    node2 --> node3["Channel AWGN n of i of t"]
    node3 --> node4["Receiver Input r of t = s + n"]
    node4 --> node5["Demodulator"]
    node5 --> node6["Recovered Message s of o + n of o"]
    node1 --> nodeA1["Compute Si power"]
    node3 --> nodeA2["Compute Ni power"]
    nodeA1 --> nodeA3["SNR_c = Si over Ni"]
    node6 --> nodeA4["SNR_o = So over No"]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5["Figure of Merit F = SNR_o over SNR_c"]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA5
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: AM Envelope Detector Noise Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rec1["Received AM Signal r of t"] --> rec2["Bandpass Filter BW 2Wm"]
    rec2 --> rec3["Envelope Detector Diode + RC"]
    rec3 --> rec4["DC Blocker Capacitor"]
    rec4 --> rec5["Audio Output s of o t + n of o t"]
    rec2 -.-> rec6["Input Noise ni t n_c + n_s"]
    rec6 --> rec3
    rec3 --> rec7["Envelope = sqrt"]
    rec7 --> rec8["Threshold Effect at Low SNR"]
    rec3 -.-> rec5
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: FM Demodulator Noise Path (FM Capture Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fm1["FM Received Signal r of t"] --> fm2["Bandpass Limiter Removes AM"]
    fm2 --> fm3["Frequency Discriminator Differentiator"]
    fm3 --> fm4["De-emphasis Filter H of f"]
    fm4 --> fm5["Audio Output so t + no t"]
    fm2 -.-> fm6["Input Noise ni t"]
    fm6 --> fm3
    fm3 -.-> fm7["Output Noise PSD Quad = kd squared f squared N0"]
    fm7 --> fm8["Integrate over W_m to get N_o"]
    fm8 --> fm9["FM Figure of Merit F = 3 beta_f squared beta_f + 1"]
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Noise Performance Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Choose Modulation Scheme"] --> q1{"Priority"}
    q1 -->|"Power Efficiency"| q2["SSB SC or DSB SC"]
    q1 -->|"Receiver Simplicity"| q3["AM DSB C Envelope"]
    q1 -->|"Best Noise Immunity"| q4["Wideband FM"]
    q1 -->|"Best Bandwidth Efficiency"| q5["SSB SC or VSB"]
    q2 --> r1["F = 1 Best Power"]
    q3 --> r2["F max = 1 divided by 2 Cheap Rx"]
    q4 --> r3["F approx 3 beta_f cubed BW cost"]
    q5 --> r4["F = 1 Lowest BW"]
```

## 4.5 Output Noise PSD Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["DSB SC Coherent"] --> blockA1["Flat Output Noise PSD = N0 over W_m"]
    blockB["SSB SC Coherent"] --> blockB1["Flat Output Noise PSD = N0 over W_m"]
    blockC["AM Envelope"] --> blockC1["Flat Output Noise PSD = N0 over W_m"]
    blockD["FM Discriminator"] --> blockD1["Parabolic Output Noise PSD = kd squared f squared N0"]
    blockE["PM Discriminator"] --> blockE1["Flat Output Noise PSD = kp prime squared N0"]
```
