# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Part A pattern. Each question carries 3 marks and demands a crisp, definition-style answer.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define an ideal reconstruction filter. State why it is non-causal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **ideal reconstruction filter** is a lowpass filter with a rectangular (brick-wall) magnitude response given by $H(f) = T_s$ for $\vert f \vert \leq f_m$ and $H(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$. It is used to recover a bandlimited message signal $m(t)$ from its samples $m(nT_s)$ at the receiver. **(1 mark)**
>
> Its impulse response is $h(t) = \text{sinc}(2f_m t) = \frac{\sin(2\pi f_m t)}{2\pi f_m t}$, which is non-zero for $t < 0$. **(1 mark)**
>
> Since the filter requires knowledge of **future** input samples to compute the current output, it is **non-causal** and hence **physically unrealizable**. It is used as a theoretical benchmark only. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Write the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula. What condition must be satisfied for it to be valid?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula expresses the reconstructed signal as a weighted sum of shifted sinc functions:
> $$m(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left[2 f_m (t - nT_s)\right]$$ **(2 marks)**
>
> The formula is valid only if the message signal $m(t)$ is strictly **bandlimited to $f_m$** and the **sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$** (Nyquist criterion) to prevent aliasing. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) is for 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**A continuous-time message signal $m(t)$ is bandlimited to $f_m = 5$ kHz and is sampled at $f_s = 20$ kHz.**

**(a)** Derive the frequency response and impulse response of the ideal reconstruction filter used to recover $m(t)$ from its samples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula, explain how the original signal is reconstructed. If a single sample value $m(0) = 2$ is non-zero and all other samples are zero, sketch the contribution of this sample to the reconstructed output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Frequency Response and Impulse Response Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Cutoff Frequency**
The message is bandlimited to $f_m = 5$ kHz, so the IRF must have a cutoff frequency $f_c = f_m = 5$ kHz. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Sampling Period**
$$T_s = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 50~\mu s$$ **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Frequency Response**
The IRF has the brick-wall response:

$$H(f) = T_s \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{f}{2 f_m}\right) = \begin{cases} 50 \times 10^{-6}, & \vert f \vert \leq 5~\text{kHz} \\ 0, & \vert f \vert > 5~\text{kHz} \end{cases}$$ **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Impulse Response via Inverse FT**
$$h(t) = \int_{-5k}^{5k} 50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot e^{j2\pi f t} \, df = 50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t)}{\pi t}$$

Simplifying:

$$h(t) = \text{sinc}(10{,}000 \, t) = \frac{\sin(2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t)}{2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t}$$ **(3 marks)**

---

**(b) Reconstruction via Whittaker–Shannon Formula (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Interpolation Formula**
$$m(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left[2 f_m (t - nT_s)\right] = \sum_n m(nT_s) \cdot \text{sinc}\left[10{,}000 (t - 50 \times 10^{-6} n)\right]$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Physical Interpretation**
Each sample $m(nT_s)$ generates a sinc pulse centered at $t = nT_s$. The reconstructed signal is the sum (superposition) of all these shifted, weighted sinc pulses. The sinc pulses overlap and interfere constructively at sample points and decay to zero at all other sample times (orthogonality property). **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Single-Sample Case**
If only $m(0) = 2$ is non-zero:

$$m(t) = 2 \cdot \text{sinc}\left[10{,}000 \, t\right] = 2 \cdot \frac{\sin(2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t)}{2\pi \cdot 5000 \cdot t}$$

This is a single sinc pulse with peak amplitude **2 V** at $t = 0$, oscillating with zero crossings at $t = \pm 100~\mu s, \pm 200~\mu s, \ldots$ and decaying amplitude. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**A message signal $m(t)$ has a maximum frequency component of $f_m = 4$ kHz and is sampled at a rate $f_s = 10$ kHz.**

**(a)** Explain the sampling process in the frequency domain and show how spectral replicas arise. Why is a lowpass filter necessary at the receiver? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the frequency response, impulse response, and bandwidth of the ideal reconstruction filter. Discuss why this filter is non-causal and what its physical implications are. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Sampling in the Frequency Domain (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Sampled Signal**
The sampled signal is modeled as:
$$m_s(t) = m(t) \cdot \sum_{n} \delta(t - nT_s) = \sum_{n} m(nT_s) \cdot \delta(t - nT_s)$$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Fourier Transform**
Using the multiplication property:
$$M_s(f) = M(f) * \left[\frac{1}{T_s} \sum_k \delta(f - kf_s)\right] = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - kf_s)$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Spectral Replicas**
The original spectrum $M(f)$ is replicated at $f = 0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ Each replica is amplitude-scaled by $1/T_s = f_s$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Need for LPF**
Since $f_s = 10$ kHz $> 2f_m = 8$ kHz, the replicas do **not overlap** (no aliasing). To extract only the baseband replica (centered at $f = 0$), we need a lowpass filter with cutoff $\geq 4$ kHz but $< 6$ kHz. The IRF is the **ideal** choice with cutoff exactly 4 kHz. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) IRF Characteristics and Physical Implication (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Frequency Response**
$$H(f) = T_s = 0.1~\text{ms} \quad \text{for} \quad \vert f \vert \leq 4~\text{kHz}; \quad H(f) = 0 \quad \text{otherwise}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Impulse Response**
$$h(t) = \text{sinc}(2 f_m t) = \text{sinc}(8000\, t) = \frac{\sin(2\pi \cdot 4000 \cdot t)}{2\pi \cdot 4000 \cdot t}$$ **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Bandwidth**
The bandwidth of the IRF is $B = f_m = 4$ kHz. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Non-Causality**
The impulse response has non-zero values for $t < 0$, meaning the filter requires **future** samples. This is **physically unrealizable** in real-time. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Practical Implications**
- Real systems use **causal approximations**: raised-cosine filters, FIR interpolation with delay, or sample-and-hold circuits.
- The IRF serves as the **theoretical reference** for evaluating the quality of these practical filters. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Watch Out!):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $T_s$ scaling factor** in $H(f)$: Students often write $H(f) = 1$ for the passband. The correct value is $T_s$ to maintain unity DC gain through the chain. **Penalty: −1 mark**
>
> 2. **Confusing normalized sinc definitions**: The engineering sinc is $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$, while the mathematical sinc is $\sin(x)/x$. Mixing them leads to wrong impulse response expressions. **Penalty: −1 to −2 marks**
>
> 3. **Missing the Nyquist condition statement**: When writing the Whittaker–Shannon formula, students often forget to explicitly state the requirement $f_s \geq 2f_m$. **Penalty: −0.5 to −1 mark**
>
> 4. **Saying "the IRF is realizable with infinite delay"** — this is a controversial statement. Examiners expect the textbook answer: **"IRF is non-causal and physically unrealizable."** Adding 'infinite delay' muddles the point. **Penalty: −1 mark**
>
> 5. **Skipping units in the cutoff frequency**: Always write $f_c = 4$ **kHz** or $4 \times 10^3$ **Hz**, not just 4. **Penalty: −0.5 mark**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Ideal Reconstruction Filter**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ The IRF is a **brick-wall lowpass filter** with magnitude $T_s$ in the passband ($\vert f \vert \leq f_m$) and zero in the stopband.
- ✅ Its **impulse response** is a **sinc function**: $h(t) = \text{sinc}(2 f_m t)$.
- ✅ It converts the **PAM impulse train** back into the **original continuous-time** message signal.
- ✅ The output is given by the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula** (Cardinal Series).

### Key Conditions
- ✅ The message must be **strictly bandlimited** to $f_m$.
- ✅ Sampling rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (Nyquist).
- ✅ Under these conditions, reconstruction is **perfect and distortion-free**.

### Physical Limitations
- ✅ IRF is **non-causal** (h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0) → **physically unrealizable**.
- ✅ Impulse response has **infinite duration** → cannot be implemented with finite components.
- ✅ Requires **infinite order** → cannot be built with finite R, L, C elements.
- ✅ The IRF is a **theoretical benchmark**, not a practical filter.

### Practical Alternatives
- ✅ **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — used in DACs, has $(\sin x)/x$ droop.
- ✅ **First-Order Hold** — linear interpolation between samples.
- ✅ **Raised-Cosine Filter** — smooth roll-off, controlled by roll-off factor $\alpha$.
- ✅ **FIR/IIR Digital Filters** — designed to approximate the IRF within tolerable error.

### Exam-Boost Quick-Fire Points
- ✅ IRF bandwidth = $f_m$ (message bandwidth).
- ✅ Passband gain of IRF = $T_s = 1/f_s$.
- ✅ Number of zero crossings in sinc pulse per unit interval = $f_s/f_m$ (related to oversampling ratio).
- ✅ Linear phase response ensures **no phase distortion** in the reconstructed signal.
- ✅ The term "ideal" refers to **zero transition band** and **infinite stopband attenuation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)** ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)** is a fundamental class of Pulse Modulation in which the **amplitude of a train of periodic pulses** is varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the message/baseband signal $m(t)$, while the pulse width and pulse position remain constant. The carrier is no longer a continuous sinusoid (as in AM), but a **periodic rectangular pulse train**. Formally, the PAM signal is expressed as: $$ s_{PAM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) $$ where $p(t)$ is the periodic pulse train (switching function) and $m(t)$ is the message signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PAM is the *first and most fundamental* member of the Pulse Modulation family, which is the gateway to understanding **PWM, PPM, PCM, and TDM**. KTU examiners often test the spectral/bandwidth relationship and the difference between Natural PAM and Flat-Top PAM. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are sitting in a dark cinema, and the only light source is a **flashlight** (the pulse) that blinks on and off rapidly. The brightness of each flash represents the loudness of the sound at that moment: - A **loud sound** → a **bright flash** - A **whisper** → a **dim flash** - The flash itself (ON/OFF timing) stays the same — only the *intensity* (amplitude) changes. That is exactly what PAM does! Instead of transmitting a continuous wave, we "flash" the amplitude of the message signal at regular sampling intervals. The signal exists in short, discrete pulses, not continuously. ### 1.3 Types of PAM (KTU Classification) | Type | Full Name | Pulse Shape at Sampling | Filter Needed? | |---|---|---|---| | **PAM-Natural (PAM-N)** | Natural Sampling PAM | Pulses follow the curve of $m(t)$ (truncated tops) | Yes (LPF) | | **PAM-Flat Top** | Flat-Top / Chopped PAM | Each pulse has constant amplitude (held by hold circuit) | Yes (LPF + Equalizer) | | **Ideal Sampling** | Impulse Sampled | Impulses scaled by $m(t)$ | Ideal LPF | > [!NOTE] > **Key Parameter — Sampling Theorem:** For faithful recovery, the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$ where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component of $m(t)$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2f_m$ is the **Nyquist Rate**. ### 1.4 Physical & Standard Metrics - **Sampling Frequency** ($f_s$): typically **8 kHz** for voice signals ($f_m = 4\,\text{kHz}$) - **Pulse Width** ($\tau$): must be much smaller than sampling period $T_s$ (duty cycle $\tau/T_s \ll 1$) - **Typical Duty Cycle**: **10%–20%** for practical systems - **Guard Bands**: Small time gaps between consecutive pulses to prevent inter-symbol interference - **Universal Constant**: All recovery systems depend on the **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** cutoff $\approx f_m$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of PAM The generation of PAM is essentially a **multiplication operation** in the time domain, which corresponds to **convolution in the frequency domain**. This is the bedrock of pulse modulation theory. **Step 1: Define the message signal** The low-pass message signal $m(t)$ has a maximum frequency $f_m$ and a baseband spectrum $M(f)$ confined to $[-f_m, +f_m]$. **Step 2: Define the pulse train** A periodic rectangular pulse train $p(t)$ with period $T_s = 1/f_s$, pulse width $\tau$, and amplitude $A$ is used as the switching/carrier function. **Step 3: Natural Sampling (Multiplication)** The message modulates the *shape* of the pulse itself. Each pulse is a literal "slice" of the message signal of duration $\tau$ — the top of the pulse is curved and follows $m(t)$. **Step 4: Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold)** A practical circuit (the **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** block) reads the value of $m(t)$ at the sampling instant and *holds* that constant value for the entire duration $\tau$. The pulse top is perfectly flat. **Step 5: Demodulation** At the receiver, the PAM signal is passed through a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** with cutoff $f_m$ to extract the original message $m(t)$ (with possible DC offset and aperture distortion in flat-top PAM). ### 2.2 Why PAM? Real-World Engineering Utility - **Foundational step** to PCM (Pulse Code Modulation), TDM (Time Division Multiplexing), and digital communication. - Enables **time-sharing** of a single communication channel among multiple users (TDM application). - Reduces power consumption compared to continuous-wave transmission. - Forms the basis of **modern digital audio (CD-quality uses PCM)**, **telephony**, and **signal processing instrumentation**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This for 14-Mark Problems!** | # | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pulse Train (Time Domain) | $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{rect}\left(\frac{t-nT_s}{\tau}\right)$ | Periodic pulses of width $\tau$, period $T_s$ | | 2 | Fourier Series of $p(t)$ | $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j2\pi n f_s t}$ | Where $C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s}\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_s}\right)$ | | 3 | Sinc Function | $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ | **Always check argument form in KTU questions** | | 4 | DC Component of Pulse Train | $C_0 = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} = A \cdot \text{duty cycle}$ | Average value of pulse train | | 5 | Natural PAM | $s_{PAM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t)$ | Direct multiplication | | 6 | Flat-Top PAM | $s_{FT}(t) = \sum_{n} m(nT_s) \cdot \text{rect}\left(\frac{t-nT_s}{\tau}\right)$ | Each pulse held at $m(nT_s)$ | | 7 | Spectrum (Natural PAM) | $S(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \cdot M(f - nf_s)$ | Replicas of $M(f)$ scaled by $C_n$ | | 8 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ | **Nyquist Criterion** | | 9 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2f_m$ | Minimum sampling rate | | 10 | Reconstruction Filter | LPF cutoff $= f_m$ | Recovers $m(t)$ from PAM | | 11 | Aperture Distortion (Flat-Top) | $H_{eq}(f) = \frac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)}$ | Equalizer needed to compensate | | 12 | Transmission Bandwidth | $BW = 2f_m$ (for baseband LPF) or $f_s$ (for first replica) | For natural sampling | | 13 | SNR (PAM, baseband) | $\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{PAM} = \frac{P_m}{\eta B}$ | Where $P_m$ is signal power, $\eta$ is PSD | | 14 | Channel SNR (after LPF) | $\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_o = \frac{A^2 \overline{m^2(t)}}{2N_0 B}$ | Flat-top PAM output SNR | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Trick:** The function $\text{sinc}(x)$ in communication theory is **always normalized** as $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$, unlike the unnormalized math version. Misreading this will cost you the entire 7-mark sub-part! ### 2.4 Aperture Effect (Aperture Distortion) A unique problem in **Flat-Top PAM** is the **Aperture Effect** because the pulse has a finite width $\tau$. The spectrum gets multiplied by a $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ factor, attenuating higher frequencies. The equalizer transfer function that **flattens** this distortion is: $$ H_{eq}(f) = \frac{1}{H_0(f)} = \frac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)} = \frac{1}{\text{sinc}(f\tau)} $$ The equalizer is placed **after the LPF** at the receiver. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Spectrum of Natural PAM We now derive the spectrum of Natural PAM, which is the **most-asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme. **Step 1: Write the pulse train using the Fourier Series.** The periodic pulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s$ can be expanded as: $$ p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, e^{j2\pi n f_s t} $$ **Step 2: Find the Fourier coefficients $C_n$.** For a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and amplitude $A$ centered at $t=0$: $$ C_n = \frac{1}{T_s} \int_{-T_s/2}^{T_s/2} p(t) \, e^{-j2\pi n f_s t} \, dt $$ Since $p(t) = A$ only between $-\tau/2$ and $+\tau/2$: $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \int_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} e^{-j2\pi n f_s t} \, dt $$ Evaluate the integral: $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \left[ \frac{e^{-j2\pi n f_s t}}{-j2\pi n f_s} \right]_{-\tau/2}^{\tau/2} $$ $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{e^{-j\pi n f_s \tau} - e^{+j\pi n f_s \tau}}{-j2\pi n f_s} $$ Using Euler's identity $\sin(\theta) = \frac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$: $$ C_n = \frac{A}{T_s} \cdot \frac{-2j\sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{-j2\pi n f_s} $$ $$ C_n = \frac{A \sin(\pi n f_s \tau)}{\pi n f_s T_s} $$ Substituting $f_s = 1/T_s$: $$ C_n = \frac{A \tau}{T_s} \cdot \frac{\sin(\pi n \tau / T_s)}{\pi n \tau / T_s} $$ This is the famous $\text{sinc}$ form: $$ \boxed{C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_s}\right)} $$ **Step 3: Write the Natural PAM signal.** $$ s_{PAM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) = m(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j2\pi n f_s t} $$ Rearranging: $$ s_{PAM}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, m(t) \, e^{j2\pi n f_s t} $$ **Step 4: Take the Fourier Transform.** Using the **frequency-shifting property**: $m(t) \cdot e^{j2\pi n f_s t} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} M(f - n f_s)$ $$ S_{PAM}(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, M(f - n f_s) $$ **Step 5: Substitute $C_n$.** $$ \boxed{S_{PAM}(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Interpretation:** The spectrum consists of **infinitely many replicas of $M(f)$**, each centered at $n f_s$ and weighted by the $\text{sinc}$ coefficient $C_n$. The replica at $n=0$ is the **baseband component** $\frac{A\tau}{T_s} M(f)$, which is the scaled original message — recoverable via LPF. ### 3.2 Derivation: Spectrum of Flat-Top PAM **Step 1: Define the flat-top pulse using convolution.** A single flat-top pulse of width $\tau$ and amplitude $m(nT_s)$ can be modeled as the message sample $m(nT_s)$ multiplied by a rectangular pulse $h(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$. **Step 2: Write the flat-top PAM signal.** $$ s_{FT}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \, h(t - nT_s) $$ **Step 3: Take the Fourier Transform.** Using the time-shifting property: $$ S_{FT}(f) = H(f) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \, e^{-j2\pi n f T_s} $$ The sum is the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of the sampled signal. By Poisson Summation Formula, this equals $\frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k} M(f - k f_s)$. Therefore: $$ S_{FT}(f) = \frac{H(f)}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s) $$ **Step 4: Substitute $H(f) = A\tau \,\text{sinc}(f\tau)$.** $$ \boxed{S_{FT}(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)} $$ **Key Difference:** Compared to Natural PAM, every replica is multiplied by an extra envelope $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ — the **Aperture Effect**. This causes amplitude distortion that must be corrected by an equalizer. ### 3.3 Derivation: Reconstruction using LPF For Natural PAM, applying an ideal LPF with gain $T_s/(A\tau)$ and cutoff $f_m$ to $S_{PAM}(f)$: The LPF retains only the $n=0$ term: $\frac{A\tau}{T_s} M(f)$. After amplification by $T_s/(A\tau)$: $$ M_{out}(f) = M(f) \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{out}(t) = m(t) \quad \checkmark $$ Perfect reconstruction, provided $f_s \geq 2f_m$ (no aliasing). ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > **Problem:** A 4 kHz sinusoidal message is sampled at $f_s = 10\,\text{kHz}$ with pulse width $\tau = 20\,\mu s$ and amplitude $A = 5\,\text{V}$. Calculate (a) Duty Cycle, (b) DC component $C_0$, (c) First harmonic amplitude $C_1$. **Solution:** (a) Sampling period $T_s = 1/f_s = 1/10000 = 0.1\,\text{ms} = 100\,\mu s$ $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{\tau}{T_s} = \frac{20\,\mu s}{100\,\mu s} = 0.2 = 20\% $$ (b) DC component: $$ C_0 = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} = 5 \times 0.2 = 1\,\text{V} $$ (c) First harmonic: $$ C_1 = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\tau}{T_s}\right) = 1 \times \text{sinc}(0.2) $$ $$ C_1 = 1 \times \frac{\sin(\pi \times 0.2)}{\pi \times 0.2} = \frac{\sin(0.6283)}{0.6283} $$ $$ C_1 = \frac{0.5878}{0.6283} \approx 0.9355\,\text{V} $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Always show the **sinc argument explicitly**. Many students lose 2 marks by skipping the intermediate form. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: PAM Transmitter and Receiver ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Message Signal m_t] --> B[Multiplier / Chopper] C[Pulse Train Generator
fs and tau] --> B B --> D[PAM Signal s_PAM_t] D --> E[Channel] E --> F[Receiver] F --> G[Low Pass Filter
Cutoff fm] G --> H[Equalizer
1 over sinc f tau] H --> I[Reconstructed m_hat_t] J[Sampler Clock fs] -.-> C J -.-> K[Sample and Hold] K --> L[Flat-Top Pulse Train] L --> B ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Waveform / Process Flow (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD node1[Message Signal m_t
Continuous Sinusoid] --> node2{Sampling Type?} node2 -- Natural PAM --> node3[Multiply m_t by p_t
Pulses follow m_t shape] node2 -- Flat-Top PAM --> node4[Sample and Hold
Each pulse held constant] node3 --> node5[PAM Waveform
Curved-Top Pulses] node4 --> node6[Flat-Top Pulse Train] node5 --> node7[Channel Transmission] node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8[Receiver LPF
Cutoff fm] node8 --> node9{Flat-Top?} node9 -- Yes --> node10[Aperture Equalizer
H_eq f = 1 over sinc f tau] node9 -- No --> node11[Direct Output] node10 --> node12[Reconstructed m_t] node11 --> node12 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid: Spectral Representation of Natural PAM ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Baseband M_f
0 to fm] --> B[Copy at 0 fs] A --> C[Copy at 1 fs scaled by C1] A --> D[Copy at 2 fs scaled by C2] A --> E[Copy at minus 1 fs scaled by C1] A --> F[Copy at minus 2 fs scaled by C2] G[LPF recovers M_f] -.-> A ``` ### 4.4 ASCII Schematic Summary ``` m(t) ___ ___ (Message: smooth sinusoid) / \ / \ ... p(t) _ _ _ _ _ (Pulse Train: narrow rectangles) PAM /\__/\__/\__/\__ (Natural PAM: pulses slice m(t)) FT __|--|__|--|__|-- (Flat-Top: held constant amplitude) Output ___ ___ (After LPF: smooth m(t) recovered) / \ / \ ... ``` > [!NOTE] > The **Mermaid diagrams** above are kept **text-clean** (no markdown formatting inside node labels) to ensure **successful rendering** in all KTU-compatible viewers. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define Pulse Amplitude Modulation. Distinguish between Natural PAM and Flat-Top PAM.** **Model Answer:** **PAM Definition:** Pulse Amplitude Modulation is a type of pulse modulation in which the amplitude of a periodic pulse train is varied in proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, while the pulse width and pulse position remain constant. | Feature | Natural PAM | Flat-Top PAM | |---|---|---| | Pulse Top Shape | Curved (follows $m(t)$) | Flat (constant during $\tau$) | | Generation | Direct multiplication | Sample-and-Hold circuit | | Aperture Distortion | Not present | Present (needs equalizer) | | Practical Use | Mostly theoretical | Used in practical PCM systems | | Reconstruction | Simple LPF | LPF + Equalizer | **[3 Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison table]** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State and explain the Nyquist Sampling Theorem as applicable to PAM systems.** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** A band-limited signal $m(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: $$ f_s \geq 2f_m $$ **Explanation:** When $m(t)$ is multiplied by a pulse train of frequency $f_s$, its spectrum $M(f)$ is replicated at integer multiples of $f_s$. If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, the replicas do not overlap (no aliasing), and a Low-Pass Filter of cutoff $f_m$ can extract the baseband $M(f)$ to recover $m(t)$. **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$ is the *minimum* sampling frequency. The frequency $2f_m$ is also called the **Nyquist Bandwidth**. **[3 Marks: 1 for statement + 1 for condition + 1 for explanation]** --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks] Derive the expression for the spectrum of Natural Sampled PAM signal. Draw its spectrum and explain how the original message is recovered.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Define the message and pulse train.** Message: $m(t)$ with spectrum $M(f)$ band-limited to $f_m$. Pulse train: $p(t)$ with period $T_s = 1/f_s$, width $\tau$, amplitude $A$. **Step 2: Fourier Series expansion of pulse train.** $$ p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n e^{j2\pi n f_s t} $$ where the coefficients are: $$ C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_s}\right) $$ **Step 3: Natural PAM signal.** $$ s_{PAM}(t) = m(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, m(t) \, e^{j2\pi n f_s t} $$ **Step 4: Fourier Transform.** $$ S_{PAM}(f) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} C_n \, M(f - n f_s) $$ $$ \boxed{S_{PAM}(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_s}\right) M(f - n f_s)} $$ **Step 5: Spectrum Description.** The spectrum contains replicas of $M(f)$ at $f = 0, \pm f_s, \pm 2f_s, \ldots$ with amplitudes scaled by $\text{sinc}(n\tau/T_s)$. **Step 6: Recovery.** An ideal LPF with gain $T_s/(A\tau)$ and cutoff $f_m$ retains only the $n=0$ term $\frac{A\tau}{T_s} M(f)$, which after amplification gives back $M(f) \Rightarrow m(t)$. **[7 Marks: 2 for setup + 2 for derivation + 2 for spectrum description + 1 for recovery]** --- **(b) [7 Marks] A sinusoidal message $m(t) = 3\cos(2000\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 8\,\text{kHz}$ with pulse width $\tau = 25\,\mu s$ and amplitude $A = 4\,\text{V}$. Find (i) Sampling theorem check (ii) DC component $C_0$ (iii) First two harmonic amplitudes $C_1, C_2$ (iv) Transmission bandwidth.** **Model Solution:** (i) **Sampling Theorem Check:** Message frequency $f_m = 1000\,\text{Hz}$. Nyquist rate $= 2 \times 1000 = 2000\,\text{Hz}$. Since $f_s = 8000 > 2000$, the condition $f_s \geq 2f_m$ is satisfied. ✓ (ii) **DC Component:** $T_s = 1/f_s = 1/8000 = 125\,\mu s$ $$ C_0 = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} = \frac{4 \times 25}{125} = \frac{100}{125} = 0.8\,\text{V} $$ (iii) **First Two Harmonics:** $\tau/T_s = 25/125 = 0.2$ $$ C_1 = C_0 \cdot \text{sinc}(0.2) = 0.8 \times \frac{\sin(0.2\pi)}{0.2\pi} $$ $$ C_1 = 0.8 \times \frac{0.5878}{0.6283} = 0.8 \times 0.9355 = 0.7484\,\text{V} $$ $$ C_2 = C_0 \cdot \text{sinc}(0.4) = 0.8 \times \frac{\sin(0.4\pi)}{0.4\pi} = 0.8 \times \frac{0.9511}{1.2566} = 0.8 \times 0.7568 = 0.6055\,\text{V} $$ (iv) **Transmission Bandwidth:** For Natural PAM, the bandwidth needed to retain the baseband and one set of sidebands is: $$ BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 1000 = 2\,\text{kHz} $$ (For the full PAM transmission considering replicas, $BW = f_s/2 = 4\,\text{kHz}$ is also accepted by KTU examiners.) **[7 Marks: 1+1+3+2 distribution]** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the generation and demodulation of Flat-Top PAM with a neat block diagram. Derive its spectrum and explain the aperture effect.** **Model Solution:** **Generation:** A **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** circuit samples $m(t)$ at $nT_s$ and holds the constant value $m(nT_s)$ for the pulse duration $\tau$. This produces a flat-top pulse train. Mathematically: $$ s_{FT}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) \cdot h(t - nT_s) $$ where $h(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$. **Spectrum Derivation:** Taking the Fourier Transform: $$ S_{FT}(f) = H(f) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m(nT_s) e^{-j2\pi n f T_s} $$ By Poisson summation, the discrete sum becomes $\frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k} M(f - k f_s)$. Substituting $H(f) = A\tau \,\text{sinc}(f\tau)$: $$ \boxed{S_{FT}(f) = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}(f\tau) \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} M(f - k f_s)} $$ **Aperture Effect:** Every spectral replica is weighted by $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$, which is not flat. Higher frequencies near $f_m$ are attenuated, causing amplitude distortion. **Demodulation:** Pass $s_{FT}(t)$ through an LPF (cutoff $f_m$) to recover the baseband, then through an **equalizer** with transfer function: $$ H_{eq}(f) = \frac{1}{\text{sinc}(f\tau)} = \frac{\pi f \tau}{\sin(\pi f \tau)} $$ to compensate the aperture distortion and obtain $m(t)$. **[7 Marks: 2 generation + 2 derivation + 2 aperture effect + 1 demodulation]** --- **(b) [7 Marks] Compare PAM, PWM, and PPM systems. Explain why PWM and PPM are superior to PAM in noise immunity, and state two disadvantages of PAM.** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | PAM | PWM (PTM) | PPM (PTM) | |---|---|---|---| | Modulated Feature | Amplitude | Width | Position | | Bandwidth | Lowest | Higher | Highest | | Noise Immunity | Poor | Moderate | Best | | Power Efficiency | Moderate | Better | Best | | Information in | Amplitude | Pulse width | Pulse position | | Constant amplitude? | No | Yes | Yes | **Noise Immunity Reasoning:** - In **PAM**, the information is in pulse amplitude. Noise directly adds to amplitude → easily corrupts message. - In **PWM**, information is in *pulse width* — small noise in amplitude does not change width significantly → better immunity. - In **PPM**, information is in *pulse position*. Amplitude noise has no effect on position → best immunity (only timing jitter matters). **Two Disadvantages of PAM:** 1. **Poor noise immunity** — amplitude is directly affected by additive channel noise. 2. **Variable amplitude requires variable transmitter power**, leading to inefficiency compared to constant-amplitude PWM/PPM. **[7 Marks: 3 for table + 2 for reasoning + 2 for disadvantages]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > 1. **Sinc Function Confusion:** KTU uses $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$. Students often write $\sin(x)/x$ form — this is mathematically the same only when argument is normalized. Always show the argument explicitly. > 2. **Missing the $\frac{A\tau}{T_s}$ factor:** Many students forget the duty cycle scaling in $C_n$ — losing 2 marks per sub-part. > 3. **Confusing Natural and Flat-Top:** Flat-Top has $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ in the spectrum; Natural has it only in the harmonic weights. This is a **favourite 7-mark trap**. > 4. **Forgetting the Equalizer in Flat-Top recovery:** Marks deducted if you recover Flat-Top PAM with just an LPF. > 5. **Aliasing Violation:** If $f_s < 2f_m$, no KTU examiner will award full marks — they specifically test this edge case. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam Day** - ✅ **PAM** = Pulse Amplitude Modulation — varies amplitude of pulses with message. - ✅ **Generation** = Multiplication of $m(t)$ with pulse train $p(t)$. - ✅ **Two Types**: **Natural** (curved top) and **Flat-Top** (constant top, S/H circuit). - ✅ **Pulse Train Fourier Coefficient:** $C_n = \frac{A\tau}{T_s} \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{n\tau}{T_s}\right)$. - ✅ **Natural PAM Spectrum:** $S_{PAM}(f) = \sum_n C_n M(f - nf_s)$ — replicated baseband. - ✅ **Flat-Top PAM Spectrum:** Has extra $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ envelope — the **Aperture Effect**. - ✅ **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ — non-negotiable. - ✅ **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$. - ✅ **Recovery:** LPF (cutoff $f_m$) for both; add **Equalizer** $1/\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ for Flat-Top. - ✅ **Bandwidth of PAM:** $BW = 2f_m$ (baseband recovery) or $f_s$ (replica). - ✅ **Disadvantages:** Poor noise immunity, variable transmitter power. - ✅ **Position in KTU Syllabus:** Gateway to **PWM, PPM, PCM, TDM** — must master this first. - ✅ **Sinc Convention in KTU:** Always $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$ — write it explicitly. - ✅ **Mnemonic:** *"**P**AM, **P**WM, **P**PM — A, W, P"* — varies **A**mplitude, **W**idth, **P**osition respectively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** When solving numericals, always convert $\tau$ and $T_s$ to **the same unit** ($\mu s$ or $ms$) **before** dividing. The examiner instantly catches unit mismatch and deducts at least 1 mark per numerical sub-part.

topics.title: Time Division Multiplexing with PAM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Division Multiplexing with PAM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) with Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)** is a composite communication technique that exploits the time-domain orthogonality between multiple bandlimited message signals by interleaving their **Pulse Amplitude Modulated (PAM)** samples into non-overlapping sequential time slots within a recurrent transmission frame.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this topic is classified under **Module 3: Pulse Modulation and Time Division Multiplexing**, and represents one of the most commercially significant applications of the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** (1948–1949).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):**
> *"TDM-PAM is a process in which several PAM signals, each derived from a different message source, are transmitted over a common channel by assigning each source a distinct time slot within a periodic frame, such that the instantaneous samples of all sources do not overlap in time."*

**Formal Symbolism:**
If we have **N** message signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \ldots, m_N(t)$, each bandlimited to $W$ Hz, then the TDM-PAM composite signal is mathematically expressed as:

$$
s_{TDM}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} m_k(t) \cdot p(t - kT_s)
$$

where $p(t)$ is the PAM pulse train and $T_s$ is the per-channel sampling interval.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Turnstile at a Cinema"**
> 
> Imagine **N people** (your N message signals) standing at a single door that leads to one hallway (the communication channel). Only one person can pass through the turnstile at a time. The doorman (the commutator switch) rotates rapidly, allowing Person 1 in for 1 second, then Person 2 for 1 second, and so on. By the time the cycle repeats (the **frame period**), every person has had their chance. To an outside observer walking down the hallway, the people appear to be moving "simultaneously" — but in reality, they passed one at a time.
> 
> **The "person" here = a PAM sample of a voice signal.**
> **The "turnstile cycle" = one TDM frame.**

### Key Intuitive Takeaways:
- **Time is the resource being divided** (unlike FDM, where frequency/bandwidth is divided).
- Every signal must be **sampled first** (PAM), then **interleaved** (TDM).
- The receiver uses a **synchronized de-commutator** to route each slot back to its correct output.
- **Synchronization** is everything — even a microsecond mismatch destroys intelligibility.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Notation | Typical Value / Constraint | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Frequency** | $f_s$ | $\geq 2W$ Hz (Nyquist) | **Critical** |
| **Nyquist Rate** | $f_N$ | $2W$ Hz | Lower bound |
| **Channels Multiplexed** | $N$ | Typically 4, 8, 12, 24, 30 | System-dependent |
| **Frame Period** | $T_f$ | $N \cdot T_s$ seconds | Determines throughput |
| **Frame Rate** | $f_f$ | $1/T_f = f_s/N$ Hz | Equal to sampling rate of each ch. |
| **Guard Time** | $T_g$ | 10–20% of $T_s$ | Prevents inter-channel crosstalk |
| **Pulse Width** | $\tau$ | $< T_s/N$ | Avoids overlap |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always explicitly state the **Nyquist criterion** ($f_s \geq 2W$) in your definitions. Examiners award marks for invoking sampling theory by name.

---

## 1.4 Why TDM-PAM Matters in Modern Engineering

- **Telephony Backbone:** The historic **T1 carrier (1.544 Mbps)** multiplexes **24 voice channels** using TDM-PAM (now TDM-PCM in practice, but the underlying principle is identical).
- **Digital Subscriber Lines (DSL):** Use TDM at the symbol level.
- **Satellite Telemetry & Spacecraft:** TDM-PAM transmits hundreds of sensor readings over a single downlink.
- **Biomedical Signal Acquisition:** ECG, EEG, EMG from multiple electrodes are routinely TDM-multiplexed before ADC conversion.
- **Industrial Process Control:** SCADA systems rely on TDM to centralize remote sensor data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Foundation — The Two-Stage Principle

TDM-PAM operates in **two distinct stages**:

### Stage 1: Pulse Amplitude Modulation (PAM)
Each continuous-time, bandlimited message signal $m_k(t)$ (bandwidth $W$ Hz) is multiplied by a periodic pulse train $p(t)$ of period $T_s = 1/f_s$, where $f_s \geq 2W$ (Nyquist criterion). The output is a sequence of amplitude-modulated pulses whose **envelope tracks the message**.

### Stage 2: Time Division Multiplexing
The PAM outputs of all N channels are routed through a high-speed **electronic commutator** (typically a rotating switch, an analog multiplexer IC like CD4051, or in modern systems, a time-slot allocator in an FPGA) that sequentially connects each channel to the common transmission line for exactly one time slot of duration $\tau$.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Exam Favourite 7-Mark Structure:** "Explain TDM-PAM with block diagram." The following 7 points are how the board expects you to answer.

1. **N independent message sources** $m_1(t), m_2(t), \ldots, m_N(t)$ — each bandlimited to $W$ Hz — are fed into N parallel **PAM modulators**.
2. Each PAM modulator uses a pulse train of period $T_s$. By Nyquist's theorem, $T_s \leq 1/(2W)$.
3. The outputs of all N PAM modulators are connected to the **N input ports** of an **electronic commutator** (rotary switch / analog mux).
4. The commutator rotates at angular velocity $\omega_c = 2\pi/T_f$, dwelling on each input for a duration $\tau$ before switching to the next. The **frame time** is $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ (ignoring guard time).
5. The commutator's serial output is the **multiplexed PAM waveform**, which is then **filtered, amplified, and transmitted** over the channel.
6. At the receiver, a **synchronized de-commutator** (rotating in lockstep with the transmitter) separates the composite waveform back into N PAM streams.
7. Each stream is passed through a **low-pass reconstruction filter** (cutoff $\geq W$ Hz) to recover the original message $m_k(t)$.

---

## 2.3 Synchronization — The Hidden Hero

The transmitter and receiver commutators **must rotate at the same speed and in the same phase**. This is enforced by:

- A **SYNC pulse** (a distinctive marker pulse, often at higher amplitude) appended to every frame.
- A **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL)** at the receiver that locks onto this SYNC pulse.
- **Guard bands in time** ($T_g$) to absorb any minor timing jitter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Synchronization failure** manifests as **inter-channel crosstalk** — bits of one conversation bleeding into another. In voice telephony, this produces a characteristic "warbling" or "echo of another caller." KTU questions frequently test this.

---

## 2.4 Crosstalk in TDM-PAM

**Crosstalk** is the unwanted leakage of one channel's sample into an adjacent channel's time slot. Two primary causes:

| Cause | Mechanism | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Insufficient bandwidth** | Channel low-pass acts as integrator, smearing adjacent samples | Increase channel BW to $B \gg N f_s$ |
| **Switching transients** | Commutator finite rise/fall time | Add **guard time** $T_g$ |
| **Timing jitter** | Clock instability at receiver | Use PLL and stable crystal oscillators |
| **Pulse width too large** | $\tau$ approaches $T_s$ | Ensure $\tau \ll T_s$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Phrasing:** "Crosstalk in TDM occurs mainly due to the finite bandwidth of the channel, which causes the flat-top pulses to spread and overlap into adjacent time slots." Memorize this exact reasoning.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2W$ | Nyquist sampling rate | Hz |
| 2 | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | Sampling interval per channel | seconds |
| 3 | $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ | Frame duration (no guard) | seconds |
| 4 | $T_f = N T_s + (N+1) T_g$ | Frame duration (with guard) | seconds |
| 5 | $f_f = 1/T_f$ | Frame rate = per-channel sampling rate | Hz |
| 6 | $\tau \leq T_s / N$ | Max pulse width to avoid overlap | seconds |
| 7 | $B_{TDM} \geq N \cdot f_s / 2$ | Minimum channel bandwidth | Hz |
| 8 | $S/N = (A^2 / 2) / (N \cdot \sigma^2)$ | TDM output SNR (M-ary PAM-like) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\eta = N \tau / T_f$ | Channel utilization efficiency | fraction (0–1) |
| 10 | Crosstalk $\propto e^{-B \cdot T_s}$ | Channel BW vs adjacent slot separation | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formula 7 — Bandwidth Derivation (most-tested):**
> The TDM-PAM composite signal has a fundamental pulse rate of $N f_s$ pulses/sec. By Nyquist's first criterion (zero-ISI), the minimum transmission bandwidth is:
> $$B_{TDM} \geq \frac{N f_s}{2} = N W$$
> where we used $f_s = 2W$ (Nyquist rate). This is the **classic KTU derivation**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bandwidth Efficiency Trade-off:** TDM-PAM needs $N$ times the bandwidth of a single PAM signal, but transmits $N$ times the information. Net: no spectral waste.
- **Cost:** A single high-quality channel is cheaper than N parallel channels. This is why TDM dominates **backhaul telecom** and **data acquisition systems**.
- **Flexibility:** Adding a new channel = increasing $N$ and reducing $\tau$. No frequency re-planning needed (unlike FDM).
- **Foundation for PCM:** TDM-PAM is the **direct precursor to TDM-PCM** (used in T1/E1 digital trunks). The only difference is that PCM quantizes the PAM pulse heights into binary codes before TDM.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Transmission Bandwidth of TDM-PAM

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 7-mark):**
*"A TDM system multiplexes 4 voice channels, each bandlimited to 4 kHz. Find the minimum transmission bandwidth of the multiplexed PAM signal."*

### Step 1: Identify the message bandwidth
Each voice channel has:
$$
W = 4 \text{ kHz}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Nyquist sampling criterion
To avoid aliasing, each channel must be sampled at:
$$
f_s \geq 2W = 2 \times 4 = 8 \text{ kHz}
$$

For **minimum bandwidth**, we choose the equality:
$$
f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the composite pulse rate
The TDM commutator switches through all **N = 4** channels per frame, producing pulses at a rate:
$$
f_{TDM} = N \cdot f_s = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ kHz (pulses per second)}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Nyquist's first criterion (zero-ISI)
For a baseband PAM signal with ideal rectangular pulses of width $\tau$, the minimum transmission bandwidth required to pass the fundamental spectral component is:
$$
B_{TDM} \geq \frac{f_{TDM}}{2}
$$

### Step 5: Substitute
$$
B_{TDM} \geq \frac{N \cdot f_s}{2} = \frac{4 \times 8}{2} = 16 \text{ kHz}
$$

### Step 6: General formula
$$
\boxed{B_{TDM} = N W \text{ (Hz)}}
$$

For this problem, the answer is **$B_{TDM} = 16$ kHz**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always state **"minimum bandwidth"** — the question is incomplete if you don't clarify this. Some problems use raised-cosine pulses, in which case $B = N f_s$ (factor of 2 higher). Read the problem carefully.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Composite TDM-PAM Signal Equation

**Starting Point:** A single PAM signal for the $k$-th channel is:
$$
s_k(t) = m_k(t) \cdot p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m_k(nT_s) \cdot p(t - nT_s)
$$

where $p(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ and height 1.

**TDM Operation:** A commutator with switching function $c_k(t)$ (a periodic gate that is "on" only during the $k$-th slot of each frame) selects channel $k$ for time $\tau$ every $T_f$ seconds.

The switching function is:
$$
c_k(t) = 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{for } (k-1)\tau \leq t \mod T_f < k\tau \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Composite Multiplexed Signal:**
$$
s_{TDM}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c_k(t) \cdot s_k(t)
$$

Substituting:
$$
s_{TDM}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} c_k(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} m_k(nT_s) \, p(t - nT_s)
$$

Since the commutator is synchronous, the $k$-th sample of channel $k$ appears in slot $k$, giving:
$$
s_{TDM}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} m_k(kT_f + (k-1)\tau) \, p\bigl(t - (k-1)T_f\bigr)
$$

In compact form:
$$
\boxed{s_{TDM}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} m_k\bigl(kT_f\bigr) \cdot p(t - kT_s)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Frame Time with Guard Band

**Given:** N channels, per-channel sampling interval $T_s$, guard time $T_g$ between adjacent slots.

**Step 1:** Time occupied by pulses per frame:
$$
T_{pulses} = N \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 2:** Time occupied by guard bands per frame (one before each slot, including the SYNC slot):
$$
T_{guards} = (N+1) \cdot T_g
$$

**Step 3:** Total frame time:
$$
\boxed{T_f = N T_s + (N+1) T_g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the guard time is symmetric and small ($T_g \ll T_s$), the KTU board often accepts the approximation $T_f \approx N T_s$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Variants

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth** | $N, W$ | $B_{TDM}$ | $B_{TDM} = N W$ |
| **Frame Rate** | $N, f_s$ | $f_f$ | $f_f = f_s / N$ |
| **Channel Capacity** | $B, W$ | Max $N$ | $N = B/W$ |
| **Pulse Width** | $T_s, N$ | Max $\tau$ | $\tau = T_s / N$ |
| **Crosstalk** | $B, T_s$ | Isolation (dB) | $\propto e^{-2\pi B T_s}$ |
| **Efficiency** | $\tau, T_f, N$ | $\eta$ | $\eta = N\tau/T_f$ |
| **Sync pulse count** | $N$ | SYNC slots/frame | 1 (typically) |

---

## 3.5 Sample Numerical Walkthrough (KTU 14-mark style)

**Problem:** A TDM-PAM system multiplexes **12 channels**, each bandlimited to **3.5 kHz**. A guard time of **10 μs** is inserted between successive samples. Find:
(a) The minimum sampling rate per channel.
(b) The frame time.
(c) The minimum transmission bandwidth.
(d) The maximum pulse width.

### Solution:

**(a) Sampling Rate per Channel:**
By Nyquist criterion:
$$
f_s = 2W = 2 \times 3500 = 7000 \text{ Hz} = 7 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(b) Frame Time:**
Sampling interval per channel:
$$
T_s = 1/f_s = 1/7000 \approx 142.86 \text{ μs}
$$

With guard time $T_g = 10$ μs and $N = 12$:
$$
T_f = N T_s + (N+1) T_g
$$

$$
T_f = 12 \times 142.86 + 13 \times 10
$$

$$
T_f = 1714.32 + 130 = 1844.32 \text{ μs} \approx 1.844 \text{ ms}
$$

**(c) Minimum Transmission Bandwidth:**
$$
B_{TDM} = \frac{N f_s}{2} = \frac{12 \times 7000}{2} = 42000 \text{ Hz} = 42 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(d) Maximum Pulse Width:**
For no overlap, the pulse must fit within one slot (between guards):
$$
\tau_{max} = T_s - T_g = 142.86 - 10 = 132.86 \text{ μs}
$$

Or using the conservative KTU formula:
$$
\tau_{max} = T_s / N = 142.86 / 12 \approx 11.9 \text{ μs}
$$

*(The exact interpretation depends on whether the guard time is between **all** samples or only between **channels**. KTU questions often state this explicitly — read carefully!)*

```mermaid
    clk1[Master Clock: fs Hz] --> pam1

    txfilt --> chan[Transmission Channel: Coaxial or RF Link]
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    chan[Incoming TDM PAM Waveform] --> rxamp[Receiver Amplifier and Equalizer]
    rxamp --> syncDet[SYNC Detector and PLL]

    rxamp --> decommut
    decommut --> chan1out[Channel 1 PAM Stream]
    decommut --> chan2out[Channel 2 PAM Stream]
    decommut --> chan3out[Channel 3 PAM Stream]
    decommut --> chan4out[Channel 4 PAM Stream]
    chan1out --> lpf1[LPF: W Hz Cutoff]
    chan2out --> lpf2[LPF: W Hz Cutoff]
    chan3out --> lpf3[LPF: W Hz Cutoff]
    chan4out --> lpf4[LPF: W Hz Cutoff]
    lpf1 --> out1[Output: m1 hat of t]
    lpf2 --> out2[Output: m2 hat of t]
    lpf3 --> out3[Output: m3 hat of t]
    lpf4 --> out4[Output: m4 hat of t]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Complete System View (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([N Independent Message Sources]) --> pamStage[PAM Modulation Stage: Pulse Train Sampling]
    pamStage --> tdmStage[TDM Multiplexing Stage: Commutator Interleaving]
    tdmStage --> chanStage[Channel Transmission Stage: Bandlimited Medium]
    chanStage --> syncStage[Receiver SYNC and Clock Recovery]
    syncStage --> demuxStage[De Commutator: Time Slot Disassembly]
    demuxStage --> reconStage[Reconstruction: Low Pass Filtering]
    reconStage --> end([N Reconstructed Message Outputs])
    start -.->|"Each source Wi Hz wide"| pamStage
    tdmStage -.->|"Slot duration tau, Frame Tf"| chanStage
    chanStage -.->|"Crosstalk if BW insufficient"| syncStage
    syncStage -.->|"PLL locks frame timing"| demuxStage
```

## 4.4 TDM Frame Structure — Time-Slot Allocation (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
graph LR
    f1[Frame 1] --> g1a[Guard]
    g1a --> s1a[Ch1 Sample]
    s1a --> g1b[Guard]
    g1b --> s2a[Ch2 Sample]
    s2a --> g1c[Guard]
    g1c --> s3a[Ch3 Sample]
    s3a --> g1d[Guard]
    g1d --> s4a[Ch4 Sample]
    s4a --> g1e[Guard]
    g1e --> synca[SYNC Pulse]
    synca --> f2[Frame 2 repeats]
```

## 4.5 Waveform Schematic (Descriptive)

The TDM-PAM composite waveform exhibits the following visual characteristics:

```
Amplitude
   |    ┌─┐          ┌─┐                  ┌─┐
   |    │ │   ┌─┐    │ │   ┌─┐            │ │
   |  ┌─┘ │   │ │  ┌─┘ │   │ │   ┌─┐    ┌─┘ │
   |  │   │   │ │  │   │   │ │   │ │SYNC│   │
   |──┴───┴───┴─┴──┴───┴───┴─┴───┴─┴────┴───┴──── Time
      Ch1  Ch2  Ch3  Ch4  Ch1  Ch2  Ch3  Ch4
       ←── Frame 1 ──→  ←─── Frame 2 ────→
      ← Tf = N*Ts + guards + SYNC →
```

**Key Observations from the waveform:**
- **Pulse heights** within each frame are **unequal** — they track the instantaneous amplitudes of the four different message signals.
- **Time slots** are **equal width** — guard intervals may be visible as small flat gaps.
- **SYNC pulse** is typically at a **distinguishing amplitude** (e.g., full-scale) so the receiver can identify frame boundaries.
- **Periodicity:** The entire pattern repeats every $T_f$ seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note for First-Time Learners:** The TDM waveform **does not resemble any individual message signal** — it is an interleaved composite. The reconstruction filters at the receiver "smooth out" the pulses back into continuous waveforms.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Conceptual)

### Question 1: Define Time Division Multiplexing. Mention its main advantage over FDM.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) is a multiplexing technique in which several independent signals are transmitted over a common channel by allocating to each signal a **distinct time slot** within a recurrent frame, such that the signals occupy the channel sequentially rather than simultaneously.

**Main Advantage over FDM (1.5 marks):**
Unlike Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM), which requires **sharp bandpass filters** to separate channels and is susceptible to **intermodulation distortion** and **guard bands in frequency**, TDM requires only **time-domain switching** and **synchronized low-pass filters** at the receiver. This makes TDM:
- **Cheaper to implement** (no precision RF filters).
- **Free from cross-talk due to nonlinearities** (crosstalk is purely timing-based).
- **Easier to reconfigure** — adding/removing a channel does not require frequency re-planning.
- **Compatible with digital processing** (the natural precursor to TDM-PCM and TDM-ΔM).

---

### Question 2: What is the role of a SYNC pulse in a TDM-PAM system?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A SYNC (synchronization) pulse is a **distinctive marker pulse** of predefined amplitude and position, inserted once per frame at the transmitter, used by the receiver to identify the start of each frame.

**Roles (2 marks):**
1. **Frame Alignment:** The SYNC pulse marks the boundary of each TDM frame so the receiver de-commutator knows "where frame 1 begins."
2. **Clock Recovery:** The Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) at the receiver locks its local oscillator to the repetition rate of the SYNC pulses, ensuring the de-commutator switches in lockstep with the transmitter.
3. **Phase Synchronization:** The SYNC pulse carries a unique signature (e.g., maximum amplitude, or a specific code) that distinguishes it from data pulses, preventing the receiver from confusing data with synchronization.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 📘 QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of a **TDM-PAM system** multiplexing **three message signals**. State clearly the role of the commutator and the synchronization mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** A TDM system handles **8 voice channels**, each bandlimited to **3.4 kHz**. A guard time of **5 μs** is provided between successive samples. Determine:
   (i) The minimum sampling rate per channel.
   (ii) The frame duration.
   (iii) The minimum transmission bandwidth.
   (iv) The maximum permissible pulse width. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) [7 marks]

**Transmitter Block Diagram (2 marks):**
[Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram]

The transmitter consists of:
- **Three message sources** $m_1(t), m_2(t), m_3(t)$, each bandlimited to $W$ Hz.
- **Three PAM modulators** (one per channel), each multiplying the message by a pulse train of period $T_s = 1/f_s$ where $f_s \geq 2W$.
- A **master clock** at frequency $f_s$ Hz driving the PAM modulators and the commutator.
- An **electronic commutator** (3-position rotary switch) that sequentially connects each PAM modulator's output to the common transmission line.
- A **SYNC pulse generator** that inserts a synchronization marker at the end (or beginning) of each frame.
- A **channel filter and line driver** for transmission.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
The commutator rotates at frame rate $f_f = f_s/3$. At any instant, only one channel is connected to the output. After dwelling on each input for a duration $\tau$ (the pulse width), the switch moves to the next input. After cycling through all 3 channels, a complete frame of duration $T_f = 3T_s$ is transmitted. The pattern repeats indefinitely.

**Receiver Block Diagram (1.5 marks):**
[Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram]

The receiver consists of:
- A **front-end amplifier and equalizer** to compensate for channel losses.
- A **SYNC detector and PLL** that extracts the master clock from the incoming waveform.
- A **synchronized de-commutator** (3-position switch in lockstep with the transmitter) that routes each time slot to the correct output.
- **Three low-pass reconstruction filters** (cutoff $W$ Hz) to recover the original messages.

**Role of Synchronization (1.5 marks):**
- SYNC pulse marks frame boundaries.
- PLL recovers the clock, ensuring receiver commutator rotates in **phase and frequency** with transmitter.
- Without synchronization, samples would be routed to wrong channels → **inter-channel crosstalk**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $N = 8$ channels, $W = 3.4$ kHz, $T_g = 5$ μs.

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate (1.5 marks):**
By Nyquist criterion:
$$
f_s = 2W = 2 \times 3.4 = 6.8 \text{ kHz}
$$

Sampling interval:
$$
T_s = 1/f_s = 1/6800 \approx 147.06 \text{ μs}
$$

**(ii) Frame Duration (2 marks):**
With N+1 = 9 guard intervals per frame:
$$
T_f = N \cdot T_s + (N+1) \cdot T_g
$$

$$
T_f = 8 \times 147.06 + 9 \times 5
$$

$$
T_f = 1176.47 + 45 = 1221.47 \text{ μs} \approx 1.221 \text{ ms}
$$

**(iii) Minimum Transmission Bandwidth (2 marks):**
$$
B_{TDM} = \frac{N \cdot f_s}{2} = \frac{8 \times 6800}{2} = 27200 \text{ Hz} = 27.2 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(iv) Maximum Pulse Width (1.5 marks):**
For non-overlapping pulses within each slot:
$$
\tau_{max} = T_s - T_g = 147.06 - 5 = 142.06 \text{ μs}
$$

(Or conservatively, $\tau_{max} = T_s / N = 147.06 / 8 \approx 18.4$ μs — depends on problem interpretation.)

**Final Answer Summary:**
- $f_s = 6.8$ kHz
- $T_f \approx 1.221$ ms
- $B_{TDM} = 27.2$ kHz
- $\tau_{max} \approx 142$ μs

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary, Module 3]**

**Q.B (a)** Discuss the sources of **crosstalk in a TDM-PAM system**. Explain how the transmission bandwidth and the pulse width affect crosstalk. Derive the relationship between channel bandwidth and pulse width to minimize crosstalk. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Compare **TDM and FDM** in terms of bandwidth utilization, crosstalk susceptibility, implementation complexity, and suitability for digital systems. A TDM-PAM system transmits **24 channels** of **4 kHz bandwidth** each. Calculate the **minimum channel bandwidth required** and the **frame rate**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) [7 marks]

**Sources of Crosstalk (2 marks):**
1. **Insufficient channel bandwidth:** If the channel's bandwidth $B$ is too small, the rectangular PAM pulses get **spread out** (low-pass filtering of a step pulse produces a slowly decaying exponential), and their tails **leak into adjacent time slots**, causing inter-channel interference.
2. **Switching transients:** The finite rise and fall times of the commutator switches cause **ringing and overshoot** that contaminate adjacent slots.
3. **Timing jitter:** Instability in the receiver's clock causes the sampling instants to drift, capturing a fraction of the adjacent pulse.
4. **Imperfect SYNC recovery:** A misaligned de-commutator routes samples to wrong channels.

**Effect of Bandwidth (1.5 marks):**
Crosstalk amplitude decays exponentially with $B \cdot T_s$:
$$
C \propto e^{-\pi B T_s}
$$
To reduce crosstalk by a factor of 10, the bandwidth must increase by approximately:
$$
B_{new} = B_{old} + \frac{\ln 10}{\pi T_s}
$$

**Effect of Pulse Width (1.5 marks):**
Crosstalk increases with pulse width $\tau$ because wider pulses have less spectral spread, so more of the pulse energy lies in the lower-frequency band that the channel can pass. Narrower pulses have wider spectrum → more low-frequency attenuation → less crosstalk. Hence, **smaller $\tau$ is better** for crosstalk, but **smaller $\tau$ means more sensitive to jitter** (trade-off).

**Derivation: Bandwidth for Negligible Crosstalk (2 marks):**
For a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$, the spectrum is a sinc function. To preserve the pulse shape and avoid inter-symbol interference, the channel's impulse response must be much shorter than the slot duration $T_s/N$. A common rule of thumb (used in KTU):
$$
B \geq \frac{N}{2\tau}
$$
Equivalently:
$$
\boxed{\tau \leq \frac{N}{2B}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | TDM | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| **Resource Divided** | Time slots | Frequency bands |
| **Bandwidth Utilization** | More efficient (no frequency guard bands needed) | Less efficient (guard bands waste spectrum) |
| **Crosstalk Susceptibility** | Mainly from finite BW and timing jitter | From filter nonlinearity, intermodulation |
| **Implementation Complexity** | Synchronized switches, easier in digital domain | Sharp bandpass filters, more analog hardware |
| **Suitability for Digital** | Excellent — natural for PCM and digital switching | Poor — requires mod/demod and A/D conversion |
| **Flexibility** | Easy to add/remove channels | Requires re-planning frequency allocation |
| **Noise Immunity** | Better (digital-friendly) | Worse (analog accumulation) |
| **Cost** | Lower (single channel) | Higher (per-channel filters) |

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given: $N = 24$, $W = 4$ kHz.

**Minimum Channel Bandwidth:**
$$
B_{TDM} = N \cdot W = 24 \times 4 = 96 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Frame Rate:**
Per-channel sampling rate (Nyquist): $f_s = 2W = 8$ kHz.

Frame rate = per-channel sampling rate:
$$
f_f = f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $B_{TDM} = 96$ kHz
- $f_f = 8$ kHz

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on TDM-PAM questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to state "minimum" bandwidth.** KTU often awards 0.5 marks for the word "minimum." Always say **"minimum transmission bandwidth = N·f_s/2"**.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up $f_s$ (sampling rate) and $f_f$ (frame rate).** $f_s$ is the rate at which ONE channel is sampled. $f_f$ is the rate at which frames repeat. They are related by $f_f = f_s / N$. Many students incorrectly equate them.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring the SYNC pulse and guard time in frame calculations.** When $T_g$ is given, you MUST use $T_f = N T_s + (N+1)T_g$. Many students write $T_f = N T_s$ and lose 2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without labeling.** The KTU examiner allocates **at least 1–2 marks** for **labeled block diagrams**. Always annotate: "Commutator," "PAM Modulator," "SYNC Generator," "LPF." An unlabeled diagram = 0 marks for that component.
> 
> 5. **Confusing PAM with PCM.** TDM-PAM transmits analog pulse heights. TDM-PCM transmits digital codes. The 14-mark question will specify which — read carefully.
> 
> 6. **No mention of Nyquist criterion.** Every definition of TDM-PAM should cite **"based on the Nyquist sampling theorem, $f_s \geq 2W$."** Omitting this = -1 mark.
> 
> 7. **Skipping the role of synchronization.** Even a 7-mark question expects at least 2–3 lines on WHY synchronization is essential. Saying only "SYNC pulse is added" is incomplete.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Time Division Multiplexing with PAM**
> 
> - **TDM Definition:** Multiplexing by allocating distinct **time slots** within a recurrent frame.
> - **PAM Foundation:** Each channel is first pulse-amplitude-modulated, then interleaved.
> - **Nyquist Sampling:** $f_s \geq 2W$ Hz per channel.
> - **Frame Time:** $T_f = N T_s + (N+1) T_g$ (with guard) or $N T_s$ (without).
> - **Frame Rate:** $f_f = f_s / N$ (always equals per-channel sampling rate when no guard).
> - **Minimum Bandwidth:** $B_{TDM} = N W$ Hz (or $N f_s / 2$).
> - **Pulse Width:** $\tau \leq T_s / N$ (no overlap) or $\tau \leq T_s - T_g$ (with guard).
> - **Commutator:** Rotating switch (TX) — selects one channel per time slot.
> - **De-commutator:** Synchronized switch (RX) — separates composite back to channels.
> - **SYNC Pulse:** Marks frame boundary, enables PLL clock recovery.
> - **Crosstalk:** Caused by **insufficient channel bandwidth**, **timing jitter**, **switching transients**.
> - **Crosstalk Mitigation:** Increase $B$, reduce $\tau$, add $T_g$, use stable PLL.
> - **Efficiency:** $\eta = N\tau / T_f$ (closer to 1 is better, but constrained by crosstalk).
> - **TDM vs FDM:** TDM is **time-domain**, FDM is **frequency-domain**; TDM is **digital-friendly**, FDM is **analog-traditional**.
> - **Key Advantage of TDM:** No cross-modulation, easier digital conversion, scalable.
> - **Real-World Example:** T1 carrier (1.544 Mbps, 24 channels), E1 carrier (2.048 Mbps, 30 channels).
> - **Block Diagram Essentials:** Always label commutator, SYNC, LPF, clock.
> - **Forgotten keywords that cost marks:** "Nyquist," "synchronized," "time-interleaved," "frame," "guard time."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) # Pulse Code Modulation (PCM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal KTU 2024 Definition **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the most fundamental and widely deployed form of **digital pulse modulation** in which an analog message signal is represented, transmitted, and reconstructed as a sequence of **binary-coded pulses**. The system sequentially performs three irreversible (lossy in amplitude, lossless in time) operations: 1. **Sampling** – Conversion of the continuous-time, continuous-amplitude signal into a discrete-time, continuous-amplitude signal (PAM pulses). 2. **Quantization** – Conversion of the continuous-amplitude samples into a discrete set of finite amplitude levels. 3. **Encoding** – Conversion of each quantized level into a unique **n-bit binary word** (typically Natural Binary Code / NBC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students must clearly distinguish between *PAM* (analog pulse amplitude modulation — sampled but not quantized) and *PCM* (truly digital — both sampled AND quantized AND coded). Examiners frequently test this conceptual boundary. ### Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter System" Imagine you want to send a handwritten photograph of a sunset to a friend living far away, but you cannot send the actual picture. The PCM equivalent is: | Step in Analogy | PCM Operation | |---|---| | You glance at the photo once every second (you cannot send it continuously) | **Sampling** at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | | You round the colour to the nearest of only **256 standard shades** because humans cannot perceive finer differences | **Quantization** into $L = 2^n$ levels | | You describe each shade using an **8-digit code** (e.g., RGB: 11010010) and send only the codes by SMS | **Encoding** to n-bit binary | The friend, upon receiving 64 such coded SMS messages (one per second for a minute), reconstructs a near-identical digital copy. The small colour mismatch from rounding is the **quantization error**. ### Key Standard Metrics (Bold) - **Nyquist Sampling Rate: $f_s = 2 f_m$** (where $f_m$ is the maximum message frequency). - **Quantization Levels: $L = 2^n$** (n = number of bits per sample). - **Standard Audio Sampling: $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$** (telephony), **44.1 kHz** (CD audio). - **Quantization Resolution (step size): $q = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{L}$**. > [!NOTE] > The **voice band** in telephony is standardized at **300 Hz – 3400 Hz**, giving $f_m = 3.4 \text{ kHz}$ and a Nyquist rate of **6.8 kHz** (practically 8 kHz). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Pipeline (Three Sequential Stages) ### Stage 1 — Sampling - The continuous-time analog signal $m(t)$ of bandwidth $W$ is multiplied by a periodic train of narrow rectangular pulses. - Output is a **PAM signal** $m_s(t)$ whose amplitude still varies continuously. - Governed by the **Sampling Theorem**: To avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. - Two practical implementations: - **Natural Sampling (Flat-top of pulse tracks the analog curve)**: aperture error present. - **Flat-top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold)**: pulse height constant over pulse width $\tau$; aperture error $\approx \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\tau}{T_s}\right)^2 \cdot m'(t)$. ### Stage 2 — Quantization - Maps each sample amplitude onto one of $L$ discrete levels. - **Quantization Step:** $q = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{L - 1} \approx \dfrac{V_{pp}}{L}$ (for large $L$). - **Quantization Error** $e(t)$ lies uniformly in $[-q/2, +q/2]$ (for mid-rise uniform quantizer). - **Quantization Noise Power:** $\sigma_q^2 = \dfrac{q^2}{12}$ (variance of uniform distribution). - **Types:** Uniform (linear) and Non-uniform (logarithmic — companded). ### Stage 3 — Encoding - Each quantized level $q_k$ is assigned a unique **n-bit binary word**. - Common codes: Natural Binary Code (NBC), Gray code (for reducing bit-error impact). - Output bit stream is **baseband digital**, ready for line coding (NRZ, RZ, Manchester). ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless | $n$ = bits/sample | | 2 | Quantization step size | $q = V_{pp} / L$ | Volts | Linear quantizer, mid-rise | | 3 | Quantization noise power | $\sigma_q^2 = q^2 / 12$ | $W$ (Watts) | Assumes uniform distribution | | 4 | SQNR (sinusoidal, uniform) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right) = 3 \cdot 2^{2n}$ | dimensionless | Linear power ratio | | 5 | SQNR in dB (sinusoidal) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | dB | Most-used exam formula | | 6 | SQNR in dB (random voice) | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n - 4.77$ | dB | Voice signal: $S/\sigma^2 = V_{pp}^2 / 8$ | | 7 | PCM Bit Rate | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/sec | After encoding | | 8 | Transmission BW (NRZ polar) | $B_T = R_b / 2$ | Hz | Minimum Nyquist BW | | 9 | Transmission BW (unipolar NRZ) | $B_T = R_b$ | Hz | Contains DC | | 10 | Companded SQNR | $\left(\dfrac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 - \alpha$ | dB | $\alpha \approx 0$ for $\mu = 5$ A-law; $\alpha = 0$ for $\mu$-law ideal | | 11 | A-law compression | $y = \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A},\ 0 \leq \vert x \vert \leq 1/A$ | — | $A = 87.6$ in Europe (ITU-T) | | 12 | $\mu$-law compression | $y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ | — | $\mu = 255$ in US/Japan | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder:** For sinusoidal input, the peak factor reduces the available signal power by 2 (since average power of a sinusoid of peak $V_p$ is $V_p^2/2$, but $V_{pp} = 2V_p$). This yields the famous **1.76 dB bonus** term in formula #5 above. Always verify whether the question says *sinusoidal* or *uniform random* signal. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telephony backbone (TDM-PCM):** 8-bit, 8 kHz sampling → **64 kbps DS0 channel** (the fundamental unit of the entire PSTN digital hierarchy — E1 = 2.048 Mbps, T1 = 1.544 Mbps). - **Digital Audio Broadcasting, CDs, MP3, VoIP, 5G voice (VoNR):** all inherit PCM's encoding philosophy. - **Secure military & satellite links** use PCM due to its ease of encryption and regeneration. - **Instrumentation & telemetry** where bit-error resilience (via Gray coding) and regeneration matter. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Quantization Noise Power **Assumption:** Quantization error $e = m_q - m$ is uniformly distributed between $-q/2$ and $+q/2$ (valid for high-resolution quantizers where input distribution is much wider than $q$). $$ \begin{aligned} p(e) &= \frac{1}{q}, \quad -\frac{q}{2} \leq e \leq \frac{q}{2} \\ \sigma_q^2 &= E[e^2] = \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \, p(e)\, de \\ &= \frac{1}{q} \int_{-q/2}^{+q/2} e^2 \, de \\ &= \frac{1}{q} \left[ \frac{e^3}{3} \right]_{-q/2}^{+q/2} \\ &= \frac{1}{q} \left( \frac{(q/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-q/2)^3}{3} \right) \\ &= \frac{1}{q} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot q^3}{24} = \frac{q^2}{12} \end{aligned} $$ **Interpretation:** Quantization noise is a *constant* $\dfrac{q^2}{12}$ Watts that depends **only on the step size $q$**, not on the signal power. Therefore, increasing the signal amplitude increases SNR linearly. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) — Sinusoidal Input **Given:** Input $m(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)$, peak amplitude $V_p$, peak-to-peak $V_{pp} = 2V_p$. **Step 1 — Compute Signal Power:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &= \frac{V_p^2}{2} = \frac{(V_{pp}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{8} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $q = V_{pp}/L = V_{pp}/2^n$:** $$ \begin{aligned} N_q &= \sigma_q^2 = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Form the Ratio:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{S}{N_q} &= \frac{V_{pp}^2 / 8}{V_{pp}^2 / (12 \cdot 4^n)} \\ &= \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 4^n \\ &= 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n} = 3 \cdot 2^{2n - 2} \end{aligned} $$ In the cleaner, universally cited form: $$ \frac{S}{N_q} = 3 \cdot 2^{2n} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to dB:** $$ \begin{aligned} \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} &= 10 \log_{10}\left(3 \cdot 2^{2n}\right) \\ &= 10 \log_{10} 3 + 20 n \log_{10} 2 \\ &= 4.77 + 20 n \times 0.3010 \\ &= 4.77 + 6.02\,n \\ \end{aligned} $$ Rearranging to the more familiar examiner form (and noting some textbooks write the constant as 1.76 since $\log_{10}(1.5) = 0.176$ when peak-to-peak convention is used): $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76 \quad \text{(sinusoidal, full-scale)} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mark-bearing insight:** Every additional bit ($n \to n+1$) increases SQNR by **6.02 dB** — exactly one "effective bit" worth of dynamic range. This rule-of-thumb is heavily tested. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Typical KTU Calculation **Problem:** A PCM system uses $n = 8$ bits/sample to encode a voice signal of $f_m = 4$ kHz. Compute (a) sampling rate, (b) bit rate, (c) minimum transmission bandwidth (NRZ polar), (d) SQNR for sinusoidal input. **Solution:** **(a) Sampling Rate** — Apply Nyquist with 25% guard band: $$ f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ samples/sec} = 8 \text{ kHz} $$ **(b) Bit Rate:** $$ R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64000 \text{ bits/sec} = 64 \text{ kbps} $$ **(c) Minimum Transmission Bandwidth (NRZ polar):** $$ B_T = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64000}{2} = 32000 \text{ Hz} = 32 \text{ kHz} $$ **(d) SQNR (sinusoidal):** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02(8) + 1.76 = 48.16 + 1.76 = 49.92 \text{ dB} $$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PCM System — Block-Level Functional Architecture The block diagram below captures the full signal chain — from analog source to reconstructed analog output — and is the single most important figure for KTU Module 3. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Analog Input
m of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing
LPF] B --> C[Sampler
Switch] C --> D[Sample and
Hold] D --> E[Quantizer
L levels] E --> F[Binary
Encoder] F --> G[Line Coder
NRZ RZ] G --> H[Channel
plus Noise] H --> I[Regenerative
Repeater] I --> J[Line
Decoder] J --> K[Binary
Decoder] K --> L[Reconstruction
LPF] L --> M[Analog Output
m hat of t] ``` ### Functional Description of Each Block | Block | Purpose | |---|---| | Anti-aliasing LPF | Restricts $m(t)$ to bandwidth $W$ to prevent aliasing | | Sampler | Produces PAM pulses at $f_s \geq 2W$ | | Sample-and-Hold | Holds voltage constant during quantization (avoids aperture error) | | Quantizer | Maps amplitude to one of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels | | Binary Encoder | Generates n-bit codeword (NBC / Gray) | | Line Coder | Converts binary to baseband waveform (NRZ, RZ, Manchester, AMI) | | Channel | Physical medium — introduces noise, ISI, attenuation | | Regenerative Repeater | Reshapes & retimes pulses → cleans noise | | Reconstruction LPF | Smooths decoded PAM back to analog | ## 4.2 Quantization Characteristics (Mid-Rise Type) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[Continuous Amplitude Domain] P1[Sample Value V in] end subgraph PROC[Quantization Decision] Q1{Round to
nearest level} L0[L0 equals 0] L1[L1] L2[L2] L3[L3] LN[Ln minus 1] end subgraph OUT[Discrete Codeword Output] C0[Code 000] C1[Code 001] C2[Code 010] C3[Code 011] CN[Code 111] end P1 --> Q1 Q1 --> L0 --> C0 Q1 --> L1 --> C1 Q1 --> L2 --> C2 Q1 --> L3 --> C3 Q1 --> LN --> CN ``` ## 4.3 Companding Process (A-law / μ-law) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Uniform PCM
12 bit Linear] --> B[Compressor
Logarithmic] B --> C[Channel
8 bit Nonlinear] C --> D[Expander
Inverse Log] D --> E[Reconstructed
12 bit Linear] style B fill:#ffe5b4 style D fill:#b4d8ff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Companding **does not change the bit rate**. It only redistributes quantization levels so that weak signals (which dominate human speech) get *finer* resolution, while strong signals get *coarser* resolution. Net effect: **constant SQNR across a 40 dB dynamic range** with only 8 bits — the same SQNR that would require 12+ bits of uniform quantization. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **What is Pulse Code Modulation? Compare it with PAM.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **PCM** is a digital modulation scheme in which an analog signal is sampled, quantized, and encoded into binary pulses for transmission. **(1 mark)** - The three operations are: (i) **Sampling** at $f_s \geq 2f_m$, (ii) **Quantization** into $L = 2^n$ discrete levels, (iii) **Encoding** each level into an n-bit binary codeword. **(1 mark)** - **PAM vs PCM:** PAM transmits analog pulse amplitudes (continuous amplitude, discrete time); PCM transmits only binary pulses (both discrete amplitude and discrete time). PCM provides noise immunity, easy regeneration, encryption compatibility, and is the foundation of TDM digital telephony, while PAM does not. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State and explain the Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited signal $m(t)$ of maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**. **(1.5 marks)** - The reconstruction uses an ideal **low-pass filter** of bandwidth $f_m$ following the sample stream. **(0.5 mark)** - **Aliasing:** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas of the sampled signal overlap, and high-frequency components irreversibly distort into lower frequencies, making faithful reconstruction impossible. It is prevented by an **anti-aliasing LPF** before sampling. **(1 mark)** --- ## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** Draw the block diagram of a PCM system and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for **signal-to-quantization-noise ratio** of a PCM system for a sinusoidal input.
