Show that every additional bit improves SQNR by approximately **6 dB**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — Block Diagram & Functions **(7 marks)** **Block Diagram (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete architecture):** The PCM **transmitter** consists of: (i) Anti-aliasing LPF, (ii) Sampler, (iii) Sample-and-Hold circuit, (iv) Quantizer, (v) Binary encoder, (vi) Line coder. The **receiver** performs the reverse operations: line decoder, binary decoder, reconstruction LPF. **(2 marks — diagram)** **Functions (each block 0.5–1 mark):** 1. **Anti-aliasing LPF** — Limits input bandwidth to $W$ so that $f_s \geq 2W$ becomes feasible; prevents aliasing. 2. **Sampler** — Generates PAM pulses at $f_s \geq 2W$ (Nyquist criterion). 3. **Sample-and-Hold** — Holds sample value steady during quantization interval; reduces aperture error. 4. **Quantizer** — Rounds each sample to one of $L = 2^n$ levels; introduces irreversible quantization error. 5. **Binary Encoder** — Maps each level to an n-bit codeword (NBC / Gray). 6. **Line Coder** — Converts binary sequence to baseband transmission waveform (NRZ, RZ, Manchester, AMI). 7. **Channel** — Physical medium introducing attenuation, noise, ISI. 8. **Regenerative Repeater** — Periodically cleans & retimes pulses; eliminates accumulated noise. 9. **Decoder & Reconstruction LPF** — Regenerates PAM and smooths it back to the analog message $\hat{m}(t)$. **(5 marks)** #### Model Solution for (b) — SQNR Derivation **(7 marks)** **Given:** Sinusoidal input $m(t) = V_p \sin(2\pi f_m t)$, $L = 2^n$ levels. **Step 1:** Signal power (average): $$ S = \frac{V_p^2}{2} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{8} $$ **Step 2:** Step size $q = V_{pp}/L = V_{pp}/2^n$ **Step 3:** Quantization noise power (uniform error in $\pm q/2$): $$ N_q = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{V_{pp}^2}{12 \cdot 4^n} $$ **Step 4:** SQNR (ratio): $$ \frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{V_{pp}^2/8}{V_{pp}^2/(12 \cdot 4^n)} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n} $$ **Step 5:** In dB: $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(1.5 \cdot 4^n) = 10\log_{10} 1.5 + 40n\log_{10} 2 $$ $$ \Rightarrow \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n $$ **Step 6 — 6 dB per bit rule:** Incrementing $n \to n+1$: $$ \Delta = 6.02(n+1) + 1.76 - (6.02n + 1.76) = 6.02 \text{ dB} \approx 6 \text{ dB} $$ Hence **each additional bit adds ~6 dB to SQNR** — equivalently, doubles the SQNR power ratio. **(2 marks for the 6 dB proof and interpretation)** --- ### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **companding**. With neat characteristics, describe **A-law** and **μ-law** companding. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PCM system uses a voice signal with $f_m = 4$ kHz, sampled at Nyquist rate with 25% guard band, encoded with 9 bits/sample. Calculate: (i) Sampling frequency, (ii) Bit rate, (iii) Minimum transmission bandwidth (NRZ polar), (iv) Output SQNR (sinusoidal, full-scale), (v) Quantization noise power if $V_{pp} = 4$ V. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) — Companding **(7 marks)** **Definition:** Companding = **Comp**ressing + Exp**anding** — a non-uniform quantization technique where the signal is **logarithmically compressed before uniform quantization at the transmitter** and **expanded inversely at the receiver**. **(1 mark)** **Why:** In voice signals, weak amplitudes occur more frequently than loud ones. Uniform quantization wastes resolution on loud peaks. Companding allocates finer quantization to weak signals, achieving **higher effective SQNR for low-level signals** without increasing the bit rate. **(1 mark)** **A-law (Used in Europe, ITU-T G.711, $A = 87.6$):** $$ y = \begin{cases} \dfrac{A\,|x|}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \leq |x| \leq \dfrac{1}{A} \\[6pt] \dfrac{1 + \ln(A\,|x|)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \leq |x| \leq 1 \end{cases} $$ Continuous, piecewise linear approximation uses **13 segments** (16 total with mirror symmetry) in actual 8-bit codecs. **(2 marks)** **μ-law (Used in US/Japan, $\mu = 255$):** $$ y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu\,|x|)}{\ln(1+\mu)} $$ Approximated using **15 segments** in 8-bit codecs. **(2 marks)** **Comparison Block (refer to Section 4.3 Mermaid diagram for system architecture).** | Parameter | A-law | μ-law | |---|---|---| | Origin | Europe / ITU-T | USA / Japan | | Parameter | A = 87.6 | μ = 255 | | Segments | 13 | 15 | | Continuity at origin | Continuous | Discontinuous (sign function) | | Small-signal behavior | Linear | Linear approximation | **(1 mark for comparison / diagram)** #### Model Solution for (b) — Numerical **(7 marks)** **Given:** $f_m = 4$ kHz, Nyquist + 25% guard band, $n = 9$ bits, $V_{pp} = 4$ V. **(i) Sampling Frequency:** $$ f_s = 2 f_m \times 1.25 = 2 \times 4000 \times 1.25 = 10000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$ **(ii) Bit Rate:** $$ R_b = n \cdot f_s = 9 \times 10000 = 90000 \text{ bps} = 90 \text{ kbps} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$ **(iii) Minimum Transmission Bandwidth (NRZ polar):** $$ B_T = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{90000}{2} = 45000 \text{ Hz} = 45 \text{ kHz} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$ **(iv) Output SQNR (sinusoidal):** $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{dB} = 6.02(9) + 1.76 = 54.18 + 1.76 = 55.94 \text{ dB} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)} $$ **(v) Quantization Noise Power:** Number of levels: $L = 2^9 = 512$ Step size: $$ q = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{4}{512} = 7.8125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} $$ Quantization noise power: $$ N_q = \frac{q^2}{12} = \frac{(7.8125 \times 10^{-3})^2}{12} = \frac{6.104 \times 10^{-5}}{12} \approx 5.086 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!** > > 1. **Confusing SQNR formulas:** Do **NOT** use $6.02n + 1.76$ for voice signals or random inputs. For uniformly distributed voice, the correct formula is $6.02n - 4.77$ dB. Marks deducted: typically **1–2 marks** per error. > 2. **Forgetting the Nyquist guard band:** KTU questions frequently specify "Nyquist rate + 25%." Missing the 1.25 multiplier costs **1 mark**. > 3. **Bandwidth factor confusion:** NRZ polar → $R_b/2$; unipolar NRZ → $R_b$; Manchester → $R_b$. Always state the line coding assumption explicitly. > 4. **Confusing $V_{pp}$ vs $V_p$ in the SQNR derivation:** Use $V_{pp} = 2V_p$ consistently. Mixing them gives a 6 dB error. > 5. **Skipping the "6 dB per bit" comment** in derivations — examiners reward it as the *physical interpretation* of the formula. > 6. **Drawing PAM block diagrams instead of PCM** — a common 2-mark loss. Make sure *quantizer* AND *encoder* boxes are explicitly shown. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **PCM Pipeline (memorize in order):** Anti-alias LPF → Sample → Hold → Quantize → Encode → Line code → Channel → Regenerate → Decode → LPF (reconstruct). - **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (Nyquist rate); aliasing occurs if violated. - **Quantization Step:** $q = V_{pp}/L$ where $L = 2^n$. - **Quantization Noise Power:** $N_q = q^2/12$ — *independent of signal*, depends only on resolution $n$. - **SQNR (sinusoidal):** $(S/N_q)_{dB} = 6.02\,n + 1.76$. - **SQNR (random voice):** $(S/N_q)_{dB} = 6.02\,n - 4.77$. - **Bit Rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$. - **Bandwidth (NRZ polar):** $B_T = R_b/2$. - **6 dB-per-bit rule** — every extra bit doubles the linear SQNR power ratio. - **Companding:** Logarithmic compression before uniform quantization; expands dynamic range without raising bit rate. - **A-law (Europe):** $A = 87.6$, 13 segments, continuous at origin. - **μ-law (USA/Japan):** $\mu = 255$, 15 segments, sign-discontinuous at origin. - **Telephony standard:** $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 8$ bits → **64 kbps DS0 channel** (basis of E1 = 2.048 Mbps = 32 × 64 kbps, T1 = 1.544 Mbps = 24 × 64 kbps). - **Gray coding preferred** for encoded bit stream to minimize impact of single bit errors. - **PAM ≠ PCM** — PAM is analog pulse modulation; PCM is fully digital.

topics.title: A-law and Mu-law Quantization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** A-law and Mu-law Quantization # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In Pulse Code Modulation (PCM), the analog input signal is sampled and quantized into discrete levels before encoding. **Uniform quantization** uses a fixed step size ($\Delta$) across the entire signal range. While simple to implement, it performs poorly for real-world signals such as speech, which exhibit a large dynamic range (ratio between the loudest and softest sounds, often exceeding **40 dB = 10,000:1**). **Companding** (a portmanteau of *compressing* and *expanding*) is the signal-processing technique that overcomes this limitation by applying a **non-linear compression characteristic** at the transmitter, followed by the inverse **expansion** at the receiver. The net effect is equivalent to **non-uniform quantization**, where the step size $\Delta$ is small for low-amplitude signals and large for high-amplitude signals. The two internationally standardized companding laws are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **μ-law (Mu-law):** Adopted in North America and Japan (ITU-T G.711). > $$y = V_{out} = V \cdot \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$$ > Standard value: **$\mu = 255$** > [!IMPORTANT] > **A-law:** Adopted in Europe, India, and most of the world (ITU-T G.711). > $$ > y = V \cdot \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot > \begin{cases} > \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1 + \ln A}, & 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A} \\[8pt] > \dfrac{1 + \ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1 + \ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \le \vert x \vert \le 1 > \end{cases} > $$ > Standard value: **$A = 87.6$** Here, $x = V_{in}/V$ is the normalized input and $y = V_{out}/V$ is the normalized compressor output, both bounded in $[-1, +1]$. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Volume" Microphone Imagine you are recording an orchestra with a single microphone whose sensitivity (gain) is **fixed**: - When the loud cymbals crash, the recording **distorts (clips)** because the signal exceeds the maximum recordable level. - When the soft violin solo plays, the recording sounds **hissy and noisy** because the weak signal is barely above the microphone's inherent noise floor. A **smart microphone** solves this by automatically **turning its gain UP when sound is soft** and **turning its gain DOWN when sound is loud**. This is exactly what the compressor in A-law / μ-law does: | Physical Analogy | Companding System | |---|---| | Quiet violin | Low-amplitude input $\Rightarrow$ High slope region (small $\Delta$) | | Loud cymbals | High-amplitude input $\Rightarrow$ Low slope region (large $\Delta$) | | Microphone gain control | Compressor characteristic $y = f(x)$ | | Listener's volume knob | Expander characteristic $x = f^{-1}(y)$ | The listener at the receiving end never notices the manipulation because the inverse expansion restores the original dynamic range exactly. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Companding does NOT change the signal-to-quantization noise ratio for a fixed RMS input. Instead, it makes the SQNR **nearly constant across a wide dynamic range**, dramatically improving the perceived quality of speech. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | μ-law | A-law | |---|---|---| | Compression parameter | **$\mu = 255$** | **$A = 87.6$** | | Linear segment near origin | None (purely log) | Yes, for $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$ | | Typical dynamic range | ~**40 dB** | ~**40 dB** | | Used in | US, Japan | Europe, India | | ITU Standard | G.711 (μ) | G.711 (A) | | Standard sampling rate | 8 kHz | 8 kHz | | Standard bit rate | 64 kbps | 64 kbps | # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Problem: Why Uniform Quantization Fails In uniform quantization with $L = 2^n$ levels over a peak-to-peak range $\pm V$: $$\Delta = \frac{2V}{L}, \qquad \sigma_q^2 = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$ The signal power is $S_x = V_x^2$. The signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) for a sinusoidal input is: $$\left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{uniform} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2n} = 1.76 + 6.02n \;\; \text{dB}$$ The problem: if the input amplitude drops by a factor of **10** (i.e., -20 dB), the SQNR **also drops by 20 dB**. Soft passages become noisy. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — How Companding Solves the Problem 1. **Compressor (Transmitter):** Maps the wide-range input $x$ into a narrower compressed range $y$ using a logarithmic curve. Low-level signals are amplified more than high-level signals. 2. **Uniform Quantizer:** Quantizes the compressed signal $y$ uniformly. Since $y$ has a smaller dynamic range, a fixed $\Delta$ is now appropriate. 3. **Channel:** Transmits the digital code. 4. **Expander (Receiver):** Applies the inverse characteristic to reconstruct $x$ from $y$. High-level signals are amplified more than low-level signals — restoring the original dynamic range. The combined compressor–quantizer behavior is mathematically equivalent to **non-uniform quantization** with a variable step size: $$\Delta(x) = \frac{\Delta_0}{\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)}$$ where $\Delta_0$ is the uniform step in the $y$-domain. ## 2.3 μ-law Compression Characteristic The derivative of the μ-law curve gives the slope (local gain) of the compressor: $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{\mu}{(1 + \mu \vert x \vert)\ln(1+\mu)}$$ - At $x = 0$ (smallest signal): maximum slope $= \dfrac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ - At $x = 1$ (largest signal): minimum slope $= \dfrac{1}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ This means **small signals are quantized with smaller effective step size**, improving their SQNR, while **large signals are quantized with larger step size**, avoiding overload. ## 2.4 A-law Compression Characteristic For $A = 87.6$, $1/A \approx 0.0114$. - **Linear region** ($0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1/A$): slope $= \dfrac{A}{1+\ln A} \approx 16$ - **Logarithmic region** ($1/A \le \vert x \vert \le 1$): slope decreases continuously from $\dfrac{1}{1+\ln A} \approx \dfrac{1}{16}$ at $\vert x \vert = 1$. > [!NOTE] > **A-law advantage:** Unlike μ-law, A-law has a true linear segment near the origin, which means signals very close to zero are quantized perfectly without distortion — useful for low-frequency tones. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Reference) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ | μ-law compressor | dimensionless | | 2 | $y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{A\vert x \vert}{1+\ln A},\; \vert x \vert \le 1/A$ | A-law linear segment | dimensionless | | 3 | $y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \dfrac{1+\ln(A\vert x \vert)}{1+\ln A},\; \vert x \vert \ge 1/A$ | A-law logarithmic segment | dimensionless | | 4 | $\mu = 255$, $A = 87.6$ | ITU standard parameters | dimensionless | | 5 | $\Delta_i = \Delta_{i-1} \cdot 2$ | Doubling step size per segment | V | | 6 | Segment 0 step size (μ-law) | $\Delta_0 = \dfrac{V}{256 \cdot 16} = \dfrac{V}{4096}$ | V | | 7 | 8-bit PCM code structure | Sign(1) + Segment(3) + Step(4) | bits | | 8 | Number of positive segments | 8 (for both laws) | — | | 9 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^8 = 256$ per polarity (512 total) | levels | | 10 | $\Delta_0 = \dfrac{V}{128}$ for μ-law uniform-PAM | Smallest step | V | | 11 | Total quantization levels | $L = 256 \times 2 = 512$ (8-bit linear) | levels | | 12 | Dynamic range of μ-law | $\approx 40 \log_{10}(1+\mu) = 40 \log_{10}(256) \approx 48$ dB | dB | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical:** Never write absolute value as `|x|` in markdown tables. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Standard | Law Used | |---|---|---| | Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) — India | ITU-T G.711 | **A-law** | | PSTN — North America & Japan | ITU-T G.711 | **μ-law** | | VoIP (e.g., Skype, WhatsApp calls) | G.711 μ/A | Both, depending on region | | Digital audio CD (16-bit linear PCM) | Red Book | No companding (linear) | | GSM mobile telephony | GSM 06.10 | A-law (variant) | ## 2.7 8-Bit PCM Code Structure For both μ-law and A-law, the 8-bit codeword per sample is structured as: | Bit Position | $b_7$ | $b_6 b_5 b_4$ | $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$ | |---|---|---|---| | Meaning | **Sign** (0=+ve, 1=-ve) | **Segment** number (0–7) | **Step** number within segment (0–15) | - **Sign bit** identifies polarity. - **3-bit segment** identifies which of 8 chord segments the sample falls into (positive side). - **4-bit step** identifies the quantization level within that segment. - The lowest segment (segment 0) is **uniformly divided into 16 equal steps** $\Delta_0$. - Each higher segment **doubles the step size**: $\Delta_i = 2^i \Delta_0$. For μ-law: $\Delta_0 = \dfrac{V}{128}$ (half-range $V$ divided into 8 segments, first segment = 16 steps of $\Delta_0$). For A-law: identical structure but the linear segment near zero uses $\Delta_0 = \dfrac{V}{64 \cdot 16}$ (since $V$ spans 64 normalized units for A-law) — same end result numerically. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the μ-law Compression Equation We seek a function $y = f(x)$ with the following properties: 1. $y$ is monotonically increasing (preserves information). 2. $y$ has odd symmetry: $f(-x) = -f(x)$. 3. Slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ is **maximum at $x=0$** and **minimum at $x=1$** to provide fine quantization for small signals. 4. The curve must be continuous and bounded: $f(\pm 1) = \pm 1$. **Step 1:** Assume the slope has the form (a first-order hyperbolic decay): $$\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{C}{1 + \mu \vert x \vert}$$ where $C$ and $\mu$ are constants. This form gives maximum slope at $x=0$ ($= C$) and decays smoothly. **Step 2:** Apply boundary condition $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\big\vert_{x=1} = \dfrac{C}{1+\mu}$ must equal $\dfrac{1}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ (chosen so that $f(1)=1$ integrates correctly). Setting $C = \dfrac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)}$ ensures the curve passes through $(1,1)$. **Step 3:** Integrate for $x \ge 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(x) &= \int_0^x \frac{\mu}{(1+\mu x')\ln(1+\mu)} \, dx' \\[6pt] &= \frac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu} \bigl[\ln(1+\mu x')\bigr]_0^x \\[6pt] &= \frac{\ln(1+\mu x)}{\ln(1+\mu)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply odd symmetry to extend to negative $x$, and verify boundary conditions: $$ y(\pm 1) = \pm \frac{\ln(1+\mu)}{\ln(1+\mu)} = \pm 1 \;\; \checkmark $$ **Final result:** $$ \boxed{\;y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \frac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}\;} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of the A-law Compression Equation We modify μ-law by introducing a **linear segment near the origin** to avoid infinite slope. **Step 1:** Define piecewise: linear for $0 \le \vert x \vert \le 1/A$, logarithmic for $1/A \le \vert x \vert \le 1$. **Step 2 (Linear segment):** Let $y = k \cdot x$ for $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$. We require continuity at $x = 1/A$ with the logarithmic segment. **Step 3 (Logarithmic segment):** Let $y = m + n \cdot \ln(\vert x \vert)$ for $\vert x \vert \ge 1/A$. **Step 4 (Boundary conditions):** - $y(0) = 0$ ✓ (linear segment passes through origin) - $y(1) = 1$ (full scale) - $y$ continuous at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$ **Step 5 (Slope continuity at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$):** To avoid a kink (which would cause harmonic distortion), $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ must be continuous. This requires the linear slope $k$ to equal the logarithmic slope at the break point. $$ \frac{A}{1+\ln A} = \left.\frac{n}{x}\right|_{x=1/A} = \frac{nA}{1} $$ Solving: $n = \dfrac{1}{1+\ln A}$. **Step 6 (Use $\vert x \vert = 1/A$ continuity and $y(1) = 1$):** $$ \frac{A \cdot (1/A)}{1+\ln A} = m + \frac{1}{1+\ln A}\ln(1/A) $$ Solving for $m$: $$ \frac{1}{1+\ln A} = m - \frac{\ln A}{1+\ln A} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; m = \frac{1+\ln A}{1+\ln A} = 1 $$ **Final result:** $$ \boxed{\; y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1+\ln A}, & 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A} \\[10pt] \dfrac{1+\ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1+\ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \le \vert x \vert \le 1 \end{cases} \;} $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — μ-law at Standard Points ($\mu = 255$) We evaluate $\ln(1+\mu) = \ln(256) = 5.5452$. | $x$ | $\mu \vert x \vert$ | $1 + \mu \vert x \vert$ | $\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)$ | $y = \ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)/5.5452$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0.0 | 0 | 1 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | | 0.1 | 25.5 | 26.5 | 3.2771 | 0.5910 | | 0.25 | 63.75 | 64.75 | 4.1703 | 0.7521 | | 0.5 | 127.5 | 128.5 | 4.8560 | 0.8757 | | 0.75 | 191.25 | 192.25 | 5.2587 | 0.9484 | | 1.0 | 255 | 256 | 5.5452 | 1.0000 | **Interpretation:** An input at $x=0.1$ (small signal) is mapped to $y=0.591$ — its effective quantization levels increase by a factor of $5.91$, dramatically improving SQNR. ## 3.4 Derivation: Relationship Between Segment Number and Step Size For an 8-bit PCM code with 8 positive segments (chords) and 16 steps per segment, the $i$-th segment ($i = 0, 1, \dots, 7$) has step size: $$ \Delta_i = 2^i \cdot \Delta_0 $$ where $\Delta_0$ is the step size of the smallest (first) segment. **Reasoning:** In the μ-law piecewise linear approximation (used in digital implementation), each segment is divided into 16 equal sub-intervals. To maintain a smooth logarithmic curve, the step size doubles from one segment to the next. For the **largest segment** ($i = 7$): $$ \Delta_7 = 2^7 \cdot \Delta_0 = 128 \, \Delta_0 $$ For the **smallest segment** ($i = 0$): $$ \Delta_0 = \frac{V}{128} $$ where $V$ is the peak signal amplitude (positive side, since we have 8 segments × 16 steps = 128 quantization intervals). The first segment occupies the range $0 \le y \le 1/128$ (in normalized terms), giving $16 \times \Delta_0 = 16/128 = 1/8$ of the dynamic range — exactly matching the first chord in the standard. **Total number of levels (positive side):** $L_+ = 8 \times 16 = 128$. **Total levels (both polarities):** $L = 256$. **Bit rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64$ kbps. ## 3.5 SQNR Comparison: Uniform vs. Companded PCM For **uniform PCM** (no companding) with sinusoidal input: $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{uniform} = 6.02n + 1.76 \;\; \text{dB} $$ For **n-bit companded PCM** (μ-law with $\mu = 255$, sinusoidal input), the effective SQNR is approximately constant: $$ \left(\frac{S}{N_q}\right)_{companded} \approx 6.02n - 9.99 \;\; \text{dB (worst case)} $$ For $n = 8$ bits: - Uniform: $6.02(8) + 1.76 = 49.92$ dB (only at full scale) - μ-law: $\approx 38.1$ dB (constant from -30 dB to full scale) **Trade-off:** Companded PCM sacrifices ~12 dB at peak signal but **maintains a constant SQNR over a 30+ dB dynamic range**, which is far more valuable for speech. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: PCM System with Companding ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Analog Input
Speech Signal] --> B[Anti-Aliasing
Filter LPF] B --> C[Sampler
fs = 8 kHz] C --> D[Compressor
A-law or Mu-law] D --> E[Uniform
Quantizer
8-bit] E --> F[Binary
Encoder] F --> G[Channel
64 kbps] G --> H[Binary
Decoder] H --> I[Uniform
Dequantizer] I --> J[Expander
Inverse A-law
or Mu-law] J --> K[Reconstruction
LPF] K --> L[Analog Output
Speech Signal] style A fill:#e1f5ff style L fill:#e1f5ff style D fill:#ffe1e1 style J fill:#ffe1e1 style E fill:#fff4e1 style I fill:#fff4e1 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Compression Curve Realization ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[Input Sample
Vin] --> SegCalc[Segment
Calculator] SegCalc --> Chk{Is Vin
greater than
threshold Ti?} Chk -->|Yes| LogComp[Logarithmic
Compressor
ylog = sgn x ln 1+mu|x|] Chk -->|No| LinComp[Linear
Compressor
ylin = sgn x A|x|] LinComp --> Normalize[Normalize
Output to -1,1] LogComp --> Normalize Normalize --> Quant[Uniform
Quantizer] Quant --> OutComp[Compressed
Digital Code] style Input fill:#e1f5ff style OutComp fill:#e1ffe1 style Chk fill:#fff4e1 style LogComp fill:#ffe1e1 style LinComp fill:#ffe1e1 ``` ## 4.3 Code Structure Hierarchy (8-bit PCM Word) ```mermaid flowchart TD Word[8-bit PCM Codeword] --> S[Bit 7
Sign
0 positive
1 negative] Word --> Seg[Bits 6 to 4
Segment
0 to 7
8 chord segments] Word --> Step[Bits 3 to 0
Step
0 to 15
16 levels per segment] Seg --> Sg0[Segment 0
smallest step
Delta sub 0] Seg --> Sg1[Segment 1
step = 2 Delta sub 0] Seg --> Sg2[Segment 2
step = 4 Delta sub 0] Seg --> Sn[Segment 7
largest step
128 Delta sub 0] style Word fill:#fff4e1 style S fill:#ffe1e1 style Seg fill:#e1f5ff style Step fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ## 4.4 Companding Process Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX[Transmitter] direction TB A1[Vin wide range] --> A2[Compressor] A2 --> A3[Quantized Vq
narrow range] end TX --> CH[Digital Channel] subgraph RX[Receiver] direction TB B1[Digital code] --> B2[Expander] B2 --> B3[Vout wide range] end CH --> RX style A2 fill:#ffe1e1 style B2 fill:#ffe1e1 style A1 fill:#e1f5ff style B3 fill:#e1f5ff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a functional stage. Red blocks are the non-linear companding stages; yellow blocks are uniform quantization; blue blocks are the analog I/O boundaries. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define companding. Why is it required in PCM systems?** **Model Answer:** Companding is the process of **compressing** the signal at the transmitter and **expanding** it at the receiver using a non-linear transfer characteristic. The two standard companding laws are **μ-law** (μ = 255, used in North America/Japan) and **A-law** (A = 87.6, used in Europe/India). It is required because speech signals have a large dynamic range (~40 dB). In **uniform quantization**, the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio (SQNR) drops proportionally with input power — soft signals become noisy while loud signals may clip. Companding effectively implements **non-uniform quantization** with smaller step sizes for low-amplitude signals, keeping SQNR approximately **constant over a wide dynamic range**, thereby improving overall speech quality. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 reason + 1 benefit)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **State the A-law compression characteristic and explain the role of the parameter $A = 87.6$.** **Model Answer:** $$ y = \mathrm{sgn}(x) \cdot \begin{cases} \dfrac{A \vert x \vert}{1+\ln A}, & 0 \le \vert x \vert \le \dfrac{1}{A} \\[10pt] \dfrac{1+\ln(A \vert x \vert)}{1+\ln A}, & \dfrac{1}{A} \le \vert x \vert \le 1 \end{cases} $$ The parameter **$A = 87.6$** (ITU-T G.711 standard) controls the degree of compression: - Higher $A$ → stronger compression near the origin → wider linear segment (small signals preserved better). - The break point at $\vert x \vert = 1/A \approx 0.0114$ marks the transition from linear to logarithmic behavior. - $A = 87.6$ provides a **dynamic range of approximately 40 dB**, well-matched to human speech. *(3 marks: 1 formula + 1 explanation of $A$ + 1 numerical example)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **KTU Exam Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Total = **14 marks**. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the μ-law compression characteristic. Explain why it is approximated by 15 linear segments in practical PCM systems. (7 marks) **(b)** An 8-bit PCM system uses μ-law companding with $\mu = 255$ and peak signal amplitude $V = 4$ V. Determine (i) the smallest and largest step sizes, and (ii) the number of quantization levels per segment. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Question A (a) **Derivation of μ-law compression:** We seek a monotonically increasing function $y(x)$ with maximum slope at $x = 0$ and minimum at $x = 1$. **Step 1:** Assume slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{C}{1 + \mu \vert x \vert}$. **Step 2:** Apply boundary $y(1) = 1$. Integrate for $x \ge 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(x) &= \int_0^x \frac{C}{1 + \mu x'} dx' = \frac{C}{\mu}\ln(1+\mu x)\bigg\vert_0^x \\[4pt] &= \frac{C}{\mu}\ln(1+\mu x) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Apply $y(1) = 1$: $$ 1 = \frac{C}{\mu}\ln(1+\mu) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C = \frac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute back and apply odd symmetry: $$ \boxed{\;y = \mathrm{sgn}(x)\cdot\frac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}\;} $$ **Why 15-segment approximation?** In digital implementation, an exponential/logarithmic curve cannot be perfectly realized with finite precision. The continuous μ-law curve is approximated by **15 linear segments** (8 positive chords + 7 negative chords, with the central segment shared between positive and negative polarities, or equivalently 16 segments with one being the shared zero-crossing segment). Each segment doubles the step size of the previous one: $\Delta_i = 2^i \Delta_0$. This piecewise-linear approximation matches the true μ-law curve to within a few percent, sufficient for 8-bit PCM. *(7 marks: 3 derivation + 2 practical reasoning + 2 approximation reasoning)* --- #### Solution to Question A (b) **Given:** $n = 8$ bits, $\mu = 255$, peak amplitude $V = 4$ V. **(i) Smallest and largest step sizes:** The 8-bit code uses 8 positive segments with 16 steps per segment. The total positive range $V = 4$ V is divided into $8 \times 16 = 128$ intervals of size $\Delta_0$ in the smallest segment: $$ \Delta_0 = \frac{V}{128} = \frac{4}{128} = 0.03125 \text{ V} $$ The largest step is in segment 7: $$ \Delta_7 = 2^7 \cdot \Delta_0 = 128 \cdot 0.03125 = 4.0 \text{ V} $$ **(ii) Quantization levels per segment:** Each segment contains **16 quantization levels** (4 bits of step code: $b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0$). The total number of positive levels: $8 \times 16 = 128$. Total levels (both polarities): $256$ → represented by 8 bits ($2^8 = 256$). *(7 marks: 2 formula + 2 calculation + 1 largest step + 2 levels per segment)* --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare A-law and μ-law companding. State their standard parameter values and regions where each is used. (7 marks) **(b)** For a μ-law PCM system with $\mu = 255$, an input voltage $V_{in} = 0.05$ V produces an output code with sign bit = 0, segment bits = 010, step bits = 1011. Determine the quantized voltage and the quantization error. Assume peak signal voltage $V = 4$ V. (7 marks) --- #### Solution to Question B (a) **Comparison Table:** | Feature | μ-law | A-law | |---|---|---| | Compression parameter | $\mu = 255$ | $A = 87.6$ | | Mathematical form | Continuous logarithmic | Piecewise: linear + logarithmic | | Behavior at $x = 0$ | Slope $\to \dfrac{\mu}{\ln(1+\mu)} \approx 46$ (no linear segment) | Linear with slope $= \dfrac{A}{1+\ln A} \approx 16$ | | Break point | None | $\vert x \vert = 1/A \approx 0.0114$ | | Used in | North America, Japan | Europe, India, rest of world | | ITU Standard | G.711 | G.711 | | Implementation segments | 15 (or 16) | 13 (or 16) | | Small-signal fidelity | Slightly worse (logarithmic all the way) | Better (linear near origin) | | Compression strength | Slightly stronger near origin | Slightly weaker near origin | **Key Differences:** 1. **A-law has a linear segment near origin** — signals very close to zero are quantized with uniform (and very fine) steps, giving slightly better fidelity for low-amplitude tones. 2. **μ-law is purely logarithmic** — easier to implement in analog hardware using diode-resistor networks. 3. Both provide comparable SQNR for speech signals in the standard 8-bit, 8 kHz PCM format (64 kbps). *(7 marks: 3 comparison table + 2 usage + 2 technical differences)* --- #### Solution to Question B (b) **Given:** $\mu = 255$, $V = 4$ V, $V_{in} = 0.05$ V (positive). **Code:** Sign = 0 (+), Segment = 010 (= 2), Step = 1011 (= 11). **Step 1 — Determine segment 2 properties:** Step size in segment 2: $$ \Delta_2 = 2^2 \cdot \Delta_0 = 4 \Delta_0 $$ For μ-law, $\Delta_0 = V/128 = 4/128 = 0.03125$ V. $$ \Delta_2 = 4 \times 0.03125 = 0.125 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2 — Locate the segment boundaries on the positive side:** - Segment 0 spans: $0$ to $16\Delta_0 = 0.5$ V - Segment 1 spans: $0.5$ V to $(16 + 32)\Delta_0 = 1.0$ V - Segment 2 spans: $1.0$ V to $(16 + 32 + 64)\Delta_0 = 2.0$ V **Step 3 — Locate the quantized level within segment 2:** The lower bound of segment 2 = $1.0$ V. With 16 equal steps of size $\Delta_2 = 0.125$ V: $$ \text{Quantized level} = 1.0 + \left(11 + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) \cdot 0.125 $$ (The $+1/2$ shifts to the midpoint of the 11th interval — standard quantization convention.) $$ V_q = 1.0 + 11.5 \times 0.125 = 1.0 + 1.4375 = 2.4375 \text{ V} $$ **Step 4 — Quantization error:** $$ \epsilon = V_{in} - V_q = 0.05 - 2.4375 = -2.3875 \text{ V} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note:** $V_{in} = 0.05$ V actually lies in **segment 0** (range 0 to 0.5 V), not segment 2! This indicates the problem provides a hypothetical code assignment. In a real system, the encoder maps $V_{in} = 0.05$ V to segment 0, not segment 2. We proceed with the hypothetical code as given. **Alternatively (re-interpreting the problem):** If we treat the **input $V_{in}$ as the compressed-domain voltage** (i.e., the value after compression but before uniform quantization), the analysis above applies directly. The quantization error is bounded by $\pm \Delta_2/2 = \pm 0.0625$ V. *(7 marks: 1 segment identification + 1 step size + 1 boundary + 2 quantized level + 2 quantization error)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Confusing segments with steps:** A segment is one of 8 chord divisions; a step is one of 16 sub-intervals within a segment. Mixing them up loses 1–2 marks immediately. > 2. **Forgetting the $\mathrm{sgn}(x)$ term:** Always include sign-bit logic. μ-law and A-law are defined on $[-1, +1]$, not just $[0, 1]$. > 3. **Using $\mu = 100$ instead of 255:** The ITU standard is **μ = 255**. Any other value is non-standard and will be penalized unless explicitly justified. > 4. **Linear vs. logarithmic segment in A-law:** Students often write A-law as a single equation, missing the break point at $\vert x \vert = 1/A$. This loses the differentiation mark. > 5. **Step size doubling:** Forgetting that $\Delta_i = 2^i \Delta_0$ is the foundational rule for the 15-segment approximation. > 6. **Dynamic range calculation:** A common error is writing "40 dB" for μ-law without specifying it is $20 \log_{10}(1+\mu) \approx 48$ dB. Clarify whether you mean the linear amplitude ratio or the logarithmic dB scale. > 7. **Code structure confusion:** The 8-bit PCM word is **Sign(1) + Segment(3) + Step(4)**, **not** Step(3) + Segment(4). Memorize this correctly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid-Revision Checklist - [x] **Companding** = Compression (TX) + Expansion (RX); implements non-uniform quantization. - [x] **μ-law:** $y = \mathrm{sgn}(x)\cdot \dfrac{\ln(1+\mu \vert x \vert)}{\ln(1+\mu)}$, with $\mu = 255$; used in US/Japan. - [x] **A-law:** piecewise linear + log, with $A = 87.6$; used in Europe/India; linear for $\vert x \vert \le 1/A$. - [x] **Standard PCM:** 8 kHz sampling, 8 bits/sample → 64 kbps bit rate. - [x] **8-bit code structure:** Sign (1) + Segment (3) + Step (4). - [x] **15-segment approximation:** $\Delta_i = 2^i \Delta_0$; smallest step $\Delta_0 = V/128$ for μ-law. - [x] **Total levels:** $2 \times 8 \times 16 = 256$ per codeword (512 total with sign). - [x] **SQNR for uniform PCM:** $1.76 + 6.02n$ dB (drops with input level). - [x] **SQNR for companded PCM:** approximately constant over 30–40 dB dynamic range. - [x] **Dynamic range of μ-law (μ=255):** $20 \log_{10}(256) \approx 48$ dB. - [x] **Boundary conditions:** $y(\pm 1) = \pm 1$; $y(0) = 0$; curve passes through origin. - [x] **Application areas:** PSTN (India uses A-law), VoIP, GSM (A-law variant), digital telephony. - [x] **Linear segment of A-law near origin:** preserves small signals; μ-law has none. - [x] **Quantization error bound:** $\pm \Delta_i / 2$ within the $i$-th segment. ### Final Memory Hook > **"A for Asia/Europe, μ for USA/Japan"** > **"Sign–Segment–Step = 1–3–4 = 8 bits"** > **"Doubling rule: $\Delta_i = 2^i \Delta_0$"**

topics.title: Amplitude Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Amplitude Shift Keying # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)** — also called **Amplitude Modulation Keying** — is a fundamental form of **digital-to-analog conversion** in which the **amplitude** of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is varied in discrete steps in accordance with a digital baseband (message) signal, while the **frequency** and **phase** of the carrier are held strictly constant. For the most common binary case, **Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK)**, the carrier is transmitted for a binary **'1'** (logic HIGH) and is suppressed (zero amplitude) for a binary **'0'** (logic LOW). This specific binary variant is popularly known as **On–Off Keying (OOK)** because the carrier is essentially switched "ON" and "OFF" by the message bits. Mathematically, the BASK/OOK signal in the time domain can be expressed as: $$ s(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for bit } b_k = 1 \\[4pt] 0, & \text{for bit } b_k = 0 \end{cases} $$ where $A_c$ is the peak carrier amplitude and $f_c$ is the carrier frequency. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ASK is the **simplest digital modulation scheme** because it is mathematically equivalent to suppressed-carrier Double-Sideband AM (DSB-SC AM) where the modulating signal $m(t)$ is a unipolar binary waveform (levels `0` and `+1`). This equivalence is a frequently tested relation. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lighthouse Intuition Imagine a **lighthouse keeper** on a cliff. He has only **one type of lamp** (single color, fixed brightness when ON). To send a coded message to ships at sea, he does **not change the color** (frequency stays fixed) and he does **not change the rhythm** (phase stays fixed). He only **switches the lamp ON for a "dot" and OFF for a "dash"** — the Morse code. That is exactly ASK. The carrier is the lighthouse lamp; the message is the Morse pattern; switching the lamp amplitude ON/OFF represents the bits. Ships (receivers) decode the message by simply checking *"Is the light present or absent in each time slot?"* > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Implementations of ASK/OOK:** > - **Optical Fiber & Free-Space Optical (FSO) Links** — lasers are switched ON/OFF to carry data (e.g., IR remote controls at **38 kHz** carrier). > - **RFID Tags** — passive tags backscatter a CW carrier from the reader with ASK modulation. > - **Early Morse Radio Telegraphy** — the original "spark gap" transmitters used OOK. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical/Standard Value | |-----------|--------|------------------------| | Carrier frequency | $f_c$ | Tens of kHz to several GHz | | Bit duration | $T_b$ | $1/R_b$, where $R_b$ is bit rate in **bits/second (bps)** | | Transmission bandwidth | $B_T$ | $\mathbf{2R_b}$ (twice the bit rate) | | Modulation index / depth | $m_a$ | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ (100% for full OOK) | | Carrier amplitude | $A_c$ | Set by link-budget / power constraint | > [!TIP] > **Memorization Anchor for KTU:** The bandwidth of ASK is **always** $B_T = 2R_b$, identical to DSB-SC. This is because the baseband binary spectrum (a sinc-squared lobe) shifts to both sides of $f_c$, creating upper and lower sidebands, each of width $R_b$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of BASK Generation The binary message $m(t)$ is a unipolar NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) waveform taking the value **+1 V** for bit `1` and **0 V** for bit `0`. ASK is generated by **multiplying** this baseband waveform with the high-frequency carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Baseband message generation:** A binary data stream (e.g., `1 0 1 1 0 0 1`) is shaped into rectangular NRZ pulses $m(t)$ of amplitude $+1$ V (or $+A$) and duration $T_b$. 2. **Carrier availability:** A stable local oscillator (e.g., a Colpitts or crystal oscillator) generates $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with $f_c \gg R_b$. 3. **Multiplication:** A balanced modulator / analog multiplier multiplies $m(t)$ and $c(t)$. Wherever $m(t) = 1$, the carrier passes through at full amplitude. Wherever $m(t) = 0$, the multiplier output is zero. 4. **Resulting waveform $s(t)$:** A "chopped" sinusoid — bursts of carrier separated by silent gaps. ### Why ASK Works (The 'How' Behind It) Mathematically, multiplication in the time domain = **convolution in the frequency domain**. The spectrum of the unipolar binary signal (a sinc-squared shape centered at DC) gets shifted to be centered at $+f_c$ and $-f_c$. Hence the transmitted spectrum contains **two sidebands** with **no carrier component** when binary `0` is sent — which is why ASK is power-efficient *only* when transmitting `1`s (a major drawback). ## 2.2 The Modulation Index — A Key Design Knob The modulation index of ASK is defined as: $$ m_a \;=\; \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}} $$ - $A_{\max}$ = carrier amplitude during bit `1` - $A_{\min}$ = carrier amplitude during bit `0` For **pure OOK**, $A_{\min} = 0$, giving $m_a = 1$ (i.e., **100% modulation**). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the modulation index of ASK with that of conventional AM. In conventional AM, $m_a \le 1$ is required to avoid distortion. In ASK (a digital scheme), $m_a$ is simply a measure of "how deep" the carrier is suppressed during a `0`-bit. It is **not** a distortion parameter in the analog sense. ## 2.3 Power Analysis of BASK The instantaneous power dissipated across a $1\ \Omega$ resistive load is: $$ P(t) \;=\; \frac{s^2(t)}{R} \;=\; \frac{A_c^2}{R}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t) \quad \text{(when bit = 1)} $$ Averaging over one bit period and over the bit stream (assuming equiprobable bits, $P(0) = P(1) = 0.5$): $$ P_{\text{avg, ASK}} \;=\; \frac{A_c^2}{4R} $$ In terms of the **carrier power** $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$ that would be transmitted if the carrier were always ON: $$ \boxed{\,P_{\text{avg, ASK}} \;=\; \frac{P_c}{2}\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Only **half** of the available carrier power is used on average because the carrier is OFF for half the time. This 3 dB power penalty is one of the chief disadvantages of BASK compared to **PSK** and **FSK**. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Boundary Condition | |---|----------|---------|----------------------------| | 1 | BASK signal (time domain) | $s(t) = A_c\, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$, $A_c$ in Volts | | 2 | Modulation index | $m_a = \dfrac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}$ | $0 \le m_a \le 1$ | | 3 | Transmission bandwidth | $B_T = 2\, R_b = \dfrac{2}{T_b}$ | Hertz (Hz) | | 4 | Average transmitted power | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4R}$ | Watts (with load $R$) | | 5 | Power relative to full carrier | $P_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{P_c}{2}$ | Equivalent to $-3$ dB | | 6 | Bit energy | $E_b = P_{\text{avg}} \cdot T_b = \dfrac{A_c^2 T_b}{4R}$ | Joules (J) | | 7 | Bit error probability (coherent) | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless | | 8 | Bit error probability (non-coherent) | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless | | 9 | Spectral efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B_T} = \dfrac{1}{2}$ | bits/s/Hz | > [!IMPORTANT] > The two error-probability expressions are the **canonical** results for BASK with **AWGN** (Additive White Gaussian Noise). They are **high-yield** for KTU derivations: coherent detection gives a $Q$-function shape; non-coherent detection gives an **exponential** shape (better at very low SNR but worse asymptotically). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Low-cost, short-range consumer products:** IR remotes, RF key fobs, simple telemetry — OOK is the cheapest digital modulation to implement (a single transistor switch is sufficient at the transmitter). - **Optical communications:** Visible-light communication (VLC) and low-rate fiber links modulate LED/laser intensity (amplitude) directly. - **Education & benchmarking:** Because ASK is mathematically the simplest digital passband scheme, every textbook uses it to introduce the receiver design, matched filter, and $Q$-function BER framework that then generalises to PSK and FSK. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Time-Domain Expression of BASK ### Starting Point Let the baseband NRZ binary message be: $$ m(t) \;=\; \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} b_k \, p(t - kT_b) $$ where $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ is the $k^{\text{th}}$ bit and $p(t)$ is a unit-amplitude rectangular pulse of duration $T_b$. The carrier is: $$ c(t) \;=\; A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ ### Multiplication (the modulation operation) ASK is generated as the product: $$ s(t) \;=\; m(t)\cdot c(t) \;=\; A_c\, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ ### Step-by-step evaluation $$ \begin{aligned} s(t) &= A_c \left[\sum_{k} b_k\, p(t - kT_b)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t) \\[4pt] &= A_c \sum_{k} b_k\, p(t - kT_b)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \end{aligned} $$ ### Substitution $b_k = 0$ or $1$ $$ \therefore\; s(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & b_k = 1 \\[4pt] 0, & b_k = 0 \end{cases} $$ This is the **standard BASK / OOK waveform**. > [!NOTE] > **Alternate viewpoint:** Because $m(t)$ has a non-zero DC value $E[m(t)] = \tfrac{1}{2}$ (mean of equiprobable $\{0,1\}$), $s(t)$ can also be decomposed into a "carrier with modulation" form, identical to DSB-AM with a DC-biased message: $$ s(t) \;=\; A_c \left[m(t) - \tfrac{1}{2}\right]\cos(2\pi f_c t) \;+\; \underbrace{\tfrac{A_c}{2}\cos(2\pi f_c t)}_{\text{residual carrier component}} $$ This residual carrier is the **continuous-tone component** that appears in the spectrum when bits are equiprobable. ## 3.2 Derivation: Frequency-Domain Spectrum of BASK The Fourier transform of $m(t)$ is: $$ M(f) \;=\; \frac{T_b}{2}\,\mathrm{sinc}(fT_b)\,e^{-j\pi f T_b} \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,\delta(f) \quad \text{(approx.)} $$ Multiplying by $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ corresponds to shifting $M(f)$ to $\pm f_c$ and halving its amplitude (modulation property of Fourier transform): $$ \boxed{\,S(f) \;=\; \frac{A_c}{2}\bigl[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\bigr]\,} $$ The spectrum therefore consists of: 1. **Two sinc-shaped sidebands** of width $2R_b$ each, centered at $\pm f_c$. 2. **Two Dirac impulses** at $\pm f_c$ of weight $\tfrac{A_c}{4}$ due to the DC bias in $m(t)$. Total bandwidth occupied: $$ \boxed{\,B_T \;=\; 2\,R_b\,} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation: Average Transmitted Power Average power over a long stream with equiprobable bits: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{avg}} &= E\!\left[\frac{s^2(t)}{R}\right] \\[4pt] &= \frac{A_c^2}{R}\, E\!\left[\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\right]\cdot E\!\left[m^2(t)\right] \quad \text{(independent RVs)} \\[4pt] &= \frac{A_c^2}{R}\cdot \frac{1}{2}\cdot \underbrace{E\!\left[m^2(t)\right]}_{=\,E[m(t)]\;=\;1/2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{A_c^2}{4R} \end{aligned} $$ Since the full-carrier power is $P_c = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2R}$: $$ \boxed{\,P_{\text{avg}} \;=\; \frac{P_c}{2}\;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right) \approx -3\text{ dB}\,} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation: Bit Error Probability (Coherent BASK) Assume an **AWGN** channel with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$. The received signal in bit interval $k$ is: $$ r(t) \;=\; \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + n(t), & b_k = 1 \\[4pt] n(t), & b_k = 0 \end{cases} $$ A **correlator (matched filter)** receiver computes: $$ z \;=\; \int_{kT_b}^{(k+1)T_b} r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt $$ Decision rule: choose `1` if $z > \gamma$, else choose `0`. The optimal threshold (for equal priors) is $\gamma = \dfrac{A_c T_b}{4}$. Under hypothesis $H_1$ (bit `1`): $z$ is Gaussian with mean $\dfrac{A_c T_b}{2}$ and variance $\dfrac{N_0 T_b}{4}$. Under hypothesis $H_0$ (bit `0`): $z$ is Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance $\dfrac{N_0 T_b}{4}$. The bit error probability is: $$ \begin{aligned} P_e &= \tfrac{1}{2}P(\text{error} \mid H_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}P(\text{error} \mid H_1) \\[4pt] &= \tfrac{1}{2}\,Q\!\left(\frac{\gamma}{\sigma}\right) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,Q\!\left(\frac{A_c T_b/2 - \gamma}{\sigma}\right) \\[4pt] &= Q\!\left(\frac{A_c T_b/2}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b/4}}\right) \\[4pt] &= Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{A_c^2 T_b}{2 N_0}}\right) \end{aligned} $$ Using bit energy $E_b = \dfrac{A_c^2 T_b}{4}$ (energy transmitted during a `1`-bit divided by 2 for equiprobable bits): $$ \boxed{\,P_{e,\text{coh}} \;=\; Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)\,} $$ ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | BASK (Coherent) | BASK (Non-Coherent) | BPSK (for reference) | |----------|----------------|---------------------|----------------------| | Detection complexity | High (needs sync) | Low (envelope detector) | High (needs sync) | | $P_e$ vs $E_b/N_0$ | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | | Power efficiency (at $P_e = 10^{-5}$) | Poor (~12.6 dB) | Slightly worse (~13.4 dB) | Best (~9.6 dB) | | Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $2R_b$ | $2R_b$ | | Immunity to noise | Weak | Weaker | Strong | | Implementation cost | Medium | Lowest | High | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Insight:** BPSK offers a **3 dB SNR advantage** over coherent BASK at the same $P_e$, because its two signal phasors are **antipodal** (separated by $180^\circ$) whereas BASK's phasors are only separated by $90^\circ$ in signal-space. This 3 dB gain is the mathematical reason BASK is rarely used in modern bandwidth-efficient systems. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: BASK Transmitter (Switching Modulator) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["NRZ Binary
Data m(t)"]:::inputNode B["Band-Limited
Filter"]:::procNode C["Local Oscillator
c(t) = Ac cos(2 pi fc t)"]:::inputNode D["Balanced
Modulator /
Multiplier"]:::procNode E["Band-Pass
Filter
fc +/- Rb"]:::procNode F["Power
Amplifier"]:::procNode G["ASK Signal
s(t) to Antenna"]:::outputNode A --> B B --> D C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G classDef inputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef procNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Working Principle:** The NRZ binary stream is filtered to limit its bandwidth (preventing spectral regrowth). A high-frequency carrier from a stable oscillator is multiplied with the baseband data in a balanced modulator. The output bursts of carrier during bit `1` and zeros during bit `0` are band-pass filtered, amplified by a power amplifier, and radiated. ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: BASK Coherent (Synchronous) Receiver ```mermaid flowchart TD R["Received RF
Signal r(t)"]:::inputNode BPF["RF Band-Pass
Filter"]:::procNode LNA["Low-Noise
Amplifier"]:::procNode MIX["Multiplier
Mixer"]:::procNode LO["Local Oscillator
2 cos(2 pi fc t)"]:::inputNode LPF["Integrate
and Dump
Low-Pass Filter"]:::procNode TH["Decision
Threshold
Comparator"]:::procNode CLK["Bit Clock
Recovery"]:::procNode OUT["Recovered
Binary m-hat(t)"]:::outputNode R --> BPF BPF --> LNA LNA --> MIX LO --> MIX MIX --> LPF LPF --> TH CLK --> TH TH --> OUT classDef inputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef procNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Working Principle:** The noisy received RF signal is filtered, amplified by an LNA, and down-converted to baseband by multiplying with a **synchronized** local oscillator (hence "coherent" detection). The integrate-and-dump circuit acts as a **matched filter** for rectangular NRZ pulses. The sampled output is compared against a threshold $\gamma = A_c T_b / 4$ to decide whether each bit was `1` or `0`. ## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: BASK Non-Coherent (Envelope) Receiver ```mermaid flowchart TD RX["Received
r(t)"]:::inputNode BPF1["RF BPF
Centre fc"]:::procNode DET["Envelope
Detector
Diode + RC"]:::procNode LPF1["Low-Pass
Filter"]:::procNode SMP["Sample
and Hold
at Tb"]:::procNode DEC["Threshold
Comparator"]:::procNode OUT2["Recovered
Bits"]:::outputNode RX --> BPF1 BPF1 --> DET DET --> LPF1 LPF1 --> SMP SMP --> DEC DEC --> OUT2 classDef inputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef procNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Working Principle:** This low-cost receiver needs **no carrier synchronization**. A simple diode + RC envelope detector follows the instantaneous amplitude of the BPF-filtered RF. The LPF removes the carrier ripple. A sampler (timed by a recovered bit clock) reads the envelope amplitude at the centre of each bit period and compares against a threshold. This architecture is why OOK is dominant in **ultra-cheap** links like RFID and IR remotes. ## 4.4 Signal-Space (Constellation) Diagram for BASK ```mermaid flowchart LR ORIG["Origin"]:::inputNode P0["Signal Point
s0 = 0
for bit 0"]:::procNode P1["Signal Point
s1 = sqrt Eb
for bit 1"]:::outputNode ORIG --- P0 ORIG --- P1 classDef inputNode fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef procNode fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#ef6c00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef outputNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` The two signal points lie **along the real axis**: `0` (origin) for bit `0`, and $\sqrt{E_b}$ for bit `1`. The Euclidean distance between them is $\sqrt{E_b}$, which directly enters the $Q$-function BER expression. > [!TIP] > **Visual Memory Aid:** ASK constellation is **1-D** (a line). BPSK constellation is also **1-D** but uses two antipodal points $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$ (distance $2\sqrt{E_b}$). The ratio of squared distances $(2\sqrt{E_b})^2 : (\sqrt{E_b})^2 = 4 : 1$ corresponds exactly to the **3 dB SNR advantage** of BPSK over coherent BASK. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK). Why is the binary version of ASK called On–Off Keying (OOK)?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)** is a digital modulation technique in which the amplitude of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier is switched between two (or more) discrete levels in accordance with a digital baseband message, while the carrier frequency and phase remain constant. For the binary case (BASK), the carrier amplitude is set to $A_c$ when the input bit is `1` and to $0$ when the input bit is `0`. Because the carrier is literally switched **ON** for bit `1` and **OFF** (fully suppressed) for bit `0`, the scheme is called **On–Off Keying (OOK)** — a name borrowed from early radio-telegraphy where the CW transmitter was keyed ON and OFF to send Morse code. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for binary signal expression, 1 mark for OOK naming rationale) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Compare BASK and BPSK in terms of bandwidth requirement and probability of error in an AWGN channel.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | BASK | BPSK | |-----------|------|------| | Transmission bandwidth | $B_T = 2R_b$ | $B_T = 2R_b$ | | Bit error probability | $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | | Power efficiency | Lower (worse by 3 dB) | Higher | Both BASK and BPSK have identical transmission bandwidth ($2R_b$) because both are linear modulations with a sinc-shaped baseband. However, BPSK is **3 dB more power-efficient** than BASK at any given $P_e$, because the BPSK signal constellation is antipodal (two points separated by distance $2\sqrt{E_b}$) whereas BASK's constellation points are separated by only $\sqrt{E_b}$. (1.5 marks for bandwidth equality, 1.5 marks for BER comparison and 3 dB rationale) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > **KTU Pattern:** Part B Module 4 questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** (concept + diagram) and **Part (b) — 7 marks** (mathematical derivation / analysis). Internal choice is **mandatory**. ### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024] **Q.A(a)** Explain the generation and detection of Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK) signal with neat block diagrams. Derive the expression for the BASK signal in the time domain. **Model Solution:** **Generation (4 marks):** The block diagram of the BASK transmitter is given in Section 4.1. The NRZ binary data $m(t)$ (unipolar, levels 0 and 1) is multiplied with the carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using a balanced modulator. The output $s(t) = A_c\, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ is band-pass filtered around $f_c$ and amplified. The time-domain expression is: $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t), & 0 \le t \le T_b \;\;(\text{bit } 1) \\ 0, & 0 \le t \le T_b \;\;(\text{bit } 0) \end{cases} $$ **Detection (3 marks):** Two receiver architectures are valid (any one with explanation): * **Coherent (Synchronous) Detection:** A local oscillator phase-locked to the carrier multiplies $r(t)$, the product is integrated over each $T_b$, and the integrator output is compared with a threshold $\gamma$. Decision: `1` if output $> \gamma$, else `0`. * **Non-coherent (Envelope) Detection:** A diode envelope detector follows the BPF-filtered RF, producing a slowly varying envelope. The envelope is sampled at the centre of each bit period and compared to a threshold. No carrier synchronization is required. **Q.A(b)** For BASK transmission over an AWGN channel, derive the average transmitted power and the bit error probability under coherent detection. **Model Solution:** **Average transmitted power (3.5 marks):** Assume equiprobable bits ($P(0) = P(1) = 0.5$). Power is delivered only during bit `1` (when carrier is ON): $$ P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{A_c^2}{2R} + \frac{1}{2}\cdot 0 = \frac{A_c^2}{4R} $$ Defining the full-carrier power $P_c = A_c^2/(2R)$: $$ P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{P_c}{2} \equiv -3 \text{ dB} $$ **Bit error probability — coherent detection (3.5 marks):** The correlator computes $z = \int_{kT_b}^{(k+1)T_b} r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$. - Under $H_1$ (bit `1` sent): $z$ is Gaussian with mean $A_c T_b/2$ and variance $N_0 T_b/4$. - Under $H_0$ (bit `0` sent): $z$ is Gaussian with mean $0$ and variance $N_0 T_b/4$. Optimal threshold $\gamma = A_c T_b/4$. Therefore: $$ P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{A_c T_b/2 - 0}{\sqrt{N_0 T_b/4}} - \text{(adjusted)}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) $$ where $E_b = A_c^2 T_b/4$. --- ### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 — Alternative] **Q.B(a)** With the help of a signal-space (constellation) diagram and a phasor representation, explain the generation of a BASK signal. Compare its bandwidth efficiency with that of BPSK. **Model Solution:** **Constellation & Phasor (3.5 marks):** In the signal-space of BASK, the two possible transmitted signals in each bit interval $T_b$ are: - $\mathbf{s}_0 = 0$ (origin) for bit `0` - $\mathbf{s}_1 = \sqrt{E_b}$ (real axis) for bit `1` The two phasors are collinear with the in-phase axis and separated by a Euclidean distance $\sqrt{E_b}$. The decision boundary is the perpendicular bisector at $\sqrt{E_b}/2$. **Generation (2 marks):** A unipolar NRZ waveform $m(t)$ modulates the amplitude of the carrier in a balanced modulator. The BASK signal can be written as $s(t) = A_c m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. Because $m(t)$ has DC bias $1/2$, the spectrum contains a residual carrier component plus two DSB sidebands. **Bandwidth efficiency comparison (1.5 marks):** Both BASK and BPSK transmit at the same bit rate $R_b$ and occupy the **same transmission bandwidth** $B_T = 2R_b$. Therefore their **spectral efficiencies are identical**: $$ \eta_{\text{BASK}} = \eta_{\text{BPSK}} = \frac{R_b}{2R_b} = \frac{1}{2} \;\text{bits/s/Hz} $$ **Q.B(b)** A BASK system transmits a binary sequence at a bit rate of $R_b = 10\text{ kbps}$ over an AWGN channel with two-sided noise PSD $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}\text{ W/Hz}$. The received carrier amplitude is $A_c = 100\ \mu\text{V}$ into a $50\ \Omega$ antenna. Compute (i) the transmission bandwidth, (ii) the bit energy, and (iii) the probability of bit error under coherent detection. **Model Solution:** **Given:** $R_b = 10\text{ kbps}$, $N_0/2 = 10^{-20}$ W/Hz (so $N_0 = 2\times 10^{-20}$), $A_c = 100\ \mu\text{V} = 10^{-4}$ V, $R = 50\ \Omega$. **(i) Transmission bandwidth (2 marks):** $$ B_T = 2R_b = 2 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 20\text{ kHz} $$ **(ii) Bit energy (3 marks):** Bit duration: $T_b = 1/R_b = 10^{-4}$ s. Bit energy (energy per information bit, averaged over 0 and 1): $$ E_b = \frac{A_c^2\, T_b}{4R} = \frac{(10^{-4})^2 \cdot 10^{-4}}{4 \cdot 50} = \frac{10^{-12}}{200} = 5 \times 10^{-15}\text{ J} $$ **(iii) Probability of bit error (2 marks):** $$ \frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-15}}{2 \times 10^{-20}} = 2.5 \times 10^{5} $$ $$ P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{5}}\right) = Q(500) \approx 0 $$ This is essentially a **noise-free link** — the received SNR is so high that the error probability is astronomically small. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the ½ in $E_b$** — BASK's $E_b = A_c^2 T_b/4R$, NOT $A_c^2 T_b/2R$. The ½ arises because the carrier is OFF half the time. Writing $E_b = A_c^2 T_b/2R$ will cost 1–2 marks. > 2. **Mixing up AM modulation index with ASK modulation index** — In conventional AM, $m_a \le 1$ prevents envelope distortion. In ASK, the index is a measure of suppression depth and **must be 1 for OOK**. > 3. **Writing bandwidth as $R_b$ instead of $2R_b$** — ASK is a passband scheme with **two sidebands**; the total occupied bandwidth is twice the baseband bandwidth. > 4. **Omitting the equiprobable-bit assumption** in power derivations — Without $P(0)=P(1)=0.5$, you cannot simplify $E[m^2(t)] = 0.5$. > 5. **Choosing the wrong BER formula** — Coherent ASK gives a $Q$-function; non-coherent ASK gives a half-exponential. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 3-mark loss. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — ASK (Module 4):** - ✅ **ASK = DSB-SC AM** with a unipolar binary message; binary case is **OOK** ($m_a = 1$). - ✅ **Time-domain signal:** $s(t) = A_c\, m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, with $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$. - ✅ **Bandwidth:** $B_T = 2R_b$ — same as BPSK and BFSK. - ✅ **Average power (equiprobable bits):** $P_{\text{avg}} = A_c^2/4R = P_c/2$ — i.e., **3 dB below full carrier**. - ✅ **Bit energy:** $E_b = A_c^2 T_b / 4R$ — note the factor of ¼, not ½. - ✅ **Modulation index:** $m_a = (A_{\max} - A_{\min})/(A_{\max} + A_{\min}) \in [0, 1]$. - ✅ **Coherent BER:** $P_e = Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$. - ✅ **Non-coherent BER:** $P_e = \tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$. - ✅ **3 dB disadvantage vs BPSK** — caused by non-antipodal constellation (distance $\sqrt{E_b}$ instead of $2\sqrt{E_b}$). - ✅ **Generator:** NRZ × Carrier → Balanced Modulator → BPF → PA. - ✅ **Coherent receiver:** Synchronous detector + matched filter (integrate-and-dump) + threshold comparator. - ✅ **Non-coherent receiver:** Envelope detector + sampler + threshold comparator (used in cheap OOK links). - ✅ **Applications:** IR remotes (38 kHz), RFID backscatter, optical wireless, telemetry, Morse-code radio. - ✅ **Spectral efficiency:** $\eta = 1/2$ bits/s/Hz (same as BPSK). - ✅ **KTU golden rule:** Always state the **assumptions** — equiprobable bits, AWGN channel, NRZ pulse shaping — before any derivation.

topics.title: Phase Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Phase Shift Keying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Phase Shift Keying (PSK) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a **digital modulation** technique in which the **phase** of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier is varied in discrete steps proportional to the digital information (baseband) signal, while the **amplitude** and **frequency** of the carrier are kept **constant**.

The general mathematical representation of an M-ary PSK signal is given by:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}} \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi(i-1)}{M}\right),\quad i = 1,2,\dots,M
$$

where:
- $E_s$ = **Energy per symbol** (in Joules)
- $T_s$ = **Symbol duration** (in seconds)
- $f_c$ = **Carrier frequency** (in Hz)
- $M$ = **Number of distinct phase states** (must be a power of 2: $M = 2^k$)
- $i$ = Integer index representing the symbol value

Each of the M phase states represents a unique group of $k = \log_2 M$ bits, giving PSK its spectral efficiency advantage over binary schemes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world analogy — A Lighthouse with Multiple Lanterns:**
> Imagine a lighthouse whose beam rotates at a fixed speed (constant frequency) and always shines with the same intensity (constant amplitude). The only thing that changes is the **direction it points** at any given moment. If we allow only **two directions** (North = `1`, South = `0`), we have **BPSK**. If we allow **four directions** (N, E, S, W), each direction can carry **2 bits** of information — this is **QPSK**. As we add more directions (8, 16, 32...), the **lighthouse encodes more information per flash**, but the directions become **closer together**, making it harder to distinguish them in fog (which represents **noise** in our engineering analogy).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Key Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> - **Carrier frequency** ($f_c$): Typically in the **MHz to GHz** range for wireless systems.
> - **Bit rate** ($R_b$): Measured in **bits per second (bps)**.
> - **Symbol rate / Baud rate** ($R_s$): Measured in **symbols per second (Baud)**.
> - **Bandwidth efficiency** ($\eta$): $\eta = R_b / B$ in **bits/second/Hz**.
> - **Power efficiency**: Expressed through **Bit Error Rate (BER)** as a function of $E_b/N_0$ (energy per bit to noise power spectral density ratio, dimensionless).
> - **Spectral efficiency** increases with M, but **power efficiency decreases** (BER worsens for the same $E_b/N_0$).

## 1.4 Why PSK? (Engineering Motivation)

| Property | Advantage over ASK/FSK |
|---|---|
| Constant envelope | Resistant to amplitude nonlinearities (e.g., in power amplifiers) |
| Lower BER for same $E_b/N_0$ (BPSK/QPSK) | Better noise immunity than ASK |
| Compact constellation | Higher spectral efficiency than FSK |
| Used in Wi-Fi, satellite, deep-space comms | Industry-standard for digital cellular (GSM uses GMSK, but QPSK/OQPSK dominate 3G/4G) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of PSK Schemes

### 2.1.1 Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK) — The Simplest PSK

- **Number of phases:** $M = 2$
- **Phase separation:** $\Delta\phi = 180^\circ = \pi$ rad
- **Bits per symbol:** $k = 1$

**Signal Representation:**

$$
s_1(t) = +\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t),\quad \text{for binary } 1
$$

$$
s_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t),\quad \text{for binary } 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Equivalently, BPSK can be seen as **DSB-SC (Double-Sideband Suppressed-Carrier)** with the binary data $b(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ modulating the carrier.
> $$s(t) = b(t)\cdot\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### 2.1.2 Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK) — The Workhorse of 4G/5G

- **Number of phases:** $M = 4$
- **Phase separation:** $\Delta\phi = 90^\circ = \pi/2$ rad
- **Bits per symbol:** $k = 2$

**Signal Representation (using I/Q decomposition):**

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right),\quad i = 1,2,3,4
$$

Equivalent baseband I/Q form:

$$
s_i(t) = I_i\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_i\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $(I_i, Q_i) \in \{(\pm 1/\sqrt{2}, \pm 1/\sqrt{2})\}$.

### 2.1.3 Offset QPSK (OQPSK) — The Envelope-Friendly Variant

- In OQPSK, the **Q-branch is offset by half a symbol period** $T_s/2$ relative to the I-branch.
- This **prevents the phase from changing by 180°** in a single symbol, limiting the maximum phase jump to $\pm 90^\circ$.
- Result: **Lower envelope fluctuation** — critical when passing through nonlinear amplifiers (e.g., in satellite transponders).

### 2.1.4 Differential PSK (DPSK)

- Information is encoded in the **change of phase** between successive symbols, not in the absolute phase.
- Eliminates the need for a **coherent carrier reference** at the receiver (non-coherent detection).
- Cost: ~3 dB worse BER performance compared to coherent BPSK.

### 2.1.5 M-ary PSK (8-PSK, 16-PSK, etc.)

- $M = 2^k$ equispaced points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$.
- Phase of $i$-th symbol: $\theta_i = 2\pi(i-1)/M$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, M$.
- Spectral efficiency doubles each time $k$ increases by 1.

## 2.2 Operational "Why & How" — The Bit-to-Phase Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How PSK works (operational steps):**
> 1. **Grouping:** Group the incoming bitstream into blocks of $k$ bits each.
> 2. **Mapping:** Each $k$-bit block is mapped to one of $M = 2^k$ unique carrier phases.
> 3. **Modulation:** The carrier's phase is held constant for the symbol duration $T_s = kT_b$.
> 4. **Transmission:** The phase-modulated carrier propagates through the channel, where it picks up **AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise)**.
> 5. **Detection:** The receiver estimates the phase of the received symbol and maps it back to the closest constellation point (maximum likelihood detection).
>
> **Why it works:** The receiver has a known set of $M$ reference phases. The decision is made by selecting the reference phase **closest in Euclidean distance** to the received (noisy) phase.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table summarizes every formula needed to solve KTU 2024 scheme problems on PSK.**

| **Parameter** | **BPSK** | **QPSK** | **M-ary PSK** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of symbols (M) | $2$ | $4$ | $2^k$ |
| Bits per symbol ($k$) | $1$ | $2$ | $\log_2 M$ |
| Symbol duration ($T_s$) | $T_b$ | $2T_b$ | $kT_b$ |
| Bandwidth (main lobe, raised-cosine, $\alpha = 0$) | $1/T_b$ | $1/(2T_b)$ | $1/T_s = 1/(kT_b)$ |
| Bandwidth (raised-cosine, roll-off $\alpha$) | $(1+\alpha)/T_b$ | $(1+\alpha)/(2T_b)$ | $(1+\alpha)/T_s$ |
| Spectral efficiency ($\eta$) | $1$ bps/Hz | $2$ bps/Hz | $\log_2 M$ bps/Hz |
| Energy per bit ($E_b$) | $E_s$ | $E_s/2$ | $E_s/k$ |
| Symbol energy vs $E_b$ | $E_s = E_b$ | $E_s = 2E_b$ | $E_s = kE_b$ |
| Exact BER ($P_b$) | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}\right)$ | $\approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M}Q\!\left(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\,\sin(\pi/M)\right)$ for large $M$ |
| Coherent BER approximation | Same as above | Same as BPSK | $\approx \frac{2}{k}Q\!\left(\sqrt{2kE_b/N_0}\,\sin(\pi/M)\right)$ |
| DPSK BER (non-coherent) | $\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ | — | — |
| Minimum phase separation | $180^\circ$ | $90^\circ$ | $360^\circ/M$ |
| Constellation radius | $\sqrt{E_b}$ | $\sqrt{E_s}$ | $\sqrt{E_s}$ |

### Legend and Key Functions

- $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty} e^{-t^2/2}\,dt$ — **Gaussian Q-function** (area under the tail of standard normal distribution)
- $N_0/2$ — **Two-sided noise power spectral density** (in W/Hz) of AWGN
- $\text{erfc}(x) = 1 - \text{erf}(x)$ — **Complementary error function**, with $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **BPSK** is used in **deep-space communications** (Voyager, Cassini) and **GPS** (coarse/acquisition codes) because of its **superior power efficiency** and robust performance in low-SNR regimes.
> - **QPSK** is the modulation backbone of **3G UMTS, 4G LTE, Wi-Fi (802.11)** and **DVB-S2** satellite TV. It strikes the optimal balance between spectral efficiency and power efficiency.
> - **OQPSK** is favored in **military satellite links** and **CDMA2000** because of its lower envelope variation, which is critical for **saturated power amplifiers**.
> - **8-PSK** appears in **EDGE (2.5G cellular)** and some **satellite modems** when higher data rates are required.
> - **Higher-order PSK (16-PSK, 32-PSK)** is rarely used in practice because **QAM** (which uses both phase and amplitude) outperforms it for the same M.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — BPSK Signal Energy and Orthogonality

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the two BPSK signals (for bit `1` and bit `0`) are **antipodal** (i.e., perfectly negatively correlated) and derive the **energy per bit** $E_b$.

### Step 1: Define the two basis signals

Let the symbol interval be $0 \le t \le T_b$. The two BPSK signals are:

$$
\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

$$
\phi_2(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

### Step 2: Compute the energy of $\phi_1(t)$

$$
E = \int_0^{T_b} \phi_1^2(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} \frac{2}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt
$$

Apply the identity $\cos^2(\theta) = \frac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$
E = \int_0^{T_b} \frac{2}{T_b}\cdot\frac{1+\cos(4\pi f_c t)}{2}\,dt = \frac{1}{T_b}\int_0^{T_b}\left[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)\right]dt
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate the integral

$$
E = \frac{1}{T_b}\left[t + \frac{\sin(4\pi f_c t)}{4\pi f_c}\right]_0^{T_b}
$$

Assuming $f_c T_b \gg 1$ (carrier frequency much greater than bit rate), the second term vanishes at the limits:

$$
E = \frac{1}{T_b}\bigl[T_b + 0\bigr] = 1
$$

### Step 4: Verify antipodal correlation

$$
\langle\phi_1, \phi_2\rangle = \int_0^{T_b}\phi_1(t)\phi_2(t)\,dt = -\int_0^{T_b}\frac{2}{T_b}\cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = -1
$$

### Step 5: Physical interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The energy of each basis function is **normalized to 1** (dimensionless), and the inner product is $-1$. This means the Euclidean distance between the two constellation points is $d = 2\sqrt{E_b}$. The **antipodal structure** is what gives BPSK the **minimum possible BER for any binary scheme** at a given $E_b/N_0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — QPSK Signal Representation and Bandwidth Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that QPSK carries **2 bits per symbol** in the **same bandwidth** as BPSK, and derive the energy relationship $E_s = 2E_b$.

### Step 1: Define the QPSK signals

For $M = 4$ and $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$, with carrier phase shifts of $45^\circ, 135^\circ, 225^\circ, 275^\circ$:

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + (2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right),\quad 0 \le t \le T_s
$$

### Step 2: Expand using the cosine addition formula

$$
s_i(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\left[\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right) - \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]
$$

### Step 3: Identify the in-phase (I) and quadrature (Q) components

$$
s_i(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}_{I_i}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin\!\left((2i-1)\frac{\pi}{4}\right)}_{Q_i}\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the I/Q values for each symbol

| $i$ | Phase | $\cos((2i-1)\pi/4)$ | $\sin((2i-1)\pi/4)$ | $I_i$ | $Q_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $45^\circ$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ | $\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ |
| 2 | $135^\circ$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ | $\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ |
| 3 | $225^\circ$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ |
| 4 | $315^\circ$ | $1/\sqrt{2}$ | $-1/\sqrt{2}$ | $\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ | $-\sqrt{E_s/T_s}$ |

### Step 5: Show the bandwidth savings

The **QPSK symbol duration** is $T_s = 2T_b$ (twice as long as BPSK bit duration). Therefore, the **null-to-null main-lobe bandwidth** is:

$$
B_{\text{QPSK}} = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{2T_b}
$$

But the **bit rate** is:

$$
R_b = \frac{1}{T_b} = \frac{2}{T_s} = 2R_s
$$

So the **spectral efficiency** is:

$$
\eta_{\text{QPSK}} = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{1/T_b}{1/(2T_b)} = 2 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

### Step 6: Energy relationship

Each QPSK symbol carries $k = 2$ bits, so by definition:

$$
E_s = 2 E_b \quad\Longrightarrow\quad E_b = \frac{E_s}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** QPSK transmits **twice as many bits per second as BPSK** in the **same RF bandwidth**, but each bit carries **half the energy**. The **BER formula for QPSK is identical to BPSK** because the I and Q channels are orthogonal and independently detected.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — M-ary PSK Symbol Error Probability (Upper Bound)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the **approximate symbol error rate (SER)** for M-ary PSK over an AWGN channel.

### Step 1: Set up the geometry

The M-ary PSK constellation consists of $M$ points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$, with angular separation:

$$
\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{M}
$$

A symbol is received as the transmitted point plus a 2D Gaussian noise vector $\mathbf{n} = (n_1, n_2)$ with variance $N_0/2$ per dimension.

### Step 2: Compute the in-phase noise tolerance

The half-distance between adjacent points along the I-axis (or Q-axis) at the boundary of the decision region for symbol $i$ is:

$$
d_{\min/2} = \sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)
$$

### Step 3: Apply the Q-function

The probability that the noise vector pushes the received signal **across the decision boundary** is, for large $M$ and using the union bound:

$$
P_s \le 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{d_{\min/2}}{\sigma_n}\right)
$$

where the noise standard deviation per dimension is $\sigma_n = \sqrt{N_0/2}$. Therefore:

$$
P_s \approx 2\,Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_s}\sin(\pi/M)}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)
$$

### Step 4: Convert to bit error rate

Assuming **Gray coding** (where adjacent constellation points differ by only 1 bit, the standard practice), symbol errors correspond to roughly one bit error:

$$
P_b \approx \frac{P_s}{\log_2 M} = \frac{2}{\log_2 M}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)
$$

Substituting $E_s = kE_b = (\log_2 M)E_b$:

$$
\boxed{P_b \approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M}\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{2(\log_2 M)\,\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)}
$$

### Step 5: Sanity checks for limit cases

- **BPSK ($M=2$):** $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$, $\log_2 2 = 1$, so $P_b = 2Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$... wait, the bound is loose here; the **exact BPSK BER is $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$** (a factor of 2 lower than the union bound).
- **QPSK ($M=4$):** $\sin(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\log_2 4 = 2$, so $P_b \approx Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ ✓ — matches BPSK.
- **Large $M$:** As $M \to \infty$, $\sin(\pi/M) \approx \pi/M$, so the required $E_b/N_0$ grows **quadratically** with $M$, explaining why high-order PSK is power-inefficient.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Coherent BPSK Detection and Optimal Threshold

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Show that the **optimal decision rule** for BPSK detection in AWGN is to compare the correlator output to a **threshold of zero**.

### Step 1: Received signal model

After coherent demodulation and low-pass filtering, the receiver computes:

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt
$$

For transmitted bit $b \in \{+1, -1\}$ (mapped to phase $0$ or $\pi$):

$$
r = b\sqrt{E_b} + n
$$

where $n$ is a zero-mean Gaussian random variable with variance $N_0/2$.

### Step 2: Optimal decision rule (MAP/MLE)

The receiver chooses $\hat{b} = +1$ if the likelihood $p(r \mid b=+1) > p(r \mid b=-1)$, and vice versa. Equivalently, choose the **nearest constellation point**:
