- $p(r\mid b=+1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)$
- $p(r\mid b=-1) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{(r+\sqrt{E_b})^2}{N_0}\right)$

### Step 3: Equating the likelihoods

$$
(r-\sqrt{E_b})^2 = (r+\sqrt{E_b})^2
$$

$$
r^2 - 2r\sqrt{E_b} + E_b = r^2 + 2r\sqrt{E_b} + E_b
$$

$$
-2r\sqrt{E_b} = 2r\sqrt{E_b} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 4r\sqrt{E_b} = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad r = 0
$$

### Step 4: Decision rule and error probability

The optimal decision rule is:

$$
\hat{b} = \begin{cases} +1, & r \ge 0 \\ -1, & r < 0 \end{cases}
$$

The probability of error is:

$$
P_b = P(\hat b \ne b) = P(n > \sqrt{E_b}) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The optimal threshold is **zero**, and the BPSK BER is $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$. This is the **lowest achievable BER** for any binary scheme in AWGN — a benchmark against which all other digital modulation schemes are compared.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why constellation diagrams matter in KTU exams:** The constellation diagram is the **visual proof** of:
> 1. **Spectral efficiency** (more points per unit area = more bits/s/Hz).
> 2. **Noise immunity** (larger separation between points = lower BER).
> 3. **Power efficiency** (all points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ = constant envelope).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK). Draw its constellation diagram and write the expression for the BER of BPSK in an AWGN channel.**

**Model Answer:**

BPSK is a digital modulation scheme in which the phase of the carrier is shifted between **0° and 180°** to represent binary digits `1` and `0`, respectively, while amplitude and frequency are held constant. Mathematically, the transmitted signal is:

$$
s(t) = b(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t),\quad b(t) \in \{+1,-1\}
$$

**Constellation diagram:** Two antipodal points on the I-axis at $(+\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$ and $(-\sqrt{E_b}, 0)$, with the decision boundary along the Q-axis ($r = 0$).

**BER expression in AWGN:**

$$
\boxed{P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)}
$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian Q-function, $E_b$ is the energy per bit, and $N_0/2$ is the two-sided noise power spectral density.

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**List any three differences between BPSK and QPSK.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **BPSK** | **QPSK** |
|---|---|---|
| Number of phase states | $M = 2$ (0° and 180°) | $M = 4$ (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°) |
| Bits per symbol | $k = 1$ | $k = 2$ |
| Bandwidth (for given bit rate $R_b$) | $1/T_b$ | $1/(2T_b)$ — **half the bandwidth of BPSK** |
| Spectral efficiency | $1$ bit/s/Hz | $2$ bit/s/Hz |
| BER (coherent, AWGN) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical** |
| Constellation shape | 1-D (antipodal) | 2-D (square) |

> [!NOTE]
> QPSK achieves the **same BER as BPSK** with **half the bandwidth**, making it strictly superior for bandwidth-limited applications.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**a)** Explain the generation and detection of BPSK signals with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for the probability of error. (7 marks)

**b)** With reference to QPSK modulation scheme, explain how it can be generated using I/Q modulator. Compare its spectral efficiency with BPSK. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — BPSK Generation, Detection, and BER Derivation

**BPSK Generation:**

A BPSK modulator consists of:

1. A **local oscillator** generating the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. A **balanced modulator (multiplier)** that multiplies the carrier by the bipolar NRZ data $b(t) \in \{+1,-1\}$.
3. A **bandpass filter** to suppress harmonics.

The output is:

$$
s(t) = b(t)\cdot A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t)
$$

where $A_c = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$. When $b(t) = +1$, the carrier is transmitted as-is (phase 0°); when $b(t) = -1$, the carrier is inverted (phase 180°).

**BPSK Detection (Coherent):**

The receiver is shown in **Section 4.2** of these notes. The key steps are:

1. **Multiplication** of the received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ with a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ (must be **phase-coherent** with the transmitter).
2. **Integration** over one bit period $T_b$:

$$
r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\cdot\sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt
$$

3. **Sampling** at $t = T_b$.
4. **Threshold detection** at $r = 0$ (the optimal threshold for antipodal signaling).

**Probability of Error Derivation:**

After demodulation, the decision variable is $r = b\sqrt{E_b} + n$, where $n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$.

- If $b = +1$: $r \sim \mathcal{N}(+\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$ — error if $r < 0$.
- If $b = -1$: $r \sim \mathcal{N}(-\sqrt{E_b}, N_0/2)$ — error if $r > 0$.

By symmetry, $P(\text{error} \mid b=+1) = P(\text{error} \mid b=-1)$. Thus:

$$
P_b = P(n > \sqrt{E_b}) = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final BER:** $\boxed{P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})}$

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — QPSK I/Q Modulator and Spectral Efficiency

**QPSK Generation Using I/Q Modulator:**

The QPSK signal is generated as:

$$
s_i(t) = I_i\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_i\sin(2\pi f_c t)
$$

The I/Q modulator block diagram is shown in **Section 4.3**. The operation proceeds as follows:

1. **Serial-to-Parallel Conversion:** The input bitstream is split into two parallel streams (I-bits and Q-bits), each running at **half the bit rate**.
2. **NRZ Level Conversion:** Each bit is mapped to $b \in \{+1, -1\}$.
3. **Multiplication:** I-bits modulate $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$; Q-bits modulate $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ (90° phase-shifted carrier).
4. **Summation:** The two modulated carriers are added to produce the QPSK signal.

**Spectral Efficiency Comparison:**

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK |
|---|---|---|
| Bit rate $R_b$ | $1/T_b$ | $1/T_b$ |
| Symbol rate $R_s$ | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ |
| Minimum bandwidth (raised-cosine, $\alpha = 0$) | $R_b/2$ | $R_b/2$ — **same!** |
| Spectral efficiency $\eta = R_b/B$ | $2$ bit/s/Hz | $2$ bit/s/Hz |
| **With roll-off $\alpha > 0$** | $(1+\alpha)R_b/2$ | $(1+\alpha)R_b/2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** QPSK has the **same bandwidth as BPSK** for the same bit rate, but encodes **2 bits per symbol**. The QPSK spectrum is identical to BPSK, but the symbol rate is half — so QPSK is the **most spectrally efficient coherent binary-equivalent scheme**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**a)** With the help of a signal-space diagram, explain M-ary PSK. Derive the expression for the bandwidth efficiency of M-ary PSK. (7 marks)

**b)** Explain the principle of Offset QPSK (OQPSK) and Differential PSK (DPSK). Compare OQPSK with QPSK. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — M-ary PSK Signal Space and Bandwidth Efficiency

**M-ary PSK Signal Space Diagram:**

In M-ary PSK, the $M = 2^k$ signals are represented as $M$ equally spaced points on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ in the I/Q plane:

$$
\mathbf{s}_i = \left(\sqrt{E_s}\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi(i-1)}{M}\right),\ \sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{2\pi(i-1)}{M}\right)\right),\quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, M
$$

The angle subtended between adjacent points is $\Delta\theta = 360^\circ/M$. Examples:

- $M = 2$ (BPSK): Two points on the I-axis, separated by 180°.
- $M = 4$ (QPSK): Four points at 45°, 135°, 225°, 315°.
- $M = 8$ (8-PSK): Eight points, separated by 45°.

**Bandwidth Efficiency Derivation:**

The symbol duration is $T_s = kT_b = (\log_2 M)\cdot T_b$. The minimum (Nyquist) bandwidth is:

$$
B = \frac{1}{T_s} = \frac{1}{kT_b} = \frac{R_b}{k^2}\cdot k = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M}
$$

Therefore, the **bandwidth efficiency** is:

$$
\eta = \frac{R_b}{B} = \frac{R_b}{R_b/\log_2 M} = \log_2 M \text{ bit/s/Hz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Each doubling of M adds 1 bit/s/Hz to the bandwidth efficiency, but the constellation points become closer (angular separation $\Delta\theta = 360^\circ/M$ shrinks), making the system **less robust to noise**.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — OQPSK and DPSK Principles

**Offset QPSK (OQPSK):**

In OQPSK, the Q-channel data stream is **delayed by half a symbol period** ($T_s/2$) relative to the I-channel. This staggered alignment means:

- The I and Q channels **never change simultaneously**.
- The phase can change by at most **±90°** in any $T_s/2$ interval (instead of up to 180° in standard QPSK).
- The envelope variation is **reduced**, making OQPSK tolerant of **nonlinear amplification**.

**Differential PSK (DPSK):**

In DPSK, the information is encoded in the **phase difference between two consecutive symbols**:

$$
\Delta\theta_n = \theta_n - \theta_{n-1}
$$

For binary DPSK:
- Bit `1`: $\Delta\theta_n = 0$ (no phase change).
- Bit `0`: $\Delta\theta_n = \pi$ (180° phase reversal).

**Detection:** The receiver computes the **product** of the current symbol with the previous symbol's complex conjugate (a 1-bit differential decoder), avoiding the need for a coherent carrier reference.

**Comparison: OQPSK vs QPSK**

| Parameter | QPSK | OQPSK |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum phase transition | 180° (when both I and Q flip) | 90° (I and Q cannot flip simultaneously) |
| Envelope fluctuation | Higher | Lower |
| Suitability for nonlinear amplifiers | Moderate | Excellent (e.g., satellite TWTA) |
| Bandwidth | Same | Same |
| BER performance | Identical | Identical |
| Complexity | Standard | Requires $T_s/2$ delay line on Q-channel |
| Application | LTE, Wi-Fi | CDMA, satellite |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in PSK questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $E_b$ with $E_s$:** Students frequently forget that $E_s = kE_b = (\log_2 M)E_b$. Using $E_s$ directly in the BER formula instead of converting to $E_b$ (or vice versa) yields **wrong answers by a factor of $\log_2 M$**.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in QPSK bandwidth:** The QPSK symbol rate is $R_b/2$, so the bandwidth is $1/(2T_b)$, not $1/T_b$. This error propagates into spectral efficiency calculations.
> 3. **Mixing up symbol error rate (SER) and bit error rate (BER):** The formula $P_s \approx 2Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0}\sin(\pi/M))$ gives SER. For BER with Gray coding, divide by $\log_2 M$.
> 4. **Drawing BPSK as having two points on a vertical line instead of horizontal:** The BPSK constellation lies on the **I-axis** (real axis), not the Q-axis. Students commonly draw it wrong.
> 5. **Writing $Q(x) = \text{erfc}(x)$:** The correct relationship is $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. The missing factor of $1/2$ leads to **off-by-a-factor-of-2 errors**.
> 6. **Forgetting the $\sin(\pi/M)$ term in M-ary PSK BER:** For QPSK, $\sin(\pi/4) = 1/\sqrt{2}$, and this factor is what keeps the QPSK BER equal to BPSK BER. Skipping it is a **classic 2-mark loss**.
> 7. **Not specifying "coherent" detection:** DPSK BER formula ($\frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$) is for **non-coherent** detection. Comparing it with coherent BPSK requires explicit mention of the detection method.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 PSK module:**

- ✅ **BPSK** uses two phases (0° and 180°); bits per symbol $k=1$.
- ✅ **QPSK** uses four phases (45°, 135°, 225°, 315°); bits per symbol $k=2$.
- ✅ **General M-ary PSK** has $M = 2^k$ phases, equally spaced at $360^\circ/M$.
- ✅ **BPSK bandwidth** (raised-cosine, roll-off $\alpha$): $B = (1+\alpha)/T_b$.
- ✅ **QPSK bandwidth** (raised-cosine, roll-off $\alpha$): $B = (1+\alpha)/(2T_b)$.
- ✅ **Spectral efficiency**: $\eta = \log_2 M$ bit/s/Hz.
- ✅ **Energy per symbol**: $E_s = k E_b$.
- ✅ **Coherent BPSK BER**: $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
- ✅ **Coherent QPSK BER**: $P_b = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **identical to BPSK**.
- ✅ **M-ary PSK BER** (approx., Gray coding): $P_b \approx \frac{2}{\log_2 M}Q\!\left(\sqrt{2(\log_2 M)E_b/N_0}\cdot\sin(\pi/M)\right)$.
- ✅ **DPSK BER** (non-coherent): $P_b = \frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ — 3 dB worse than coherent BPSK.
- ✅ **OQPSK** limits max phase change to 90°; better for nonlinear amplifiers.
- ✅ **DPSK** encodes data in **phase differences**; uses non-coherent detection.
- ✅ **Constellation radius** for all PSK schemes: $\sqrt{E_s}$ (constant envelope).
- ✅ **Minimum phase separation**: $360^\circ/M$ — determines noise immunity.
- ✅ **I/Q modulator** is the standard implementation for QPSK and higher-order PSK.
- ✅ **Gray coding** minimizes BER by ensuring adjacent symbols differ in only 1 bit.
- ✅ **For KTU 2024:** Remember to draw block diagrams with **clear labels** (carrier, multiplier, integrator, threshold detector) and label axes as **I (in-phase)** and **Q (quadrature)**.
- ✅ **$Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$** — memorize this conversion for KTU derivations.
- ✅ **Noise spectral density** $N_0/2$ is **two-sided**; total noise power in bandwidth $B$ is $N_0 B$.

---

topics.title: Frequency Shift Keying
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET411 - COMMUNICATION ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Frequency Shift Keying

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** is a digital modulation technique in which the instantaneous frequency of a sinusoidal carrier wave is varied in discrete steps in accordance with the digital (binary or M-ary) baseband message signal, while the amplitude and phase of the carrier are held constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** FSK is classified under the family of "constant-envelope, angle-modulated" digital modulation schemes. It belongs to the same mathematical family as analog FM, but with a discrete (digital) message input rather than a continuous analog input.

Mathematically, the FSK signal can be represented as:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t + \phi_1), & \text{for binary input } m(t) = 1 \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_2 t + \phi_2), & \text{for binary input } m(t) = 0 \end{cases}$$

where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_1$ and $f_2$ are the two distinct carrier frequencies representing binary '1' and '0' respectively, and $\phi_1, \phi_2$ are the initial phase constants.

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Aligned to KTU 2024 Module 4)

- **Mark frequency ($f_1$)**: The frequency used to represent binary digit '1'.
- **Space frequency ($f_2$)**: The frequency used to represent binary digit '0'.
- **Frequency deviation ($\Delta f$)**: The absolute difference between $f_1$ and the nominal center frequency.
- **Frequency separation ($\Delta f_{sep}$)**: The absolute difference between $f_1$ and $f_2$.
- **Center frequency ($f_c$)**: The arithmetic mean of $f_1$ and $f_2$, i.e., $f_c = (f_1 + f_2)/2$.
- **Modulation index ($h$)**: Ratio of frequency separation to the bit rate, $h = \Delta f_{sep}/R_b$.
- **Coherent FSK**: A form of FSK in which the receiver uses locally generated reference signals that are phase-locked to the transmitted signals.
- **Non-coherent FSK**: A form of FSK in which no phase reference is used at the receiver; detection is based purely on energy/envelope detection.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long corridor and you need to send a secret message to a friend at the other end using only two whistles:

- If you blow the **high-pitched whistle**, your friend understands it as the dot (Morse code •).
- If you blow the **low-pitched whistle**, your friend understands it as the dash (Morse code –).

You are not changing how *loud* you blow (amplitude is constant), nor are you pausing in a complex pattern (phase is constant). You are simply *switching between two specific pitches* to encode information. This is precisely what FSK does with a radio carrier: it picks between two (or more) preset frequencies to carry the bits across the channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why FSK Matters in Real Engineering:** Because FSK has a **constant envelope**, it is highly tolerant of amplitude non-linearities in power amplifiers and channel fading. This is why FSK is the modulation of choice for **low-cost, low-power devices** such as garage door openers, walkie-talkies (CTCSS signalling), wireless key fobs, RFID systems, and early-generation wireless data modems. It is also the underlying modulation in legacy **FSK-modulated pager networks** and the physical layer of **Bluetooth LE** (Gaussian FSK variant).

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally adopted in KTU 2024 scheme problem sets:

- **Minimum frequency separation for orthogonal BFSK (coherent detection): $\Delta f_{sep} = R_b/2$ Hz**, where $R_b$ is the bit rate in bits per second (bps).
- **Minimum frequency separation for orthogonal BFSK (non-coherent detection): $\Delta f_{sep} = R_b$ Hz**.
- **Modulation index (Sunde's FSK): $h = 0.5$** — produces minimum-bandwidth coherent FSK.
- **Modulation index (non-coherent FSK): $h = 1.0$** — produces minimum-bandwidth non-coherent FSK.
- **Standard values of $E_b/N_0$ for BER = $10^{-5}$**: approximately **9.6 dB (coherent BFSK)** and **12.6 dB (non-coherent BFSK)**.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of FSK — A Structured Breakdown

#### A. Binary FSK (BFSK)
Only two frequencies are used. Each binary digit maps to one frequency.

**Sub-categories:**
1. **Coherent BFSK (orthogonal)**
   - Both transmitted frequencies are integer multiples of half the bit rate.
   - Demodulated using two correlators (matched filters) with locally generated references locked in phase.
   - Better BER performance.
2. **Non-coherent BFSK**
   - Demodulated using bandpass filters and envelope detectors — no phase reference required.
   - Simpler hardware, slightly worse BER.
3. **Continuous-Phase FSK (CPFSK)**
   - The carrier phase is continuous at the bit transitions, eliminating abrupt phase jumps.
   - Reduces out-of-band spectral sidelobes.
4. **Gaussian FSK (GFSK)**
   - The baseband data is first filtered through a Gaussian low-pass filter before frequency modulation.
   - Used in Bluetooth (BT = 0.5) — extremely compact spectrum.

#### B. M-ary FSK (M-FSK)
Uses $M = 2^k$ frequencies, where $k$ is the number of bits per symbol.
- Bandwidth increases roughly with $M$, but **power efficiency improves** dramatically.
- For the same bit energy $E_b$, increasing $M$ reduces the required $E_s$ per symbol, but the noise bandwidth also grows.
- Used in **LF/HF radio modems** and certain **deep-space telemetry** links (e.g., NASA standards with $M=16, 32, 64$).

### 2.2 Generation (Modulation) — The 'How'

**Method 1: Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**
- The binary NRZ data is applied to the VCO tuning input.
- When input is '1' → VCO outputs frequency $f_1$.
- When input is '0' → VCO outputs frequency $f_2$.
- Naturally produces **continuous-phase FSK**.

**Method 2: Frequency Shift Keying by Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS / Frequency Synthesizer)**
- Two independent oscillators (or a divider-based synthesizer) are gated by the data bit.
- Switches between $f_1$ and $f_2$ abruptly — this produces **discontinuous-phase FSK**, which has a wider spectrum.
- Often followed by a band-limiting filter to recover CPFSK behavior.

### 2.3 Detection (Demodulation) — The 'How' at the Receiver

**Method 1: Coherent Detection (Correlator / Matched Filter Bank)**
- Two parallel correlators, one matched to $f_1$ and one matched to $f_2$.
- The decision device picks the larger of the two correlator outputs.
- Requires a local carrier reference (often derived via Costas loop or PLL).
- **Optimal in the AWGN channel** — minimizes BER.

**Method 2: Non-Coherent Detection (Envelope Detector / Energy Detector)**
- Two bandpass filters centered at $f_1$ and $f_2$ separate the two tones.
- Envelope (or square-law) detectors compute the energy in each band.
- A comparator selects the larger.
- **Sub-optimal by ~3 dB** in $E_b/N_0$ compared to coherent detection.
- Robust against phase noise and frequency drift — preferred in low-cost hardware.

**Method 3: Zero-Crossing Detector**
- Counts the zero-crossings of the received waveform per bit interval.
- Higher zero-crossing count → higher frequency → bit '1' (or vice versa).
- Simple and cheap, but sensitive to noise.

**Method 4: FM Discriminator + Sampler**
- A standard FM demodulator (e.g., Foster-Seeley, PLL, or quadrature detector) recovers the instantaneous frequency.
- Sampled at the bit center to make a decision.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | BFSK signal (general) | $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_i t + \phi_i)$ for bit $i$ | Volts |
| 2 | Frequency separation | $\Delta f_{sep} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$ | Hz |
| 3 | Center frequency | $f_c = (f_1 + f_2)/2$ | Hz |
| 4 | Modulation index (digital) | $h = \Delta f_{sep} / R_b$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Modulation index (analog FM) | $\beta_f = \Delta f / W$ where $W$ is message BW | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Minimum $\Delta f$ for coherent orthogonal BFSK | $\Delta f_{min} = R_b / 2$ | Hz |
| 7 | Minimum $\Delta f$ for non-coherent orthogonal BFSK | $\Delta f_{min} = R_b$ | Hz |
| 8 | BFSK transmission bandwidth (Carson's rule analog) | $B_T = 2(\Delta f_{peak} + W)$ | Hz; for FSK, $W = R_b/2$ |
| 9 | BFSK transmission bandwidth (approx, KTU standard) | $B_T \approx 2 \Delta f_{sep} + 2 R_b$ | Hz |
| 10 | M-ary FSK transmission bandwidth | $B_T = M \cdot \Delta f_{sep} + 2 R_b / \log_2 M$ | Hz |
| 11 | M-ary FSK symbol rate | $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M = R_b / k$ | Baud |
| 12 | Average transmitted power (constant envelope) | $P_s = A_c^2 / 2$ | Watts |
| 13 | Bit energy | $E_b = P_s T_b = P_s / R_b$ | Joules |
| 14 | Symbol energy (M-FSK) | $E_s = E_b \log_2 M = k E_b$ | Joules |
| 15 | Coherent BFSK BER (AWGN) | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b / N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 16 | Non-coherent BFSK BER (AWGN) | $P_b = \tfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-E_b / 2 N_0\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 17 | M-FSK symbol error probability (coherent) | $P_s \le (M-1)\, Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_s / N_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless |
| 18 | M-FSK symbol error probability (non-coherent) | $P_s = \sum_{j=1}^{M-1} \binom{M-1}{j} \frac{(-1)^{j+1}}{j+1} e^{-j E_s / [(j+1)N_0]}$ | Dimensionless |
| 19 | M-FSK bandwidth efficiency | $\eta = (\log_2 M) / B_T$ | bits/s/Hz |
| 20 | M-FSK power efficiency at high $M$ | $P_e \approx e^{-k E_b / 2 N_0}$ for $M \to \infty$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Sanity Checks (KTU 2024 Module 4 Emphasis):**
> - All logarithms in the formulas above are **natural logarithms** unless otherwise specified.
> - The $Q$-function is defined as $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2}\, du$.
> - The quantity $E_b/N_0$ is dimensionless but is conventionally reported in **decibels**: $(E_b/N_0)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(E_b/N_0)$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of FSK

- **Wireless PANs (Bluetooth Classic)**: GFSK with $h = 0.32$ at 1 Mbps.
- **RFID (ISO 14443, ISO 15693)**: BFSK/ASK hybrid for contactless smart cards.
- **Amateur radio packet systems (AX.25)**: AFSK at 1200 baud using Bell 202 modems.
- **Two-way radio CTCSS/DCS sub-audible signalling**: FSK below 300 Hz.
- **Paging networks (POCSAG)**: BFSK at 512 bps or 1200 bps.
- **Power-line communication (PLC) for smart metering**: Narrowband FSK in the CENELEC bands.
- **Modems for legacy HF radio links**: M-FSK with $M = 4, 8$ for robust low-SNR operation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the BFSK Signal Representation

**Step 1 — Start with a generic binary data stream.**
Let the baseband data be a sequence of bits $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ with bit duration $T_b$. The NRZ representation of the data is:

$$m(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} (2 b_k - 1) \, p(t - k T_b)$$

where $p(t)$ is a unit-amplitude rectangular pulse of duration $T_b$.

**Step 2 — Map the data onto frequency.**
For BFSK, binary '1' maps to frequency $f_1 = f_c + \Delta f$ and binary '0' maps to frequency $f_2 = f_c - \Delta f$, where $\Delta f$ is the frequency deviation from the center frequency $f_c$.

**Step 3 — Express the instantaneous frequency.**

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \Delta f \cdot m(t)$$

**Step 4 — Express the instantaneous phase.**
The instantaneous phase $\theta_i(t)$ is the integral of the instantaneous angular frequency:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2 \pi \int_0^t f_i(\tau) \, d\tau = 2 \pi f_c t + 2 \pi \Delta f \int_0^t m(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 5 — Write the FSK signal.**

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left( 2 \pi f_c t + 2 \pi \Delta f \int_0^t m(\tau) \, d\tau \right)$$

This is the **continuous-phase FSK (CPFSK)** representation. The integral $\int_0^t m(\tau)\, d\tau$ is a piecewise linear (staircase-like) function that ramps up during '1' bits and ramps down during '0' bits.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Orthogonality Condition for Coherent BFSK

**Step 1 — Define orthogonality.**
Two signals $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$ are orthogonal over $[0, T_b]$ if:

$$\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t) \, s_2(t) \, dt = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply to two BFSK tones.**

$$s_1(t) = A_c \cos(2 \pi f_1 t), \qquad s_2(t) = A_c \cos(2 \pi f_2 t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the product-to-sum identity.**
$\cos A \cos B = \tfrac{1}{2} [\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t) s_2(t) \, dt = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \int_0^{T_b} \left[ \cos(2\pi(f_1 - f_2)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_1 + f_2)t) \right] dt$$

**Step 4 — Integrate.**

$$= \frac{A_c^2}{2} \left[ \frac{\sin(2\pi(f_1 - f_2)T_b)}{2\pi(f_1 - f_2)} + \frac{\sin(2\pi(f_1 + f_2)T_b)}{2\pi(f_1 + f_2)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the assumption $f_1 + f_2 \gg 1/T_b$.**
The second term integrates to approximately zero (it oscillates rapidly). The first term vanishes when:

$$(f_1 - f_2) T_b = \frac{n}{2}, \qquad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 6 — Identify minimum frequency separation.**
The smallest positive separation occurs for $n = 1$:

$$\Delta f_{sep, min} = \frac{1}{2 T_b} = \frac{R_b}{2} \; \text{Hz}$$

This is the **Sunde's FSK minimum** for coherent detection, with $h = 0.5$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Coherent BFSK Bit Error Probability (AWGN)

**Step 1 — Model the received signal in AWGN.**

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \qquad i \in \{1, 2\}$$

where $n(t)$ is a zero-mean white Gaussian noise process with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

**Step 2 — Receiver structure.**
The receiver correlates $r(t)$ with two locally generated references $s_1(t)$ and $s_2(t)$ over a bit duration $T_b$:

$$z_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \, s_1(t) \, dt, \qquad z_2 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \, s_2(t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Compute the decision variable.**
Assume $s_1(t)$ was transmitted. Then:

$$z_1 = \int_0^{T_b} s_1^2(t) \, dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t) s_1(t) \, dt = E_b + n_1$$

$$z_2 = \int_0^{T_b} s_1(t) s_2(t) \, dt + \int_0^{T_b} n(t) s_2(t) \, dt = 0 + n_2$$

(using the orthogonality of $s_1$ and $s_2$).

**Step 4 — Statistics of the noise components.**
$n_1$ and $n_2$ are zero-mean Gaussian random variables with variance $\sigma^2 = (N_0/2) E_b$ each.

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**
A bit error occurs if $z_2 > z_1$ when $s_1$ was transmitted. The error probability is:

$$P_b = P(z_2 - z_1 > 0 \mid s_1 \text{ sent}) = P(n_2 - n_1 > E_b)$$

**Step 6 — The decision statistic is Gaussian.**
Since $n_2 - n_1$ is Gaussian with mean 0 and variance $2\sigma^2 = N_0 E_b$:

$$P_b = Q\!\left( \frac{E_b}{\sqrt{N_0 E_b}} \right) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} \right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{b,\,\text{coh}} = Q\!\left( \sqrt{E_b / N_0} \right)}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the Non-Coherent BFSK Bit Error Probability (AWGN)

**Step 1 — Receiver structure.**
Two bandpass filters centered at $f_1$ and $f_2$, followed by envelope detectors, produce envelope samples $r_1$ and $r_2$ at the end of each bit period.

**Step 2 — Statistics of the envelope.**
If $s_1$ is transmitted, then:
- $r_1$ follows a **Rician distribution** with non-centrality parameter $\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}$ and variance $N_0/2$ per dimension.
- $r_2$ follows a **Rayleigh distribution** (no signal component).

**Step 3 — Error event.**
A bit error occurs when $r_2 > r_1$.

**Step 4 — Compute the error probability.**
The standard result for the probability that a Rayleigh variable exceeds a Rician variable is:

$$P_b = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_b}{2 N_0} \right)$$

$$\boxed{P_{b,\,\text{noncoh}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \exp(-E_b / 2 N_0)}$$

### 3.5 Derivation of M-ary FSK Bandwidth

**Step 1 — Symbol rate.**
For $M = 2^k$ frequencies carrying $k$ bits per symbol:

$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{k} = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M} \; \text{Baud}$$

**Step 2 — Frequency spacing between adjacent tones.**
For orthogonal M-FSK, adjacent tones must be separated by at least $1/T_s = R_s$ Hz to maintain orthogonality.

**Step 3 — Total occupied bandwidth.**
With $M$ tones, the total span is:

$$B_T = (M - 1) \cdot \frac{1}{T_s} + \frac{2}{T_s} = \frac{M + 1}{T_s} \approx M \cdot R_s \; \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R_s$.**

$$\boxed{B_T \approx \frac{M \cdot R_b}{\log_2 M} \; \text{Hz}}$$

**Step 5 — Bandwidth efficiency.**

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{\log_2 M}{B_T} \approx \frac{(\log_2 M)^2}{M \cdot R_b} \; \text{bits/s/Hz}}$$

This shows that **bandwidth efficiency degrades as $M$ grows** — a fundamental trade-off with M-FSK's superior power efficiency.

### 3.6 Comparison Matrix: Coherent vs. Non-Coherent BFSK

| Parameter | Coherent BFSK | Non-Coherent BFSK |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation index $h$ | 0.5 (Sunde's FSK) | 1.0 |
| Min. frequency separation | $R_b/2$ | $R_b$ |
| Bandwidth (approx) | $\sim 2 R_b$ | $\sim 3 R_b$ |
| Receiver complexity | Higher (carrier recovery needed) | Lower (envelope detection) |
| BER (AWGN) | $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ |
| $E_b/N_0$ for $P_b = 10^{-5}$ | ~9.6 dB | ~12.6 dB |
| Phase-noise tolerance | Poor | Excellent |
| Typical use case | Synchronous data links, telemetry | Low-cost RF links, RFID, paging |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 BFSK Modulator — Voltage-Controlled Oscillator Method

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Data NRZ] --> B[Level Shifter -1 to +1 V]
    B --> C[VCO Tuning Input]
    C --> D{VCO Outputs}
    D -->|bit 1| E[Frequency f1]
    D -->|bit 0| F[Frequency f2]
    E --> G[FSK Signal s of t]
    F --> G
```

### 4.2 Coherent BFSK Demodulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Received r of t] --> M[Multiplier 1]
    R --> N[Multiplier 2]
    L1[Local Oscillator f1] --> M
    L2[Local Oscillator f2] --> N
    M --> I1[Integrator 0 to Tb]
    N --> I2[Integrator 0 to Tb]
    I1 --> S1[Sample at t = Tb]
    I2 --> S2[Sample at t = Tb]
    S1 --> C[Comparator]
    S2 --> C
    C --> O[Decision Binary Output]
```

### 4.3 Non-Coherent BFSK Demodulator (Envelope Detection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Received r of t] --> BPF1[Bandpass Filter f1]
    R --> BPF2[Bandpass Filter f2]
    BPF1 --> ED1[Envelope Detector]
    BPF2 --> ED2[Envelope Detector]
    ED1 --> S1[Sampler at t = Tb]
    ED2 --> S2[Sampler at t = Tb]
    S1 --> C[Comparator]
    S2 --> C
    C --> O[Decision Binary Output]
```

### 4.4 Time-Domain and Frequency-Domain Intuition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD[Time Domain View]
        B1[Bit 1 period] --> W1[High frequency sinusoid]
        B0[Bit 0 period] --> W2[Low frequency sinusoid]
    end
    subgraph FD[Frequency Domain View]
        SP[Two Spectral Lines at f1 and f2] --> BW[Total Bandwidth = 2 delta f plus 2 Rb]
    end
```

### 4.5 End-to-End BFSK Communication Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Binary Source] --> ENC[Line Encoder NRZ]
    ENC --> MOD[FSK Modulator VCO]
    MOD --> CH[AWGN Channel]
    CH --> RX[FSK Demodulator]
    RX --> DEC[Decision Device]
    DEC --> DST[Binary Sink]
    style MOD fill:#ffe599
    style RX fill:#b6d7a8
    style CH fill:#f4cccc
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each rectangular box represents a functional block; arrows represent the unidirectional flow of the baseband or passband signal. In the coherent demodulator, both integrators are dumped and reset synchronously at every bit boundary — this requires a **bit-clock recovery** circuit (often a PLL) that is not shown for clarity.
